abstract: Hilmar Cheese Company, located in Hilmar, California, is a leading producer of cheese for global distribution. During production, large cheese blocks are vacuum-sealed in plastic wraps, a process prone to defects when small cheese curds become trapped in the seal. These trapped curds make the bags unusable later on in the distribution process. They are difficult to detect manually due to their small size and the speed of the production line. The team was tasked with developing a computer vision-based solution to automatically detect trapped curds and alert operators to take corrective action. Confronted with the challenge of a limited dataset, the team employed multiple approaches, including OpenCV and various detection models, to identify the most consistent and promising solution with the potential to improve as more data becomes available.
studentNames: Aadi Sharma, Amrita Vilku, Anirud Vorugu, Ivan Vargas

projectTitle: Crop Yield Estimation via Robotic Image Capture
abstract: SupHerb Farms, a company whose business involves herb production, experiences costly accuracy problems with its current visual inspection-based pre-harvest crop weight estimates. To solve this, the team designed an image capture system using DepthAI-powered cameras on the Farm-ng Amiga robot and collected data on cilantro crops at the UC Merced Experimental Smart Farm. Using this data, the team engineered a Python algorithm that implemented voxelization to estimate the crop volume and linear regression to predict the harvest weight. With this system, SupHerb can automate and increase the accuracy of harvest weight estimation.
studentNames: Garret Castro, Hans Guillen, Jason Yuen, Nick Mount

projectTitle: Digital AI Backpack Opportunity Web Scraping
abstract: Conectado is transforming the path of education with technology by developing a platform that bridges the gap between schools and students, empowering the next generation of underrepresented learners and revolutionizing pathways to success in STEM fields. At the heart of this innovation is the company’s use of web scrapers—algorithms designed to search and extract the internet for valuable resources and opportunities. However, Conectado faced significant hurdles with its initial scrapers: limited data, frequent website blocks, and an inability to capture the wide variety of opportunities students need to launch their careers. To overcome these challenges, the team enhanced their scrapers with technologies like Selenium, IP proxy rotation, and other bypass techniques, unlocking access to over 1,000 career-enhancing opportunities a month for students. This solution highlights the team’s commitment to providing underrepresented students with access to STEM career opportunities that level the playing field.
studentNames: Harshul Satija, John Tran, Kirthana Kavali, Pranav Chakilam

projectTitle: Digital AI Backpack Web Application - Community Connect
abstract: Conectado, an EdTech startup, is committed to empowering members of Latino and underrepresented communities to succeed by building safer, simpler, and more sustainable connections from the classroom, to career, to the boardroom. Conectado sought to improve their Digital AI Backpack Web Application to combat the need for better opportunities to help Conectado users further expand their professional networks and connect with others who share similar interests. The team enhanced user connectivity on the application by improving user profiles and integrating LinkedIn, enabling users to stay connected with each other. In addition, the team enhanced an AI Chatbot to better assist users in discovering and connecting with like-minded individuals and opportunities tailored to their goals. Together, these advancements are accelerating social capital development among Conectado users.
studentNames: Evan Lu, Jeffrey Peng, Kristina Wong, Tanush Maddala

organization: United States Department of State Diplomacy Lab
abstract: The Language Learning Consultation Service (LCS), supported by the Diplomacy Lab in collaboration with the Department of State, provides personalized guidance to diplomats and their families during intensive language training, helping them navigate the language training effectively to develop the language and cultural skills they need to support diplomatic missions and foreign policy objectives. However, the current scheduling and consultation management processes rely on multiple platforms, leading to inefficient workflows and excessive back-and-forth email communication.  To address these challenges, the team developed a centralized system platform tailored to LCS's needs, ensuring smooth and reliable operations and enhancing the user experience. By simplifying scheduling, improving accessibility, and addressing issues like high turnover, the team’s solution condenses the entire process into just a few clicks, reducing inefficiencies and advancing LCS's mission to support diplomats in language training more effectively.
studentNames: Cheska Cinderella Friend, Quan Nguyen, Sergio Angulo, Tanner Jackson

organization: United States Department of State Diplomacy Lab
abstract: The School of Language Studies (SLS), part of the U.S Department of State Diplomacy Lab initiative, prepares U.S. diplomats for work abroad by providing foreign language training and assessments. Currently the Language Training Specialists (LTS) use Microsoft Excel to manually schedule progress assessments, which is labor intensive and time consuming. The Polyglot team developed a desktop application to help the LTS view and schedule assessments via a shared calendar, automatically send confirmation emails, process student rescheduling requests through a web panel, and generate assessment reports by examiners, students, or language. This new application will make the scheduling process quicker, straightforward, and easier to track.
studentNames: Andrew Reed, Anthony Rojas, Jacob Thompson, Raul Pena Verduzco, Spencer	Stafford

projectTitle: Automated Matching of Testing Raters to Language Test Examinees
organization: United States Department of State Diplomacy Lab
abstract: Diplomacy Lab is a U.S. Department of State initiative launched in 2013, which allows for students and faculty nationwide to tackle real-world foreign policy challenges. As a crucial part in this program, the Foreign Service Institute (FSI) handles over 5,600 language exams in 58 languages that need assigned raters and examinees to each. To streamline this process, the team developed a Python-based desktop application utilizing an algorithm to automate the rater-examinee assignment process, thus optimizing the matching process while also adhering to the department’s scheduling needs and requirements. This innovation reduces the time spent assigning the exams, balances workloads for raters, and enhances operational scalability.
studentNames: Chris Hernandez, Ethan Chou, Emily Puente, Luis Fujarte, Nevin Jom

abstract: Sweep specializes in designing big data workflow software to process diverse data types, including images, text, and machine data. Using the Sweep API, a cloud-based platform for scalable data storage and analysis, the team collaborates with businesses to develop tailored workflow solutions. To address the challenge of integrating AI models efficiently while minimizing costs and resource usage, this project developed a task automation system combining large language models (LLMs) with Apache Airflow. The system automates daily tasks step-by-step, triggering LLMs only when necessary, scheduling tasks, providing real-time monitoring, and securely delivering results via encrypted email, ensuring streamlined and cost-effective business operations.
studentNames: German De La Luz, Joseph Tadrous, Nawfal Abdul Khader, Yash Dhillon

abstract: Western Digital had a need for a solution to tracking and logging mentor-mentee interactions in order to enhance soft-skills, facilitating engagement and professional growth within the workers of the company. The team developed a mentor-mentee progress tracking application, leveraging MySQL, Amazon Relational Database Service, React Native, and Node.js. This application allows Western Digital to efficiently monitor 1:1 sessions, set objectives, and record progress, ensuring transparent, structured, and easily schedulable mentorship. The system’s user-friendly interface and robust backend integration provide a scalable solution to improve organizational learning and development. By addressing the needed solution, the project enables Western Digital to create impactful mentor-mentee relationships while maintaining detailed interaction records.
studentNames: Fernando Campos Chavez, Hikaru Kurusu, Kyle Sulit, Shrivaikunth Krishnakumar

projectTitle: Shuttle Truck Robotic Arm Automation: Computer and Camera Setup
abstract: Morning Star, a leader in tomato processing, seeks to automate the manual task of connecting and disconnecting shuttle brake lines, which is currently performed by truck drivers. The team developed a foundation for this process using a UR10 robotic arm, ZED X Mini stereo camera, and the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano to deliver a precise system of operation. The Jetson Orin Nano serves as the computational hub, enabling real-time processing of ZED SDK-based object detection and data transfer with the ROS 2 framework. Advanced motion planning through MoveIt 2 was researched and tested in order to enable external control of the robot arm. This project demonstrates how robotics and AI, powered by cutting-edge computing platforms, can revolutionize agricultural automation and inspire advancements across industries.
studentNames: Cameron Fabela, Ethan Hom, Kobe Kimoto-Ross, Kirath Pandal, Marcelo Ramirez

abstract: Turlock Irrigation District is the first water district in California to provide irrigation water and electrical services to the Central Valley. While providing irrigation water, water distribution operators need up-to-date information while out on the field servicing customers. Our team was tasked with generating and enhancing their set of dashboards with new, real-time graphics and figures. The project was completed by developing and deploying these figures into their existing dashboard environment.
studentNames: Adrian Navarro, Jacob Myung, James Odanga, Yuepeng Lee

projectTitle: Wine Inventory Management Using Machine Vision
abstract: Jack Oswald, CEO of ISOThrive and founder of Owligator, identified a challenge faced by restaurants and personal wine cellar owners: managing large wine cellars accurately is often difficult and time-consuming. To address this, he envisioned an automated solution leveraging computer vision enabling cellar owners to use an app with a live camera feed to monitor the cellar and determine whether a bottle is placed in a specific slot. The Wine Guys team developed a computer vision algorithm capable of detecting the wine cellar grid with the assistance of markers physically placed in the cellar. The algorithm identifies which grid slots are occupied by bottles. This solution is integrated into a user-friendly application that provides cellar managers an online inventory management system, allowing users to add or remove bottles while seeing real-time updates upon prompt.
studentNames: Cristian Galarza Torres, Edmond Lee, Raghav Sinha

projectTitle: Automated Identification of Paste Visual Finish - Phase 2
abstract: The Morning Star Company is a tomato harvesting and processing company, supplying over  forty percent of national markets with their tomato paste and diced tomatoes. The tomato paste is categorized using a range of finishes, achieved using screen meshes. Variability of these finishes and potential mislabeling due to human error can affect efficiency and quality control. To address this issue, our team has developed a web application that can be easily integrated into their work space. Our application allows the quality control technician to take a picture of the tomato paste, uses an ensemble machine learning model to automate the analysis of the tomato paste, and plots the analysis on a graph for better visualization. This solution minimizes human error, enhances operational efficiency, and ensures consistent product quality, significantly improving the company's quality control process.
studentNames: Ethan On, Ryan Milstrey, Salvador Nunez, Sakayt Singh

projectTitle: Curation and Tracking of Linked Information
abstract: Globidea is a company whose mission is to make information easier to find and navigate. Our team developed a browser extension designed for "curators" to transparently log content selected from any website and the respective hyperlink.  The extension allows curators to select content and incorporate it into new digital works with the reference. To ensure curators always have up-to-date content, the extension periodically checks the sources for any changes. If a change is detected, the curator is notified and given the option to update their work with the new content.  Additionally, curators can review and organize their saved sources and hyperlinks into groups, customize the interval at which the extension checks for changes, and set preferences for notification methods and auto-updates. This ensures a seamless and efficient curation experience, keeping digital works current and relevant.
studentNames: Fernando Martinez, Genesis Vazquez, Mark Araza, Psalm Bautista

projectTitle: Networking Engagement - Information Capture and Aggregation App
abstract: Innovate to Grow (I2G) is a unique experiential learning program where teams students to solve real-world problems, including problems and user needs that we identity to search and organize information to create new knowledge.  The team’s project builds on this foundation by addressing the inefficiencies of networking at conferences and expos, where gathering, organizing, and analyzing data from several sources is essential to success. Without satisfactory data accumulation, important insights or information can get lost, and the data remains as disjointed pieces or forgotten. To address this challenge, the team developed a web and mobile application designed to simplify the process of recording and linking digital data to the individuals you interact with. The app enables users to compile information from various sources, ensuring the data is organized and refined for better usability and more effective connections.
studentNames: Brian Gao, Christopher Huang, Kyle Shyffer, Victor Aguele, Quincy Walter-Eze

projectTitle: Development of an LLM model for Health Data
abstract: Current biomarker devices face challenges in translating complex health data into actionable insights due to limitations in real-time interpretation and feedback. X10e, a company specializing in biofluid analysis through advanced electronic skin sensors, has addressed this issue by developing a Large Language Model (LLM) trained on health data and scientific research. Our team utilized retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to enable the LLM to transform complex biomarker data into clear, actionable insights and provide instant health recommendations. By employing structured prompts - including system messages, user health summaries, and medical documents - alongside a retriever interface, the system efficiently extracts information from unstructured queries. This innovative solution, now ready for deployment, offers personalized health guidance through a user-friendly platform, promoting better patient engagement and proactive health management.
studentNames: Alex Sanchez Rubio, Flor Aguilar, Jessie Mendoza, Nebyou Abebe, Ralph Sulay

projectTitle: Customized LLM Development for Assisting Seniors with Smartwatch and Related Device Usage
abstract: Semcorel, a company that makes smartwatches for senior citizens, needed help improving customer support for their products. Our team, (317-SeaCoral), helped address their problem using AI, we developed a fine-tuned large language model (LLM) using Llama from Meta. This LLM helps guide the elderly on using their smartwatches, reducing the need to contact Semcorel's customer support. We created a simple and accessible interface for our product. Our features include large adjustable fonts and multiple input and output options, such as speech-to-text, text-to-speech, in addition to typing. These features aim to accommodate for any needs those using the app might have. Additionally, we built a tool for Semcorel to easily add new questions to the database, allowing the LLM to handle future customer needs and making our product adaptable. These improvements make the smartwatches more accessible, helping senior users solve issues on their own.
studentNames: Berrydal Moshe, Christine Doan, Kavin Rajasekaran, Karina Zamora

projectTitle: Hopper Trailer Modification to Dry and Discharge Wet Almonds
abstract: Almondry is an innovative agricultural project dedicated to addressing challenges in almond harvesting. Our team addressed the problem of wet almonds becoming stuck in hopper trailers. When loading almonds that are wet or with high moisture content, they may stick to walls and become prone to clumping in critical areas of the trailer, especially with the presence of soil. This leads to inefficient unloading processes, requiring hazardous and labor-intensive efforts to manually dislodge the almonds. Currently, workers must climb onto hopper trailers to remove the stuck almonds and break clumps, posing significant safety risks and increasing labor costs, which consumes valuable time that could be spent unloading additional trailers. To solve this, we developed a modified hopper trailer system that integrates industrial compressed air nozzles, air knives, and a modular vibration pad to dislodge wet almonds effectively. This system eliminates the need for dangerous manual labor, significantly improves unloading efficiency, and enhances worker safety. Additionally, our system facilitates off-ground harvesting and ensures efficient transportation to the huller, where the Almondry stockpile aeration system can be used to dry almonds. By implementing this innovative solution, Almondry is helping hullers reduce costs, save time, and increase operational safety.
studentNames: Arcadio Mercado, Daniel Carranza Gomez, Hassan Imran

projectTitle: Scaled Removable and Reusable Stockpile Aeration System for Almond Drying
abstract: The almond industry recognizes the significant dust generated during harvest, and the Almond Board of California has set a goal to reduce harvest dust by 50%. Traditional harvest methods involve shaking, sweeping, and gathering nuts, followed by a drying period on the orchard floor. Off-ground harvesting consolidates these steps and reduces dust generation but omits the critical drying period. This poses a problem, as hullers require almonds to arrive dried. The objective of the project was to design a reusable, land-conscious system to enable on-site almond drying. A previous capstone team developed and tested a proof-of-concept system utilizing negative air pressure to dry almond hulls in stockpiles on a small scale. While their results demonstrated the effectiveness of negative pressure drying, their design did not account for industrial-scale applications. Building on their concept, we developed and tested a full-scale stockpile aeration system maintaining drying effectiveness of the original proof of concept. The final design of the Stockpile Aeration System is an in-ground platform utilizing negative air pressure to pull ambient air from the surface of the almond stockpile.  We demonstrated that our system can dry a stockpile equivalent to a set of hopper trailers (about 45000 lbs) effectively, uniformly, and faster than on the orchard floor.  Cost of operation for the stockpile aeration system is minimal (less than half a cent per pound).
studentNames: Abdi Rodriguez Vizarretea, Carlos Rodriguez, Enrique Estrada, Manuel Ortega

projectTitle: Shuttle Truck Connection Automation with Robotic Arm
abstract: The Morning Star Company transplants, harvests, processes, packages, and ships tomato products nationwide. During the tomato processing season, shuttle truck drivers move full trailers of tomatoes from the factory yard to unloading stations and then return the empty trailers to the yard. Part of this process involves connecting the shuttle brake lines to the trailer glad-hand brake ports prior to moving them to unload and then disconnecting the brake lines from the trailers when they are returned to the yard after emptying. The Morning Star Company wants to fully automate the factory yard movement of tomatoes and has thus tasked our team with taking the first steps toward fully automating the repetitive task of connecting and disconnecting the trailers’ brake lines. To automate this process, we used the provided UR10 robotic arm for which we designed and built an end effector that performed the movements of connecting and disconnecting a trailer’s glad hands. Our team designed a claw-like end effector that can grip onto the glad hands along with programming the robotic arm to follow a set motion path from the shuttle to the trailer's brake ports.
studentNames: Alfredo Cervantes-Agabo, Alex Hernandez Chamu, Elias Moreno, Eliana Moreno, Jazmin Claros

projectTitle: Sweet Potato Planter Robotic End Effector - Phase 2
abstract: Arnold Farms, located in Atwater, CA is a family owned farm looking to modernize their sweet potato planting process through automation. Sweet potato seedlings, referred to as slips, are difficult to singulate because they grow in clusters, which leads to entanglement. Understanding these challenges, our team redesigned the Automatic Seedling Singulation and Conveying system, which aims to singulate slips from a bin while minimizing damage to them. Slips are fed into a hopper, singulated using notched cams and flappers, dropped onto a belt, and are manually planted. This system is designed to attach to a tractor, and a NEMA 23 stepper motor is used to match the speed of the tractor, which allows planting speed to be matched. With the implementation of this system, Arnold Farms is expected to enhance planting efficiency by minimizing skips, reduce labor costs, and become a pioneer in advanced planting technology.
studentNames: David Hoehler, Hailey Romero, Morgan Phung, Shanald Lal, Xavier Walle

abstract: Sun-Maid, one of the largest raisin and dried fruit processors in the world, operates a Kingsburg, CA plant responsible for preparing, packaging, and shipping multiple products to customers globally. Our group worked with the engineering team within the plant to automate a line, taking steps towards their goal of fully automating the facility. The line required a solution to replace the need for a human to straighten misaligned packages on the conveyor belt and remove defective bags, as these issues can cause downstream damages in packaging. We approached these challenges as separate pain points, allowing us to develop a solution that effectively addresses each one individually, ensuring maximum impact. We installed a guiding arm on the line to straighten misaligned bags as they move along the conveyor belt. Additionally, we implemented a computer vision camera connected to an air rejection system, which identifies defective bags and removes them from the conveyor for recycling.
studentNames: Favour Nnaji, Kenneth Gampol, Luis Munguia Ruiz, Ricardo Barrera, Ricardo Bueno

projectTitle: Evaluating the radiolucent guide  prototypes in a cadaver model and analysis for different spine injections
abstract: Pain Away Solutions, LLC develops innovative solutions to improve drug delivery during surgical procedures. Traditional methods involve the manual needle placement at specific anatomical landmarks, each requiring real-time imaging using radiation to confirm proper positioning. This process is ineffective due to manual needle placement, long radiation exposure, inconsistent medication delivery, and extended procedure time. To address this problem, our team designed a multiport needle system with an internal splitting mechanism which distributes medication equally from one inlet to eight outlets. To validate the mechanism functionality, we conducted hydrostatic, and fluid dynamics analyses to guide the prototype design, developed and optimized using SolidWorks, a computer aided design software. We engineered the system to be compatible with standard medical connectors, like luer locks, incorporating internally tapered ports and external flaps that facilitate integration with needles or tubing. This innovative idea has the potential to streamline drug delivery by reducing procedure time and minimizing radiation exposure, ultimately advancing both healthcare and surgical efficiency.
studentNames: Airam Martinez Rodriguez, Angelin Prasad, Bryan Resuleo, Maliyah Brasil

abstract: Hilmar Cheese Company, a leading producer of high-quality cheese in Hilmar, California, strives to maintain the best possible product quality. A key challenge identified is inconsistent salt distribution during the cheese-making process, which affects the aging and overall quality of the cheese. Salt serves not only to flavor the cheese but also as an antimicrobial agent, preventing microbial growth that can lead to excessive acidity, discoloration, and "bleaching" during aging. To address this issue, our team developed a new salt distribution design, a half-Hershey’s kiss-shaped attachment for the end of the salt pipe in the cheese vat. This innovative design ensures a more even spread of salt throughout the system, reaching previously underserved areas and preventing under-salting. The solution was created within stringent dimensional and food-grade criteria to ensure safety and compatibility with consumable products. This enhancement aims to improve the quality and consistency of Hilmar Cheese's products, supporting the company’s commitment to excellence.
studentNames: Alberto Alves, Benedict-Eli Mugar, Litzy Pena, Rudy Castillo

abstract: The UC Merced Central Plant generates and distributes chilled water, heating hot water and steam. The main component that does all this is the Thermal Energy Storage Tank located on the North-East side of campus. Our project was to create a functional scale model of the Thermal Energy Storage (TES) tank and the process of distribution that occurs. The project was proposed by Mr. James Brugger, the Director of Engineering at the Central Plant at UC Merced. Mr.Brugger holds STEM outreach tours for younger students who may be interested in going into the field. Mr.Brugger currently demonstrates this with a graduated cylinder and a plastic cup with cold and hot water. While this approach demonstrates what he’s trying to explain, it is too simple and our task was to more accurately demonstrate this process. The TES tank works using water stratification, which is the process of water separating due to having different density values at different temperatures, meaning it doesn’t mix. With this in mind, our team developed a project that heats and cools water with different systems and uses a scale-model diffuser to diffuse water from the systems into our scale-model TES tank.
studentNames: Bryan Lopez, Mitchell Chan, Ricardo Cervantes, Tim Burton

abstract: The Gallo Winery is a global leader in wine production and distribution, with an impressive production capacity of approximately 300 bottles per minute per line. Currently, frequent alignment issues and part failures disrupt operations, leading to inefficiencies and increased maintenance costs. Our project addressed this challenge by designing a modified driveshaft system tailored to the winery’s needs. Inspired by the mechanics of an automotive driveshaft, our design allows for controlled deformation of the inner rod under stress, preventing complete line shutdowns. This innovative solution maintains alignment, reduces downtime, and prolongs the lifespan of critical components. The improved driveshaft system shall enhance production efficiency and contribute to cost savings by minimizing maintenance interventions and part replacements.
studentNames: Dan Fraser, Eriverto Jimenez, Efren Lizarraga, Jesus Acevedo, Jonathan Gonzalez

abstract: Dedicated to the production of spices and flavor enhancers, Sensient Natural Ingredients (SNI) provides farm-to-table solutions by cultivating natural ingredients to enhance flavor, texture, color, and nutrition. SNI transports garlic bulbs, packaged in wooden bins, from the fields of Nevada, Washington, and Oregon to dehydration facilities in Livingston, California. To ensure quality, the garlic must be clean and packaged in sanitized containers. However, the current bin-cleaning process is labor-intensive, requiring two individuals to manually wash the bins. To address this challenge, our team designed and built an operational bin washing station that can be operated by a single person. This station features a self-propelling rotor arm that uses high-pressure water to remove garlic residue from the bins effectively. With this innovation, the same individual who previously handled bin washing can now also operate a forklift to load dirty bins into the station and store them after the cleaning cycle is complete. This solution maximizes operational efficiency, reduces labor costs, and ensures the cleanliness of the bins.
studentNames: Eduardo Castaneda Manzano, Erik Gramajo, Russell Mondy, Erick Modesto Aban

abstract: The Morning Star Company, a leading tomato processor in California, experiences up to six costly collisions annually between harvesters and tractor cabs, primarily due to driver miscommunication. Each incident results in approximately $10,000 in repairs, creating a significant operational challenge. To address this, we engineered a collision mitigation system that enhances safety and reduces repair costs. The system uses an ultrasonic sensor to measure the distance and speed of approaching tractor cabs, with data processed by an Arduino. When a potential collision is detected, the system activates visual and auditory alerts, prompting the harvester driver to take corrective action. Field testing validated the system’s effectiveness at two operational heights, confirming reliable performance in real-world conditions.
studentNames: Alexander Perez, Derian Cervantes Campos, Jonathan Curiel, Kevin Kuang

abstract: The UC Merced Experimental Farm utilizes a soil sterilization chamber to eliminate organic matter, contributing to sustainable agricultural practices and advancing research. However, the current process is inefficient and labor-intensive, requiring significant manpower to operate effectively. To address these challenges, we developed an improved design for the Terra Dumper, a specialized cart engineered to optimize the soil sterilization workflow. The redesign incorporates a secure locking system to ensure stable operation by securely interfacing with the chamber, along with a trap door mechanism that enables controlled and efficient discharge of sterilized soil, reducing manual labor requirements. Constructed with lightweight yet durable materials, the Terra Dumper enhances mobility without compromising structural integrity. Additionally, custom-designed components were precision-engineered and fabricated to ensure seamless functionality and long-term reliability. This innovative redesign replaces the previous labor-intensive process with an efficient, user-friendly solution, significantly reducing labor demands while enhancing overall productivity and operational efficiency.
studentNames: Anthony Jones, Daniela Pineda, Joshua Golka, Mark Reyes

abstract: Stellantis, a multinational automotive corporation managing brands like Dodge, Jeep, and Ram, is advancing its electric vehicle (EV) transition under the "Dare Forward 2030" initiative, aiming to produce over 75 battery-electric vehicles by 2030. The U.S. Department of Energy (DOE), with support from Stellantis, launched the Battery Workforce Challenge (BWC) to foster innovation in scalable and efficient battery systems.  Our team, in partnership with the Castro Lab and Merced College, contributed to designing and testing a smaller-scale prototype EV battery pack. Initially a remote-controlled car, this model serves as a training platform, bridging new members' transition from small-scale projects to full-size vehicles like the 2024 Dodge EV ProMaster Van. Thermal and electrical simulations optimized efficiency, enabling students to design an enclosure that routes air effectively and accommodates batteries while minimizing obstruction from critical components.  These findings will guide the development of a full-scale battery pack, advancing Stellantis' and DOE’s goal of creating reliable, efficient EV batteries for commercial use.
studentNames: Cristopher Granados, David Gutierrez, Edwardo Aguilar

projectTitle: Sustainable Wildfire-Driven Sediment Management in Reservoirs
industry: Water Resource Engineering and Water Quality
abstract: The 2020 Creek Fire devastated the Upper San Joaquin Basin, a critical region supporting Millerton Lake and Friant Dam operations, providing water storage, recreation, flood control, and hydroelectric generation. The fire increased the soil erosion potential, influencing an increase in sediment deposits in Millerton Lake. The California Department of Water Resources (CA DWR) wanted to quantify the soil erosion and determine the best viable solution to prevent and manage sedimentation. With limited research on soil erosion caused by the fire, this study utilized Google Earth Engine to model soil loss and sediment yield using the Revised Universal Soil Loss Equation (RUSLE) under pre- and post-fire conditions. Design and analysis on the effects of seeding and mulching versus dredging were performed, including an in-depth evaluation of the economic feasibility over a 30-year period to find an optimal approach for reducing soil erosion in increasingly fire-prone watersheds around California’s reservoirs.
studentNames: Jessica Lau, Marco Nava, Manjot Singh

projectTitle: Bioretention for Removal of Microplastics in Stormwater
abstract: Microplastics are an emerging pollutant that can affect our health and enter our environment through stormwater runoff. Although various types of microplastics are found in stormwater, the major contributors are rubber fragments and fibers from tires on the road. Our project consists of designing a bioretention cell that can be inserted into storm drains to filter out microplastics from roads on the UC Merced campus. UC Merced has a Triple Zero Initiative that strives for outstanding environmental goals such as zero net greenhouse gas emission, waste, and net energy consumption. Therefore, our storm drains insert address the issue of microplastic waste using organic materials like sand and pea gravel. The design structure utilizes stainless steel like that already used in storm drains to avoid using more plastics. Our research highlights the need for novel, cost-effective ways to address the issue of microplastics before they enter the stormwater system.
studentNames: Julian Bowles-Baji, Juan Martinez-Aquino, Natalie Salinas, Christopher Rodriguez

projectTitle: Inland brackish water desalination with solar energy
abstract: Climate change and population growth are straining California's freshwater resources. The energy demands and pollution of desalination efforts create economic and environmental strains. Our goal is to design a sustainable water supply with a target cost of $2-3 per 1,000 gallons, through an inland brackish water desalination plant paired with solar technology producing 3.5 million gallons per day of potable water, with total dissolved solids under 500 mg/L. Our research reviewed data publicly available by the United States Geological Survey, to find an ideal location, which is Gloria Valley and its surrounding communities of Soledad and Gonzales, which have a combined population of 32,000 residents. We qualitatively looked at solar potential, transportation accessibility, and brackish water availability to optimize cost and energy-efficiency. The plant will use multiple-effect distillation with concentrated solar thermal technology. To minimize environmental impact, solar evaporation ponds will be implemented for brine mitigation.
studentNames: Diana Condori, Diana Martinez-Gomez, Alex Ramirez, Christopher Rodriguez

abstract: Modern buildings are built to withstand both internal and external forces, with the safety of the occupants as a top priority. When confronted with an unexpected force, their capacity to remain standing becomes a key test of resilience. Resilience was tested and ultimately failed on May 16, 1968 in East London when a gas explosion on the 18th story of the Ronan Point residential tower resulted in a progressive collapse that injured seventeen people and killing four. The lack of redundancy, poor material quality, and incorrect installation, current building practices and codes were brought into question nationwide. Our project evaluates the structural failure and redesigns the residential high-rise with a modular steel framework and precast concrete panels, which increases tensile strength and construction efficiency while also withstanding live, dead, wind, seismic, and instantaneous loads. Our recommendation, based on testing and structural analysis, would regularly assess new materials and construction techniques and apply them to reduce material usage, construction costs, and improve efficiency while assuring safety and code compliance.
studentNames: Eric Colomes, Jason Colomes, Eghosa Isibor

abstract: Agrecom Inc. is a leader in biosecurity solutions, servicing clients in many sectors. Agrecom faced challenges in scheduling jobs due to strict quarantine restrictions that limit technician mobility across sites. The process was manual and time consuming, commercial solutions were unable to correctly handle the necessary constraints. The team developed a CP-SAT (Constraint Programing - Satisfiability) Solving Algorithm to automate schedule creation within a web application. This system efficiently generates optimized schedules by incorporating customer, job, and employee data, while prioritizing important jobs, and managing recurring jobs. Completed schedules are automatically integrated with Microsoft Calendars, streamlining operations and enhancing efficiency.
studentNames: Ariana Curtis, Jaime Gomez, Lauren Lane, Simone Romero

abstract: Conectado is a company dedicated to providing access to numerous opportunities, targeting those underrepresented in the STEM workforce. Conectado is looking to accelerate this initiative by reaching a broader audience. The team has created a mobile application that gives users ready access to a centralized database of opportunities while also providing a platform to network with others on the app. It offers a dynamic experience for users to find and save opportunities matching their needs, and a robust and scalable chat solution utilizing the ChatKitty API. With this app, Conectado will be one step closer to bridging the gap between underrepresented individuals and opportunities in the STEM workforce, fostering greater diversity and inclusivity across various industries.
studentNames: Christian Gallien, Ernesto Reyes, John Lor, Kristopher Tern

projectTitle: Digital AI Backpack - Opportunity Matcher and Recommender
abstract: Conectado, a Bay Area-based EdTech company, is dedicated to uplifting the next generation of Latino and underrepresented groups in STEM. The company’s mission is to empower students and working professionals through educational bootcamps, positioning them as leading candidates for academic and career pursuits. Although traditional recommendation algorithms are reliable, Conectado is seeking a more adaptive and sophisticated solution to better meet user needs. In response, our team has developed an AI chatbot integrated into their web platform. This chatbot employs Flowise AI and LangChain to deliver personalized job recommendations, thereby enhancing user satisfaction and fostering career advancement. Our team’s initiative underscores our dedication to innovation and the seamless integration of advanced AI technologies within educational and career development frameworks.
studentNames: Ben Dong, Haolin Li, Justin Chang, Simran Jhinger

projectTitle: Building LLM-powered Tools to Improve INNOVIT’s Internal and External Operations
abstract: INNOVIT is an Italian Innovation and Culture Hub, supported by the Italian Ministry of Foreign Affairs and managed by the Italian Consulate General in San Francisco and the Italian Embassy in Washington DC. They assist Italian startups and Small Medium Enterprises (SMEs) in entering the US market and connecting with US investors through workshops and events. INNOVIT sought the development of a customized AI Chatbot to aid in event planning and organization. To implement this, the team developed an AI chatbot within Slack that has access to various data such as mentors, workshop ideas, and workshop schedules. Additionally, the team was requested to create a public-facing chatbot that would be incorporated into INNOVIT’s website, that could answer frequently asked questions to visitors. Both applications strive to fulfill the task of providing information efficiently and innovatively with a user-friendly interface.
studentNames: Ariana Quiroz-Alvarez, Claire Hallock, Jose Ruiz, Kianna Ng

abstract: Olixus Inc., renowned for its Silicon Valley-based enterprise strategy SaaS innovations, is currently working to provide a knowledge learning framework that will assist the transition from the 10th revision of International Classifications of Diseases (ICD-10) to the 11th, ICD-11. The previous transition from ICD-9 to ICD-10 took 10 years when there were around 15000 codes. Now with ICD-11 having over 55,000 codes, this is a monumental task to achieve. The primary objective was to empower medical coders while potentially extending support to diverse knowledge domains. To address this, the team designed a comprehensive learning system that integrated knowledge organization principles and neuroscience-based learning methodologies such as W-O-P-R (Write, Organize, Practice, Rehearse). Notably, the team incorporated features like matching descriptions, code inputting, and note-saving functionalities to facilitate a seamless learning experience
studentNames: Draven Medina, Daniel Xing, Eric Rydberg, Harold Aranda Salas, Kiera Pfann

projectTitle: Deadlock Prevention/Recovery for Mobile Robot Fleets
abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies, Inc. (ORT) is an automation technology development company focused on robotics and design manufacturing. Omron’s autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) have had issues regarding deadlocks, where two or more robots are unable to move due to mutual blocking. Correcting this requires manual intervention, which slows production time. Our team employed a machine learning approach for preventing a specific type of deadlock that frequently occurs, by detecting if a robot is “Taxilining” or not with surrounding robots. The models explored include Random Forest, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Support Vector Machine (SVM), and XGBoost, with different models achieving varying accuracies in the 90% ranges.
studentNames: Carolyn Cui, Damian Gabriel, Zakaria Fares

abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies, Inc., a leader in robotics, is transitioning from utilizing basic computer vision to advanced AI tools to enhance operational efficiency and quality control. In collaboration with Omron, our team developed Anomaleaf, a React Native mobile application aimed at identifying anomalies in submitted images based on a leaf dataset. Utilizing an 8-layer convolutional neural network (CNN), the CNN was trained on a dataset of 10,000 images to detect irregular patterns (Bacterial infections, mold, etc.) resulting in high precision when classifying/predicting. The application enables users to upload single or batch images for real-time anomaly detection. This proof of concept not only demonstrated the potential of machine learning in automating quality control processes but also may reduce time and costs associated with manual inspection.
studentNames: Andrew Garza, Alexander Hitt, Angel Ibarra, Cristian Espinosa, Javier Miranda

projectTitle: Goal Reach Time Estimation and Update Based on Previously Cached Results
abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies tasked Team PathFinder with developing algorithms and writing code in C++ to enhance the previous time estimation design for robots navigating to jobs. The team's methodology involved extracting data from map files to establish goals and obstacles in the workspace. By employing graph theory and A* algorithms, optimal routes and time estimations were determined. These predictions optimized job assignments, and upon task deployment, initial positions and traversal times were stored to refine future estimations. The team implemented efficient data structures to facilitate robot assignment and accurate time estimation updates, ensuring seamless fleet management.
studentNames: Raivat Alwar, Tyler Truong, Vansh Venugopal

abstract: Sweep designs big data workflow software for processing various data types. Using their API, they create custom workflow solutions for their customers. Large Language Models (LLMs) require extensive data for effective training, but information can become lost due to the sheer volume of documents created by the company on a regular basis. Our project was to leverage Apache Solr to index and extract machine-readable text while allowing users to categorize documents into "buckets" based on their directories. We designed an API and a user-friendly interface to go with it, streamlining document retrieval and organization, and enabling efficient categorization and indexing for enhanced workflow management.
studentNames: Aaron Nam, CJ Cox, Cameron Wat, Jose Cano Gallardo, Wendy Haw

abstract: Sweep, a data-driven solutions company in Merced, CA, aims to enhance workflow efficiency. Artificial Intelligence (AI) assistants hold promise but pose security risks with sensitive data. To address security risk concerns, we developed a Flask-based Application Program Interface (API) integrating a locally deployed Large Language Model (LLM). This API manages conversations, generates responses, and handles user authentication securely using Microsoft Single Sign-On (SSO) with OAuth 2.0. It employs PostgreSQL for data storage.The API allows clients to have conversations with the LLM safely within their local network, ensuring privacy and fostering user trust. Subsequent to May 2024, forthcoming efforts may emphasize integrating a web browser extension user interface (UI), and compartmentalization of the application using docker.
studentNames: Arav Gupta, Jason Holmes, Lester Kendrick, Nikita Yadav, Stephen Schertz

abstract: Sweep is a Merced-based company that creates workflow optimization tools.  One of its objectives is to provide clients with the ability to privately train an LLM on a custom dataset, in order to protect customer data and tailor a selected Large Language Model, or LLM, to a specific task. Our team, ChatCats, has developed a solution for this challenge, creating a database manager and model trainer that work in tandem to allow users to securely select and train their own LLMs.
studentNames: Ashley Gonzalez Perez, AJ Grant, Rodolfo Galvan Nunez, Rohit Rao, Thomas Kellogg

projectTitle: Customer Process Improvement: IoT Evaluation Made Simple
abstract: Telit Cinterion is a leading global technology firm specializing in Internet of Things (IoT) products and services. Among these services is the provision of small-scale innovation kits, enabling customers to explore IoT innovations before committing to larger enterprise solutions. The problem is that each piece of the bundled solution has its own manual acquisition process, making the ordering experience for customers tedious. Our team was tasked with creating a Proof-of-Concept web application to solve this problem by simplifying the process for customers while providing data traceability for Telit Cinterion. This application shall be tested by Telit and shall produce a simplified ordering process for customers and clear data analytics for Telit.
studentNames: Bailey Davis, Corbin Ward, Eric Contreras, Kyle Awayan, Rafiul Islam

abstract: The Morning Star Company is a tomato harvesting and processing company that uses pen and paper to log field repairs. Our team was tasked with creating an app that would allow mechanics to scan parts in and out of supply trailers to improve speed and accuracy. Mechanics are able to specify what machine a part is for, the trailer the part is from, and update a remote database with their activity. An admin page allows for designating which trailers and machines a given mechanic has access to. The app sends admins an email report twice a week on repair activity.
studentNames: Artur Musayelyan, Briam Martinez, Heath Bacon, Isaac Sousa, Steven Spangler

projectTitle: Automated Identification of Paste Visual Finish
abstract: Morning Star, based in California’s Central Valley, operates three tomato processing factories with the largest production scale in the world, supplying over 40% of domestic markets with diced tomatoes and tomato paste. They process their tomato paste in a range of finishes using various screens and filters, with a paste technician inspecting a sample from the batch to determine its final finish. However, even with standardized processing and equipment, finishes can vary, and human error can lead to different finishes being labeled the same. Through a machine learning model, the team automated the analysis of the tomato paste with a high level of accuracy and consistency while enabling detection of anomalies and data compilation. Packaged into a full stack web app, the design significantly streamlines the analysis of tomato paste and is a powerful tool for analyzing various metrics of the company’s paste processing.
studentNames: Eric Ferguson, Jack McGuinness, Kevin Ramirez Guzman, Liam Morgan, Lorenzo Stewart

abstract: Turlock Irrigation District has been providing water to Central California farmers since 1882.  Using water from Turlock Lake and a series of canals, their water distribution officers work in the field as well as in the office to organize and schedule water orders.  Since they updated their workflow to use Microsoft Excel as one of their primary tools, water distribution officers have struggled to mold the software to meet their needs, specifically with regards to ease of use, data security and synchronicity.  To fix this, the team has developed a cloud based organizational tool designed to mimic excel while providing functionality tailored to TID’s unique work environment.
studentNames: An Nguyen, Clayton Cygnet, Christian Duan, Henry Wang, Noorulain Irshad

abstract: Turlock Irrigation District (TID) has provided reliable irrigation water and electric service since 1887. TID relied on a spreadsheet application for scheduling orders, but the process was slow and lacked flexibility. The team streamlined the process by developing a new application with drag-and-drop features, significantly enhancing the speed and efficiency of order processing. The application also includes a dynamic user interface that allows users to collapse and expand spreadsheets, optimizing visual space management. This innovative approach boosts productivity and simplifies order scheduling tasks.
studentNames: Christopher Tapia, Esteban Orihuela, Michael Moua, Steven Plascencia Galvan, Virginia Manandeg

abstract: The Computer Science and Engineering Department at UC Merced has been committed to optimizing educational pathways for its students, specifically in aligning course selections with their academic prerequisites and career ambitions. The existing tool, My Degree Path, although effective for general academic planning, has limitations in accommodating the specialized needs of Computer Science and Engineering students, particularly in selecting CSE electives that match their career trajectories. The team has leveraged the OpenAI API to develop a system that utilizes ChatGPT to interact directly with students. This system understands students' career interests and integrates this data with their academic prerequisites and UC Merced’s available courses. By providing personalized educational guidance tailored to individual career goals, this approach ensures a more aligned and effective educational journey for each student.
studentNames: Calvin Bayaua, Gaige Bonderer, Jennifer Rosen, Sofia Nicole Cando

projectTitle: Relevance Weighted Multiple Meeting Scheduler - Phase 2
abstract: Innovate to Grow (I2G) is a unique “experiential learning” program that connects external partner organizations with teams of students to solve real-world problems. One of these problems is the limited capabilities of services or applications that allow attendees and organizers to determine optimal meeting times. Team Sync addressed this problem by creating a visual analysis tool that gives more power to event organizers when creating schedules and displays real-time variations of different attendee schedules for physical and virtual availability. The team put together a web application that allows attendees to create a schedule with an availability range for each block and allows the organizer to view an aggregate schedule based on the adjustable relevance of each attendee.
studentNames: Gabriel Lee, Ikenna Onuorah, Paul Stratton, Richard Edwards, Raul Viray Jr

projectTitle: I2G Mobile App and Event Registration
abstract: UC Merced’s Innovate to Grow (I2G) is a program that brings together students and external partners to solve complex, real-world problems. Currently, the program faces challenges with its time-intensive manual check-in process. To address the issue, our team devised a QR scanning system for effortless use by both staff and attendees, while concurrently enhancing the mobile view of the website. This addition significantly reduces wait times and provides a modern, streamlined way to engage with the event, demonstrating a substantial improvement over the previous system.
studentNames: Aakash Koirala, Donte' Williams, Luis Zaragoza, Valerie Zaira Pulido, Zhijian Wang

abstract: The School of Engineering (SoE) Instructional Lab Services currently supports Capstone students with purchasing items and equipment through a time-consuming, manual process. To address the issue, the Capstone Purchasing App was introduced to automate the purchasing process. For Phase 2, the team built on the foundation of the existing web app implementing core features that enhance the efficiency and user experience for both students and faculty. These features and changes include automatic saving of requests, updated data storage and documentation, an email notification system, dynamic account creation, and improved administrative control in team, roster, and budget management. As a result, the web app will effectively improve and cut current and future time expenses for students and faculty during the purchasing process.
studentNames: Adam Bowen, Madeline Reiterman, Swapnil Kumar, Said Olguin

abstract: United States Cold Storage (USCS) operates 42 refrigeration and distribution facilities in 13 states across the US. The facility in Turlock wants us to create app that streamlines the data collection and California Air Resource Board (CARB) regulation compliance for the refrigerated trucks and allow for drivers to enter pertinent information before they arrive to check into the facility. Our team has developed an app that collects this information from the driver, verifies the CARB Identification Number drivers provided by using the CARB Transport Refrigeration Unit Compliance Website, and aggregates the information into a QR code that is scannable by the guard shack when drivers check into the facility. This QR code leads back to the form with all the information and allows it to be checked by the guard and extracted into the USCS Yard Management System by the clerks inside the offices.
studentNames: Alex Wan, Dovran Rejepov, Kameron Tankhamvang, Ramiro Cuevas, Rohan Makkar

teamName: five merry men and also one merry woman
projectTitle: Remote Herd Management Enabled by Technology
abstract: California Ag Solutions, based in Madera CA, is an agritech consulting company that sends specialist experts to farms to help clients grow premium crops efficiently and sustainably. Overgrazing has been a consistent problem for a lot of their clients, and they want to address this with their newest project. As of recently, Ag Solutions are looking into how to remotely move livestock around a grazing area using various technologies to prevent overgrazing. In accordance with this goal our team was tasked with simulating the desired experience for a herd manager using an application. Our team designed a project where a user would log in to an app where they can interact with a map by editing zone areas and cattle, and moving said cattle between zones over a period of time.
studentNames: Aditya Lakkoju, Allyson Saelee, Brandon Jia, Carlos Trimbell, Ritesh Patro

projectTitle: Remote Herd Management Enabled by Technology
abstract: The client, California Ag Solutions, is an agronomy consulting company servicing agricultural operations throughout central California. The client has devised an innovative solution aiming to revolutionize livestock management through the use of real-time location data and geofencing. The team was tasked with creating an application to illustrate the system’s functionalities, and developed a user-friendly web application that allows for defining grazing zones, setting boundaries, and initiating herd movement seamlessly. The live map provides real time tracking of animal locations, which allows herd managers to optimize grazing strategies. The team’s application will be used by the client as the basis for implementing the full solution, which will boost overall efficiency and sustainability when keeping livestock.
studentNames: Angel Gurrola, Ethan Chen, Erick Hieng, Francisco Arias, Ryan Zhou

abstract: VoiceAdvance is a transformative platform developed by our team for Western Digital Corporation, empowering employees to anonymously voice their opinions and provide constructive feedback. This fosters transparency and engagement within the enterprise, creating a safe space for communication where every member can contribute to the organization's growth and success without fear of scrutiny. Our aim in developing VoiceAdvance for Western Digital is to increase employee engagement, improve response efficiency and decision-making, and foster a transparent and proactive workplace culture. The user interface was designed for a fast and responsive user experience using React, complemented by a backend powered by Python for efficiency and served with SQLite3 for a lightweight yet robust database.
studentNames: Austin Brown, Andre Limos, Luis Ornelas-Montes, Seth O'masters

abstract: Western Digital is looking for an efficient and effective way to gather feedback from its employees. Currently, there is no effective software for the client to gather employee feedback that supports nonstop service, anonymity, and privatized data. Our team has developed a solution to fill this void in the form of the VoiceAdvance project. VoiceAdvance is a new and efficient web application that supports these features while providing an enjoyable user experience. VoiceAdvance will allow Western Digital to harness the power of employee feedback by having the ability to view and respond to their employees comments, suggestions, and concerns and provide their employees with a solution which will drive positive change and enhance engagement within the organization. Our team focused extensively on developing efficient and accessible web pages using REACT that both employees and resolvers will interact with when submitting and resolving comments. The backend integrates Node.js and the team used PostgreSQL as the secure database.
studentNames: Haris Khan, Julien Crespo, James Phillips, Lucero Ascencio, Simon Liu

abstract: The client, Western Digital Corporation, headquartered in San Jose, CA, is a global leader in designing, manufacturing, and marketing data technology solutions worldwide. As a multinational corporation, Western Digital Corporation faces the challenge of effectively recognizing and rewarding outstanding employee performance across its global operations. To address this challenge, the team embarked on creating a robust web application aimed at establishing a cohesive system for tracking and acknowledging achievements within Western Digital. This initiative resulted in the development of a user-friendly web application, serving as a centralized platform to efficiently record and celebrate employee accomplishments utilizing adaptable industry-standard software.
studentNames: Bodrul Babul, Daniel Ramirez, Michael Vangsycha

abstract: Western Digital Corporation (WDC) is an American computer hard disk drive (HDD) and solid-state drive (SSD) manufacturer and data storage company. Western Digital oversees many employees and needs a streamlined process of promoting their employees. That’s why the Talent Review Team created the Engineering Talent Review Application to assist with the internal promotion process of WDC. The application will allow for employees to be nominated for a promotion, be recommended by their peers, and finally sent to the review committee for final approval.
studentNames: Braydon Hart, Crystal Rajan, Elvis Bui, Eric Casillas, Raphael Cruanes

abstract: The Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) system serves as a crucial heavy rail public transit network connecting California's Bay Area. Vent structures situated on the surface are imperative for expelling air and controlling the tunnel environment of underground rail alignments through fan operation to supply or exhaust air. However, the noise generated by the expulsion of air poses a notable disturbance to nearby residential areas. To mitigate this issue while preserving optimal airflow, our proposal entailed a practical construction solution atop the existing structure, incorporating sound-absorbing concrete engineered to diminish noise levels in alignment with predominant frequencies emitted at the site. Furthermore, we advocated for the deployment of acoustic louvers to supplement noise mitigation efforts without compromising airflow rates. This integrated approach aimed to enhance the environmental sustainability and community livability surrounding BART infrastructure. Additionally, the project enabled BART to develop a straightforward capital construction contract for pilot testing and future installation.
studentNames: Brinda Pandi, Elijah Lopez, Omar Estrada Marquez, Samuel Simoes

abstract: Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) is a public transportation system that connects the San Francisco Peninsula to the East Bay and South Bay through 50 stations across six routes. During revenue operating hours, there are a few sections of the circuit where an audible screech can be heard. Our group was tasked with finding a solution to reduce the noise and make the commuting experience more pleasant. Our team of engineers developed a solution consisting of a combined train skirt and ring damper. With this design our team focused on modeling the skirt for the train, using Matlab to simulate the sound attenuated by our design; proving the skirt was viable as a means of reducing the noise passengers experienced.
studentNames: Daniel Suriano Quiroa, Esai Lucatero, Erick Torres, Leonardo Lujan

abstract: This project addresses an issue with the water efficiency of the Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) train washing system. BART aims to decrease water loss in the system by at least 10%. Currently, although 90% of the water used is recollected, approximately 10.4 gallons are still lost per train car washed. The main source of water loss occurs when the wet trains exit the area with recollection reservoirs, leading to water remaining on the trains being lost. As a result, BART tasked us with developing a solution to enhance water recollection or reduce water usage in the system. Our team devised a design to tackle this challenge: a frame equipped with squeegees intended to push water off the train cars while they are still within the area containing water recollection reservoirs. This design incorporates a torsional spring mechanism to apply suitable pressure, ensuring efficient water removal from the tops of the trains where water tends to accumulate. Importantly, this mechanism was designed not to damage the trains or disrupt the cleaning rate of the system. Through small-scale testing, we confirmed the effectiveness of this solution in addressing the water loss issue.
studentNames: Andres Aguilar, Jason Baltazar, Jeremy Dominguez, Ronaldo Maldonado

abstract: Blue Diamond Growers’ Salida facility located in Modesto, receives a substantial number of Almonds between August to December. The almonds received go through a sampling process that determines their variety, moisture content, and quality. Current sampling methods involve using a probe and a scoop that are inadequate in collecting a representative sample of an entire almond bin and lack some ergonomic usability. For this reason, our team was tasked to create a handheld sampling tool that is ergonomic, efficient, and effective in obtaining a representative sample of almonds. Our team designed a motorized hollow auger with one-way entrance hole slots for the almonds. The auger also includes a push lock cone insert for the almonds to be released. From this design, our team created a prototype that underwent substantial testing exploring almond breakage, quantity of almonds collected, and speed of collection. This tool provides a new solution for a sampling method that is currently inadequate.
studentNames: Breanna Canfield, Isela Pina, Juan Estrada, Kevin Arrieta

abstract: CFT Group, based in Parma, Italy, specializes in designing and manufacturing machinery for various industries, including food and beverage. The Morningstar Company in California, a key client, relies heavily on  cylindrical screens for processing tomato paste and fruit purees. However, challenges such as frequent replacements, long-distance shipping, and potential damage during transit pose operational risks. Our objective was to create an environmentally friendly, ergonomic, and affordable packaging solution that will not only prevent damage to the screen but also track its location, acceleration, and velocity. To achieve this, we created two sets of corrugated cardboard inserts that surround and protect the filter. Two polygonal cone inserts go in the top and bottom of the screen, preventing damage to the rims. Four corner inserts reinforce the structural integrity of the box and support the screen. All the empty spaces are full of craft paper that cushions the screen. A tracking chip located on the door of the shipping container tracks location, acceleration and velocity which allows for the client to deduce when, where and how a screen could receive damage.
studentNames: Alexander Serrano, Myca Erica Icban, Yajaira Elizondo, Yessica Mares Barriga

abstract: Del Monte Foods, Inc., a leading producer and distributor of premium branded food products in the U.S. retail market, has identified a few bottlenecks in the production line of fruit cups for bulk orders. The bottleneck we observed was at a 90-degree turn from production to packaging, where fruit cups frequently become congested, leading to downtime and reduced output potential. To address this issue, our team collaborated with Del Monte to develop a targeted solution that modifies the existing diverter arms used to direct the product through the turn. By integrating bearings into the design of the diverter arms, our innovation facilitates a smoother transition of fruit cups at the turn, effectively reducing jam frequencies and enhancing overall production efficiency. Additionally, we conducted a site analysis to identify other potential areas for implementing this solution, ensuring broad-scale improvements in operational performance at Del Monte Foods.
studentNames: Erick Rosiles, Isaiah Johnson, Juliana Leal, Christian-Alexa Jimenez

abstract: E. & J. Gallo Winery, a global leader in the wine industry, has taken charge of much of its production, from wine cultivation to shipping to the nearest supermarket. However, due to some outsourcing, problems have arisen, particularly regarding the deformation of cardboard from various structural and thermal standpoints. In response, our team was tasked with designing and creating a machine capable of fixing both types of deformations. The machine we developed is a combination of a heat press and an industrial roller, designed to be durable, transportable, efficient, safe, and ergonomic. With this innovative piece of machinery, our company will be able to reduce waste of unusable fiber (cardboard) and reallocate those savings to other departments.
studentNames: Angelica Uvalle, Jose Saenz-Dominguez, Katherine Ng, Ryan Atapattu

abstract: E&J Gallo Winery is a winery and distributor headquartered in Modesto, California. Mechanics internally rebuild filler valve assemblies which are installed on line 2/93, a machine that fills wine bottles. For most of the filler valve assemblies, the mechanics do not know if the component was rebuilt successfully until it is installed on the line, which could be several months after the mechanics engage with it. Our team was tasked with creating a test stand that could determine if the filler valves assembly were rebuilt correctly offline. Our team designed a test stand that can support the weight of the filler valve assembly, and is ready to be utilized with the manufacturer's programming when received. Using this design, the mechanics will be able to test the operation of the filler valve assembly without installing it on line 2/93.
studentNames: Alan Ramirez, Jayesh Prakash, Josue Rivas, Ramon Vargas

abstract: The client, Frito Lay, is a large manufacturer of snack foods located in Modesto, California. This facility is responsible for preparing, packaging, and shipping multiple products to customers throughout the state. Their Geographic Enterprise Systems (GES) department has been experiencing issues with boxes turning improperly on the conveyors, which results in them being sent to the Perfect Labeling Line (PSL) for a new label. This delay has caused a decrease in efficiency and has impacted the conveyor system's ability to ship out boxes to customers. Our team looked at various areas in the conveyor system where the boxes appeared to be turning and decided to focus on one area. We implemented the addition of speed rollers to the side railing to increase efficiency and prevent boxes from turning when hitting a corner or curve of the conveyor system.
studentNames: Gustavo Hernandez, Jack De Jesus, Renato Santana

projectTitle: Design and implementation of a robotic actuation mechanism for collecting leaf spectral data.
abstract: The Internet of Things for Precision Agriculture (IoT4Ag) is an engineering research organization whose mission is to transform and revolutionize agricultural technologies. IoT4Ag proposed to develop an autonomous robotic activation mechanism that supports a spectrometer to determine and limit water stress and disease in crops and notify growers to protect crop health and prevent loss in produce. Since manual data collection is time-consuming, labor-intensive, and ineffective, CAP-124 has designed key components and assembled the low-cost and robust robotic arm. The telescoping arm extends using a pulley system and autonomously collects leaf spectral data. The arm can be mounted onto various mobile robots, which will operate to help reduce water stress and ultimately reduce crop loss.
studentNames: Brock Fusi, Cameron Mercer, Juan Vigil, Valerie Larios

projectTitle: 3D Printing Funnel CPC Blocks 2-Set for Thru-Reflector-Wall (TRW) Solar Cooker-Kitchens
abstract: Joel H. Goodman is a retired professor of architecture who noted inefficient and unsustainable cooking methods in rural communities. Motivated by bridging the gap between sustainable living and underdeveloped communities, Joel began developing the idea of a reflective solar cooker. To bring forth Joel’s vision, we were tasked with designing a solar cooker to be  permanently integrated into a building and able to direct variable sun rays towards the cooking surface with its funnel-like shape. Through the use of both a fixed mirror reflector attached above the funnel structure and an adjustable East-West portable reflector, the cooker can be adjusted to the Sun. Focusing on tropical and subtropical communities, our design is innovative as it can accommodate cooking through multiple times of day while being durable enough to withstand the region's various seasons. This solar cooker design gives rural communities a reliable method of cooking while decreasing the carbon footprint of traditional methods.
studentNames: Daniel Christian Abrenica, Ruth Agorrilla, Santiago Garcia, Ukamaka Ezimora

abstract: Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory (LLNL) is an organization that focuses on the U.S global stability and national security by supporting multidisciplinary teams to develop new technology. LLNL has an advanced manufacturing program where metal additive manufacturing (AM) plays a central role. Their current machines do not have any form of monitoring or diagnosis for the ongoing manufacturing, making it difficult to troubleshoot issues. Our team developed a mechanical system that is able to record a video and audio live feed of the AM process. We designed a fixed mounting system to be installed on the build volume of the AM machine that includes a metallic plate where the camera is mounted and a metallic cylindrical-cup that holds the microphone. Another part of our system was a slip window with a welding glass shade which covers the porthole of the build volume and protects the camera from any bright flares produced from the AM process.
studentNames: Bill Pham, Cameron Bullock, Jayro Ortiz-Ayala, Viridiana Barreto Bermudez

abstract: This Capstone project, in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Lab, addresses the need for enhanced safety when removing components for cleaning and sanitation after metal powder reactivity testing. We have designed and constructed specialized tools capable of safely and efficiently removing each component of the explosive power nozzle used in testing procedures.  The tools we developed ensure a safe and repeatable method for removing the powder dispersion nozzle, grate, nipple, and sleeve without necessitating modifications to the manufacturer-supplied components. Through meticulous design and testing, we have created a set of unique tools that significantly improve the safety and cleanliness of the cleaning operation. These tools are poised to enhance the efficiency of metal powder reactivity testing, leading to increased turnover rates between experiments. By streamlining the removal process and maintaining the integrity of the testing equipment.
studentNames: Christopher Navarro, Emmanuel Maldonado, Jason Estevez, Nicolas Nicosia

abstract: OFI, an almond company based in Hughson, CA, specializes in the production and distribution of almonds to various retail outlets. The company has encountered an issue during the packaging process where there is a spillage occurring between the funnel and claw machinery. To address this challenge, we developed a prototype 3D printed funnel designed to mitigate almond spillage. Upon successful testing and validation of the 3D printed model, OFI plans to manufacture a permanent stainless steel funnel based on the design we had done. This stainless steel funnel will be positioned between the claw and funnel, effectively preventing future spillage incidents during the packaging process.
studentNames: Bryan Brito, Phong Le, Viviana Dazo, Zeth Carmona

abstract: OFI is an almond company located in Hughson, CA. OFI produces roasted almonds, salted almonds, macadamias, and flour. OFI is currently encountering product loss in their PK3 belt line. The main cause for this loss is due to product residue built up in the top corners of the container where the product is being weighed at a 25-pound limit. The main goal of this project was to reduce the amount of product loss by removing the residue left behind in the container. Our solution to this problem was to extend the length of the centron and the length of the top conveyor. The reason for these modifications is to prevent the product from building up near the corners. The design was tested by OFI, and the product was dropped further into the container avoiding the back wall and it would overall improve the system.
studentNames: Alan Armenta Vazquez, Madelyn De Leon, Omar Martinez, Victor Haro

projectTitle: Precise Measurement Analysis for Interventional pain procedures to under fluoroscopic guidance
abstract: Our client, Dr.Gabriel Garcia-Diaz, is a spine orthopedic surgeon and the founder of OrthoSpine Advance Health, Inc. OrthoSpine is a medical clinic specializing in neck and back pain management. The pain management injections are performed under fluoroscopic guidance. The current technique requires individual placement and checking of needles, which can be time-consuming and may lead to radiation overexposure in healthcare providers and patients. Our project involved collecting the measurements/distances from the spinal needles after insertion under fluoroscopic guidance for different pain procedures such as transforaminal epidural blocks, medial branch blocks, and radiofrequency ablations (thoracic/lumbar). These measurements were obtained during a clinical study determining if there is a statistical difference between the current technique and the PAS technique.  Utilizing this data, our team created a radiolucent guide for precise needle placement to mediate exposure during these procedures.
studentNames: Aaliyah Ruiz-Corral, Brian Lu, Jenifer Hernandez Garcia, Jarmaine Roxas

projectTitle: Precise Syringe Pressure Analysis for spinal pain management injections and joints
abstract: Dr. Gabriel Garcia-Diaz is an orthopedic spine surgeon and co-founder of Pain Away Solutions, here in Merced. Currently and historically, drug delivery via syringe is done manually and requires great pressure to deliver the medication to the patients especially when performing anesthetic nerve blocks. The major problem observed after a high repetition of these procedures is the increased rate of overuse injuries such as carpal tunnel syndrome. Our Capstone engineering team assembled a research project prototype that measures the real-time force applied on a syringe when injecting medication into the spine of a patient. The measurements obtained provided the amount of force required to deliver medication into the spine using different needles and syringe sizes performed at different anatomical spine locations for a safe outcome.
studentNames: Edward Franco, Joseph Robles, Sherwin Serrano, Yumie Lee

abstract: In collaboration with Morning Star, California's largest tomato product processor, our team aimed to boost warehouse efficiency and safety.
