The existing system's frequent path collisions and the manual labor needed to move drums to pallets compromised both safety and throughput. We designed a system featuring pallet dispensers, a conveyor, and a camera system that allows operators to sort pallets and streamline barrel placement. Enhanced by real-time visual feedback and a programmable logic controller (PLC), this robust design is expected to significantly enhance productivity and workflow efficiency, aligning with Morning Star’s commitment to safety. Once implemented, it will help manage 10% of the world’s processed tomato products.
studentNames: David Millet, Maximo Zendejas, Ragav Mohan, Rafael Valencia

abstract: UC Merced will open a Chemical Engineering Department in the near future with Dr. Kara McCloskey in charge of CHemE department. She has tasked our group with making an electrospinner that contains a flat plate and a mandrel to use in future CHemE labs. Electrospinning is an essential process for lab experiments since it creates fibers that are less than one micrometer in diameter. The spun fibers can be bound with cells or any other elements in the development of medical technologies. Before creating a design from scratch, our team conducted extensive research on the different design approaches followed by manufacturers of electrospinners. Our design incorporates all the required components requested by the client and consists of a base collector that supports both the flat and mandrel collection plate. We also have acquired a syringe pump and a high voltage power supply (kV) which connects the entire system together and makes it operational. Our design is capable of spinning the required fibers under prescribed rotational speed, polymer and solvent mixture, distance between syringe and base collector, and applied voltage.
studentNames: Allen Nicasio, Joshua Ancheta, Juan Hernandez, Viridiana Moreno

projectTitle: 3D Printed swag used to give out on Bobcat Day for outreaching and engagement purposes
abstract: The Materials Science and Engineering (MSE) major is a very small but distinguished part of the School of Engineering at the University of California, Merced. UC Merced wants to increase enrollment for the Materials Science department as well as bring about awareness for how MSE impacts the engineering world. Christopher Viney, a professor and former chair of the MSE department, proposed to fabricate physical incentives to encourage incoming UC Merced students to enroll into the MSE major. This idea led to the creation of credit-card sized tripods to be passed out as department merch to the students during Bobcat Day, a day dedicated to showcasing what UC Merced has to offer to incoming students. The credit-card sized tripod was designed for photo taking on-the-go and is meant to be carried around in your wallet. Our team is made up of students in different engineering disciplines, with various engineering skills, where our different strengths were used to design and create a unique and creative prototype. These skills included, but were not limited to, designing specific components using computer-aided design programs and performing materials selection processes. The final design was chosen under the constraints of withstanding extreme heat, bending, and load bearing. Streamline 3D, a local additive manufacturing company in Fresno, printed the various prototypes which completed the last step in our design process. One hundred units were handed out and surveyed by incoming students. Feedback was collected to further improve the credit-card sized tripod in its functionality and usability. The data collected will be utilized by following capstone teams to create the best design possible for future Bobcat Days.
studentNames: Gen Duenas, Jaeden Pulanco, Michael Millan Amaro, Simon Shinozaki

projectTitle: Computer controlled motion control of a cell stretcher
abstract: Our client is the MECHANO3B[i]OLOGY Laboratory at the Department of Materials Science and Engineering from the University of California, Merced. The overall research efforts are geared towards understanding how mechanical forces at the molecular scale affect function in articulated joints and epithelial tissues. The Cell Stretcher is an apparatus designed to produce an equibiaxial strain on elastic substrates for dynamic imaging of cells on physiologically relevant substrates. Through this device, the client will be able to mechanically stimulate epithelial cells. The challenges we faced were: i) a non-operational apparatus, resulting from the overcharge of the device’s stepper motor circuit, and ii) the device’s leveling stage having a furrowed glide when in operation, causing unequal biaxial strain on the substrate. These challenges were addressed by replacing the original motor with an equal replacement and including a machined brass collar to the leveling stage’s unmotorized stabilization beam, to increase the surface contact area, allowing a smoother glide when the apparatus is in operation. The team also worked to test the device for step loss and program compatibility.
studentNames: David Amezcua, Dominique Gabri Inocencio, Iyari Romo, Moises Amezcua

abstract: Our project task was to perform heating and cooling load calculations and select an HVAC system for a four story library in São Paulo, Brazil, for the ASHRAE student design competition. To accomplish our task, we examined the building's envelope and construction, created thermal zone plans, used energy modeling software to produce heating and cooling load calculations, and designed the HVAC and ductwork systems.
studentNames: Carlo Camello, Edwards Castro, Nahun Hernandez, Nicholas Nunez

abstract: Umida Ag is an agricultural/irrigation company whose focus is to reduce the amount of water used in transpiration in modern agricultural practices. This project's main focus is to reduce the amount of carbon emissions and water used in planting. The goal is to supply cotton that has released fewer carbon emissions, thus leading to carbon-negative garments. In an effort to decarbonize the world, we created an auger that specifically penetrates the soil compaction layer, as opposed to a traditional till penetrating the entire field. The device is used in conjunction with the virtual water table irrigation system to reduce water and energy consumption in cotton production by 80%. After the soil is penetrated, a hopper refills the hole with soil while dispensing seeds to keep carbon inside the soil, therefore rebuilding healthy soil ideal for farming. This innovative prototype is a significant step into achieving carbon-negative cotton production.
studentNames: Alejandro Hernandez-Ramirez, Lina B-Hernandez, Ricardo Victorio Felix, Steven Silva

projectTitle: Urine Collection for Agricultural Fertilizer Use
abstract: UmidaAg based in Fresno, California is an irrigation company, striving for zero carbon production crops. Their objectives include manufacturing, selling, and installing virtual water table irrigation systems for crops. The goals are to reduce carbon inputs on cotton production and decrease limited resource usage. The problem is that synthetic fertilizer emits vast amounts of carbon. The solution is to find a source of natural fertilizer, and the idea is to use human urine as fertilizer for cotton plants. Due to urine’s nutritional properties, it’s a good synthetic fertilizer substitute. Our team designed a collection system that can transport urine from portable and custom-made urinals into a storage system for accessibility. The system has a notification system to notify the user when it is time for the collection to avoid spillage, and it is intended for plumbing/waste disposal companies in outdoor events while reducing disposal cost and human waste pollution. Carbon Negative T-shirts are the end goal for the design, and the urine collection design is important in fighting climate change to offset carbon which secures a better future for generations.
studentNames: Karson Chu, Mark Kim, Nicholas Anggriani, Serjio Orozco-Rivera

abstract: USA Produce is a family-owned business located in Los Banos focused on producing flavored nuts, dehydrated fruits, teas, olives, honey, royal honey, and garlic. As a producer of pistachios, they have been confronted with the challenge of reducing water usage for removing the skins off pistachios shells. AgriTech Innovation engineers innovated a new design to remove the skin off a pistachio nut without using water. This design involves using a drum with an extruded metal surface and a metal wall with edges to shear the skin of the pistachio off into a collection bin. The proposed method of removal allows for a sustainable design compromised of off-the-shelf components and food-grade stainless steel. This innovative design will reduce the processing cost of skin removal for pistachios and maintain the pistachio nuts’ structural integrity.
studentNames: Colby Priest, Isaias Teran, Justin Arroyo, Todd Miller

projectTitle: Effect of Climate Change  on Cardiovascular Diseases and Vascular Age using smart-sensors technology
abstract: X10e Inc is a biotech company that aims to reduce vascular age and minimize the risk of cardiovascular diseases. Due to climate change, temperatures have been surpassing the global average, which increases the risk to cardiovascular health, among individuals with pre-existing health conditions. In response, X10e requested a methodology to obtain hemodynamic parameters using optical bio-sensing technology (PPG) and its associated mobile application under varying heat stress conditions. Our team conducted a scientific clinical study involving an expected 50 participants of diverse demographics, including age, gender, BMI, occupation, sleep pattern, ethnicity, and physical ability. Participants were asked to perform physical exercises under different heat stress conditions. Key parameters such as blood pressure and cardiac output were analyzed to identify heat vulnerability factors and assess short and long-term effects. Recommendations for data analysis pre and post-experiments were provided to enhance understanding of heat stress impacts and recovery mechanisms. Ultimately, this product can contribute in detecting and managing early onset cardiovascular disease if used regularly.
studentNames: Jose Avila-Garcia, Julian Pena Verduzco, Laura Gonzalez Rodriguez, Leonela Gutierrez, Orlando Hernandez

abstract: Agrecom is a company providing water treatment, sanitation, and pest control services to local agricultural, residential, and commercial properties. Agrecom’s workers are required to follow strict quarantine restrictions when going from one job site to the next. Consequently, schedules need to be created with these constraints in mind, which is currently a manual and time-consuming process.  The team took on this problem and developed a comprehensive solution to automate schedule creation in the form of a web application. Using customer, job, and employee information inputted by a user, an optimal schedule can be generated that follows all of the necessary constraints. This schedule can then be exported as a file or sent directly to employees as calendar invite emails.
studentNames: Damian Soto, Kevin Lafreniere, Michael Tapia, Tejas Bhartiya

projectTitle: CTTA Member Database Management and Automation
abstract: The California Tow Truck Association (CTTA) is a non-profit organization providing solutions and resources that empower towing companies across the state to be more professional and progressive within the towing and emergency road-service industry. CTTA’s current database management systems are not only difficult to navigate, but also split across multiple providers—resulting in issues of data loss when transferring information between those separated systems. The team developed a new, in-house database management system to both simplify workflows and congregate the multiple databases into one centralized system and deployed their prototype to a cloud server via Amazon Web Services.
studentNames: Cameron Tobin, Derek Stanford, Shelly Anne Abu, Tyler Armstrong

projectTitle: Digital AI Backpack - Backpack Opportunities Crawler
abstract: The Digital AI Backpack project was presented in collaboration between Conectado and Linker Finance. Conectado aims to revolutionize underrepresented inclusion in STEAM, offering a virtual platform connecting educational institutions, social impact organizations, and businesses to empower these same students in tech. Meanwhile, Linker Finance, an embedded finance platform, facilitates the rapid launch of FinTech and Bank-like apps. The project addressed the absence of a centralized database for students' opportunities, scattered across various formats. The team's objective was to extract, store, and structure opportunities, employing AI methods for text recognition, Optical Character Recognition, and Large Language Models. The result was a user-friendly app, fostering tailored recommendations for students while seamlessly integrating; financial, educational, and professional resources.
studentNames: Ana Sanchez Duran, Baixi Guo, Leo Rozay, Rodrigo Melendez

projectTitle: Building a Database for Community and Mentors Engagement
abstract: INNOVIT is a multifaceted organization with a presence in San Francisco, Milan, and the Silicon Valley, serving as an Italian Innovation Center, Cultural Institute, and Trade Agency. The organization facilitates connections between mentors and speakers from prominent industries with startups, hosting various workshop events. Recognizing the need for a more efficient method of identifying specific mentors for events or partnerships, INNOVIT identified a challenge within its current system— an abundance of disorganized spreadsheets and CSV files. To address this issue, the team has developed a comprehensive database system. This system can house diverse data related to mentors and speakers, allowing users to employ search criteria such as company name and expertise to generate specialized lists. Additionally, the system offers Import/Export functionality for CSV files, providing a user-friendly interface. In response to the current database's limitations, our team collected feedback, identified areas for improvement, and conducted thorough research to design the new system.
studentNames: Carlos Lopez, Elvis Mercado, Jorge Cruz, Maya Chidambaram

abstract: NutStar Software has been working in the tree nut industry for over 15 years providing inventory management, financial tracking, and comprehensive reports for their clients.  Lost and mishandled inventory using traditional hand scanners has caused problems for the tree nut industry and NutStar aims to solve that problem. Our team has built from a previous team’s foundation software to get the exact location of inventory in a 3D matrix. We use Bluetooth angle of Arrival locators and tags to track where inventory is, and if it has been moved. With a simple interface, a warehouse worker can easily track the exact location of inventory in a 3D matrix.
studentNames: Colton Kaelin, Justin Nguyen, Rogelio Barajas, YiXiang Ren

abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies, Inc. (ORT) develops industrial, collaborative, and mobile robots for various applications. ORT currently implements an error-prone calibration method for their six-axis robots. Team 305’s task was to develop calibration methods both for a camera mounted on the robot and for a fixed camera. These calibration methods should be accurate within 0.5mm and be easy to use. This was accomplished by using the 3D camera and stitching together point clouds of a sphere to calculate the center of the ball accurately. That center was used to generate the transformation between the robot and the camera. After this calibration method, the robot can locate objects within 0.2 mm.
studentNames: Alexander Lagarda, Angel Perez Mendoza, Ronald Chen, Robert Palermo, Sahus Nulu

abstract: OMRON is a robotics company that develops industrial, mobile, and collaborative robots to improve lives and contribute to a better society.  The objective was to estimate the State of Charge (SOC) in lithium-ion batteries found in the robots. We used Python to code the backend in which we take the CSV files given to us from OMRON, convert them to SQLite database files to then separate the charge and discharge values to calculate the charging and discharging ratios. These ratios are sent to the front end to display the graphs and show the charging and discharging times which will be updated every 5 seconds. We utilized Message Queuing Telemetry Transport (MQTT) in Python to create an application enabling multiple users to monitor battery progress in real-time. This project will provide OMRON with the ability to streamline the power management of the batteries and enhance user awareness by showing them the current battery charge/discharging status.
studentNames: Luis Gonzalez, Margarito Avila, Mario Barrera, Maithreyee Vatsan, Ray Aguilar

abstract: The client, Sunburst Ag Research and Innovation Center, conducts plant, soil, and water diagnostics for diseases caused by agronomically important plant pathogens. The assigned task was to create a user-friendly desktop application that accepts plant pathogen images as seen from a microscope and outputs the predicted plant pathogen. Our project consisted of using a pre-trained CNN, EffNet, and then fine tuning the weights based on a client provided dataset that we applied image processing techniques to. The resulting model will be employed on our user interface, where it will be used for teaching opportunities and for assisting our client.
studentNames: Adam Crawford, Alexandra Diaz, Alex Frias, Alberto Gutierrez, Christopher Fabian

abstract: Sweep creates large data workflow software for handling many data kinds, including text, machine, and image data. Sweep collaborates with customers to deliver unique workflow solutions using the Sweep API, a straightforward cloud-based data gathering platform for storing and analyzing time series data in a scalable manner. Big Language Models have the power to fundamentally alter the nature of knowledge work and the methods by which we gather, process, and comprehend data.  Sweep had the need for a straightforward solution that makes use of an online API or huge language model that is available as open source in order to tackle the problem of information loss in a company's internal PDF documents. This application can read PDF files, translate them into text that is readable by computers, and produce summaries that may be included into a unique process. The program will speed up data processing and improve access to important data kept on web intranets or local network filesystems.  The solution we came up with was using OpenAI's GPT 3.5 Large Language Model to take in compressed files we give it to have an understanding of the files contents. Once the files are received, we can then ask the system questions based on those files. The files we feed the LLM are compressed to ensure cost optimality.
studentNames: Jonathan Ballona Sanchez, Kobey Pabellan, Manuel Macias, Samuel Saldivar

projectTitle: PhoenixSync: Remote Firmware Management for IoT
abstract: Telit Cinterion serves as a pivotal connection catalyst, facilitating seamless connectivity with the transformative potential to enhance lives, redefine businesses, and shape a better future in today's hyperconnected world. Leveraging Internet of Things (IoT) devices, this approach accommodates solutions involving a diverse range of devices. Telit often engages in large-scale implementations with numerous field devices lacking efficient remote management capabilities, necessitating manual intervention for processes such as updates, management, and rollouts. In response, we have developed PheonixSync, a innovative solution utilizing Eclipse Hawkbit. This prototype streamlines the management of IoT devices, offering a robust and user-friendly interface for remote device management, firmware updates, improved security, and comprehensive device information.
studentNames: Cara Hill, Emmanuel Jerez, Hrishikesh Tiwari, Zhong Cai

abstract: Gradient Crop Yield Solutions, a Morning Star Company subsidiary, aims to transform crop health monitoring for Central Valley growers through an innovative and cost-effective web platform. Led by a dedicated team, the platform leverages AWS database, Flask on the backend, and React on the frontend. It enables seamless KML file uploads, triggering the backend to process Sentinel 2 imagery using NDVI/NDRE algorithms. The user-friendly interface integrates mapping libraries to showcase the most recent processed images overlaid on grower fields, delivering a cost-efficient solution that empowers growers with timely insights into crop health. This innovative approach offers a competitive alternative for informed decision-making.
studentNames: David Bates, Dmitri Lopez, Jonathan Leon-Rios, Kevin Torres-Anaya

abstract: The School of Engineering faces significant challenges with its current capstone project purchasing process, causing delays for both faculty and students. To address these pain points, our team developed a web application that offers a plethora of features that aims to enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of capstone purchasing process. This includes a straightforward and user-friendly Purchase Request Wizard, real-time status updates throughout the purchase request process, an efficient approval, inventory and budget management system, an Admin Dashboard for at-a-glance access to crucial information, and a responsive and scalable web application that utilizes AWS infrastructure.
studentNames: Julian Balbuena, Liam Stelly-Hawkes, Mark Soria Medina, Regina Chavez

abstract: United States Cold Storage is a leading warehousing company specializing in frozen and refrigerated food industries. The company emphasizes employee welfare and skills by conducting regular task observations. USCS faced challenges such as an unresponsive user interface (UI) and needing to adapt to evolving customer demands. Our team developed a web application that aims to overhaul the existing employee coaching and observation system, addressing issues and incorporating new functionalities to enhance efficiency. As a result, the company can incentivize employees to perform tasks safely and efficiently to earn bonuses.
studentNames: Esha Sarfraz, Harold Reyes, Ricardo Pimentel, William Le

abstract: Our team has engineered a sophisticated data purification tool for United States Cold Storage (USCS), a leading warehousing company specializing in frozen and refrigerated food industries. Our application tackles the pervasive issue of erroneous data within their legacy Warehouse Management System. The tool excels in transforming and aligning disparate data formats—including XML, CSV, and PDF—into a clean, functional state, ready for the system transition. It delivers a seamless, user-friendly experience, allowing easy data manipulation through a graphic interface, reducing laborious manual corrections. The solution boasts not only standard data structuring features but also advanced functionalities to weed out duplications, rectify formatting errors, and facilitate data amalgamation. This tool significantly optimizes data integrity and resource allocation for USCS.
studentNames: Fatima Amin, Federico Moreno, Mohamed Hasan, Tom Le

abstract: Western Digital is looking for an efficient and effective way to train its employees. Currently, the client lacks a training system that supports usability and information retention. The purpose of the project is to develop a fully functioning app that facilitates learning and content creation. The app the team created allows Western Digital the freedom to upload content and organize it into training plans that the employees will review and learn from. The neat and accessible webpages being accessed by the employees and managers were programmed with REACT. Meanwhile, the back end integrates Java Spring Boot for organized program management, and MySQL for a secure database.
studentNames: Eric Ponce, Izaac Ramirez, Kenzie Maxoutopoulis, Megan Phillips

projectTitle: Defect Detection on Silicon Wafer Using Neural Network
abstract: Western Digital Corporation, a prominent American manufacturer, specializes in computer drives and data storage solutions, including hard drives and Flash Memory Solid State Drives (SSD). The company aims to enhance the detection of defects in silicon wafers, a crucial component in manufacturing data storage devices. The objective is to employ a sophisticated open-source Regional Convolutional Neural Network (R-CNN) for the early identification of subtle defects that are not easily detected by human inspection. This approach involves generating synthetic greyscale images of clean wafers, superimposing random defects, and training a U-net model to classify these defects. This initiative is expected to significantly reduce the risk of electrical malfunctions in final products, leading to higher efficiency in the manufacturing process and maintaining Western Digital's reputation for quality. The team has developed a program to generate digital grayscale wafers with adjustable parameters and another program that creates random, commonly seen defects, also with adjustable parameters. Furthermore, the team has created a model that detects the defects on the wafer and provides information about them. Western Digital began initial testing on real data, achieving a capture ratio above 80%, with the Faster R-CNN model successfully detecting real defects. The team's contributions are pivotal in implementing proactive quality control measures that promise to set a new standard in the industry.
studentNames: Deep Singh Mehroke, Henry Rodas Hernandez, Joshua Alberti, Tai Pham

projectTitle: The Ronan Point Apartment Tower Case
abstract: The Ronan Point apartment tower experienced a progressive collapse in 1968 after a load bearing wall failed due to a small gas explosion. Our team was tasked with evaluating the structural failure of the building and recommending a new design that would have restricted failure to the floor at which it occurred. We have deemed that the fault of the failure was a lack of alternative load paths. Our design recommendations include using reinforced concrete columns and structural beams to brace across the sides of the building.
studentNames: Marco Buenrostro, Elana Glenny, Rahim Moulanazada, Ollie Sears

projectTitle: Lake Hodges Reservoir Sustainable Hypolimnion Oxygenation System
abstract: Levels of dissolved oxygen in bottom water decline to zero in Hodges Reservoir, San Diego, harming aquatic biota and complicating water treatment. To improve water quality, pure oxygen gas will be added to bottom waters using a submerged cone capable of delivering 4,000 kg/d of oxygen (O2) to bottom water. Around 2,000 kg/d of oxygen gas will be generated sustainably via electrolysis powered by a wind turbine system and a solar panel system. This will be supplemented by purchased liquid oxygen which will be delivered and stored on site. Any excess energy that is generated by the wind/solar system will be sold back to the regional energy grid. The oxygenated water will be cheaper to treat, and this cost savings will offset much of the operating cost of the oxygenation system. The oxygenation system will economically and sustainably establish well-oxygenated conditions in the bottom of Lake Hodges.
studentNames: Sebastian Guitron, Jessica Saad, Douglas Uchoas

projectTitle: Solar Pump Station Including Water Treatment
abstract: The proposed project design consists of a solar powered pumping well station and ion exchange water filtration system to deliver potable and palatable water to the 1500 residents of Le Grand, California. The various sub-components for the system include filtration, pump, water storage, and backup battery units. The economic feasibility of the project is to ensure the affordability for the residents, generate profit, and perform a risk assessment for the design. To conclude, testing methods were conducted on the components of our system to deliver a sustainable, safe, and affordable engineered solution to the residents of Le Grand.
studentNames: John Betbabanta, Evamercedes Gallardo, Cristian Recinos, Angie Rosales

projectTitle: Solar Pump Station Including Water Treatment
abstract: The Solar Pump Station Including Water Treatment project, focused on Le Grand, California, with approximately 1,500 residents, aims to address the complex challenges faced by rural American communities in their pursuit of clean and affordable water supplies. To accomplish this, a rural community water pumping station will be established, powered by a 1 MW Solar Photovoltaic System, and supported by a backup battery system to ensure a reliable water supply. The installed capacity of 1 MW will power the water pumping station as well as supplying excess energy to the Le Grand community. To meet these goals, the project team plans to implement monocrystalline solar panels and utilize two types of pumps, submersible and centrifugal. Additionally, water treatment will be conducted using reverse osmosis technology. The water pump station will transfer the water to the water tower, where it will be stored to ensure a minimum of two days' water supply for the Le Grand community. This project also seeks to tackle water quality concerns while estimating the system's capacity to serve households and calculating associated costs.
studentNames: Julie Contreras, Maria Contreras, Laurenz King, Jeremy Veramendi

projectTitle: Solar Pump Station Including Water Treatment
abstract: Access to clean and reliable water still remains a critical issue in many rural communities within the Central Valley. Our project addressed this challenge through the design of a solar-powered water pumping station aimed at providing a clean and affordable water solution for the 1,500 residents of Le Grand, CA. The proposed design consists of an 800 kW solar panel array, serving as the primary power source for the water pump and filtration system. This solution represents a significant step towards enhancing water accessibility and sustainability in underserved rural regions.
studentNames: De'Liz Amador, Serena Arzabal, Abel De La Torre-Fuentes, Steve Lee

projectTitle: Sweet Potato Planter Robotic End Effector
abstract: Arnold Farms, leading the California Sweet Potato Council, sought efficiency in their planting process. Addressing the challenge of manually separating and planting intertwined sweet potato seedlings, our team devised the Automatic Seedling Singulating and Conveying system. The process involves placing seedling bundles into a hopper, guiding them through a rotating notched cylinder that selects a single seedling. This seedling moves onto a conveyor belt, which directs it to a duct where it rests on a door mechanism. Ultrasonic sensors detect the approaching rotating clamp, signaling servo motors to open the door. Simultaneously, the pushing mechanism rotates the seedling to the clamp. Once complete, the door closes, and the pushing mechanism returns to its initial position. Rigorous testing confirmed the system's efficacy in separating and reaching a single seedling. The client stands to benefit significantly—reduced labor costs and minimized skips enhance cost savings and planting efficiency.
studentNames: Ariadne Castaneda, Efrain Lopez, Joe Priester, Stanley Carhee

abstract: Betts Company, a Fresno-based spring manufacturer serving heavy-duty transportation and the automotive aftermarket, required an innovative solution for accurately and reliably testing their truck springs. With no affordable options available on the market, we designed a Hydraulic press machine specifically tailored to Betts' needs. This robust and adaptable system can compress truck springs up to 150,000 cycles from free length to solid length. The machine, capable of withstanding forces up to 40k pounds, provides a cost-effective and versatile solution for testing different spring lengths. Beyond immediate applications, our design offers the potential for infinite life testing, enhancing Betts' ability to rate their springs accurately. This tool not only aids Betts in precise spring fatigue estimations but also allows consumers to better understand when to replace their springs, minimizing maintenance costs. Additionally, it enhances Betts' economic planning accuracy. Our tested and ready-to-use prototype pneumatic press machine signifies an advancement in spring testing technology, promising durability and reliability for Betts Company's manufacturing processes.
studentNames: Christian-Alexa Jimenez, Edgardo Benito Argueta, Giovanny Gonzalez, Paulino Flores

abstract: Delicato Family Wines, based in Manteca CA, is a top 5 wine company in the US. As a large wine company, Delicato often has to recycle already bottled wine to be made into newer batches of wine. As part of this process it is necessary to open many wine bottles quickly, but the current method is to do this by hand resulting in broken corkscrews and wasted time. In response to this problem our team was tasked with creating a better wine bottle uncorker that is durable, transportable, efficient, safe, and ergonomic. Our team designed a drill combined with a chuck that that drills through the cork of bottles in a wide range of sizes allowing wine to flow through the hole. Using this design we constructed a prototype that will be given to Delicato for them to do with as they see fit whether that be using it as we designed it or to improve upon it themselves.
studentNames: Balraj Basra, Mark Henen, Tadhg Mcinerney, Timothy White

abstract: Flory Industries specializes in the production and manufacturing of nut-harvesting equipment. Nuts are first taken from trees and placed into the shuttle trucks which then pour the nuts into the elevators which transfer them into the large hopper trailer ready for transport. However, the nuts being poured into the hopper trailer have been found to overflow due to Flory’s current 4890-elevator's inability to handle two simultaneous requests from the operator of the shuttle truck. The solution to this issue would be designing a trigger that will enable the operator the capability to input two commands to the 4890-elevator belt, in turn providing greater and efficient operator control.
studentNames: Adam Escobar, Andrew Valdivias, Daisy Barrueta, Isaias Zea Tavera

teamName: To Seed or Not to Seed
abstract: Based out of Fresno, CA, Fowler Packing specializes in cultivating grapes and mandarins. Within their Mandarin department, they face challenges with seeded mandarins making it into their seedless product. The current method utilized to prevent seeded mandarins is cutting and visually inspecting a small sample from a larger batch. This method leads to the destruction of products and proves inefficient in confirming the presence of seeds. This project aims to provide a non-destructive method for identifying seeded mandarins through Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) and Image Detection Software. Our proposal would replace the current method to maintain their current volume tested while retaining the once-destroyed mandarins. After testing X-Ray technology at 30 and 50 kvP we determined that the seeds could not be differentiated from the pulp and natural gaps within the fruit. We found that MRI is possible to differentiate the seeds from other parts of the fruit, with no fear of radiation. Using low-tesla MRIs and being able to maximize the echo and repetition time, the image speed and detection will be able to test more mandarins in one hour than what is typically done in one day.
studentNames: Anthony Pino, Ed Fabian, Leonel Esquivel Chi, Sandra Hernandez Gonzalez, Tsion Michael

abstract: Hilmar Cheese Company, situated in Hilmar, CA, stands as one of the nation's leading cheese producers, renowned for its large-scale manufacturing operations. The company encountered a challenge in achieving consistent cheese block dimensions during the production process, along with reducing human interaction with the cheese block. In order to resolve these issues, we have decided to optimize their pneumatic system (pneumatic pistons) by replacing a solenoid valve with an SMC-style flow control valve and implementing a Standard Operation Procedures (SOPs) to increase the frequency of maintenance of the pneumatic system. This solution allows for better control in regulating airflow in the pneumatic system to allow for more control on cheese block dimensions.
studentNames: Brandon Mendez Salinas, Diego De Jesus Torres, Janet Reyes-Zamora, Luis Gatica-Diaz, Jeffrey Ball

abstract: Live Oak Farms, since 1926, has been an essential agricultural producer located in the Central Valley that aims to deliver the community’s produce needs. From their implementation of various packing sheds and drip irrigation systems, the team at Live Oak Farms are dedicated to efficient agriculture. Therefore, they embarked on another journey, with the objective to further their efficiency of their water usage.   Currently, the tomatoes are placed into a 3000 gallon open water tank which washes off the silt, twigs and other debris found. Towards the end of day, the water is then disposed of, and the tank is cleaned for use the following day. This resulted a daily loss of, at minimum, 3000 gallons. Our team, Vanguard Solutions, was entrusted to develop a water management system with a recycle rate of 70%. Having examined different industrial systems, the team designed one that utilized a sedimentation tank coupled with a coagulant and sediment filter that would maintain the pH levels and ppm restrictions following state and FDA guidelines.
studentNames: Angel Fuentes, Dominic Bazzini, Felipe Zamarripa Mendoz, Gonzalo Gonzalez Avila

abstract: Our partnership with OFI’s Hughson Nut facility in Livingston tackled their 7% almond product loss. We identified three key areas causing spillage. First, a stainless steel barrier reduced spillage by 1% between conveyor belts. Second, fixing a displaced bumper at the Cooling Belt cut spillage by 3%. Lastly, inefficient handling of non-compliant almonds on the deviation belt resulted in intermittent but significant loss. Upgrades to the deviation belt are planned to further reduce losses. Our initial solutions dropped product loss to 3%, saving Hughson Nut approximately $200,000. Further modifications to the deviation belt are expected to decrease losses even more.
studentNames: Alyssa Olszewski, Clarissa Duran, Daniela Reyes Aguilar, Gary Vivar

projectTitle: Robots Making Robots Using Cobots to Weld and Screw-Drive
abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies based in the East Bay area in Pleasanton, CA, is an automation technology development company focused on robotics design and manufacturing. Omron is a leader in the industrial automation field developing new mechanics, software, and electronics for a wide variety of robots. Currently, they are working on making a logistics robot and during production, they are spending too much time fastening 40+ screws onto a part of the robot. The company would like to automate this tedious process that takes 45 - 60 minutes and free up the workers to have them allocate their time elsewhere. Our goal was to match or reduce the time by creating an end effector that could complete the task with minimum worker intervention. With this in mind, we designed an end effector that is capable of picking up screws, switching between four bits, and torque sensing. The end effector is capable of doing the fastening procedure without any assistance from the workers. The length of the hood exceeds the range of the cobot arm so we designed a mount that would allow the workers to rotate the part. This would allow the arm to reach all parts of the hood and for full assembly.
studentNames: Brandon Fernandez Rosales, Christian Garcia, Joshua Judilla, Jake Pugh

projectTitle: Removal of MOO (Material Other than Olives)
abstract: Situated in the heart of Madera, CA, ENZO Olive Oil, a producer of premium olive oil, confronts operational challenges tied to manual sorting inefficiencies, specifically materials other than olives (MOO) and the problematic presence of olive knots. Olive knots result from the formation of tumor-like growths on olive branches, they share the same shape as olives which poses a problem to the labor workers who sort them. Our project responds to these challenges, proposing an automated olive sorting system for heightened efficiency. The Olivematic Engineers devised a stainless-steel rotating drum with strategically placed sifting holes all around it. The olives are fed into the rotating drum at a set RPM and sorted accordingly. To ensure the practicality of our design we successfully developed and tested a small-scale prototype, showcasing the viability of our approach. Emphasizing materials meeting food safety standards, easy maintenance, and minimal supervision requirements. Operationally, the full-scale rotating drum will be positioned post-hopper in the current physical setup at Enzo Olive Oil with the aid of conveyor belts, ensuring an uninterrupted flow of olives while effectively managing MOO for responsible disposal.
studentNames: Abigail Caporal, Andrew Starkman, Juan Hernandez Maciel, Tiffany Murga Duarte

abstract: Sensient Natural Ingredients (SNI) is committed to and specializes in the production of spices and flavor enhancers. Among the diverse range of products manufactured by SNI are garlic bulbs, cultivated in the high desert regions of Nevada, Washington, and Oregon. In the process of transporting these garlic bulbs to the Livingston, CA facility, empty wooden bins were used for transportation. However, the reliance on wooden bins presented challenges related to repair costs and transportation expenses. Due to the disadvantages of using wooden bins, the transition to bulk bags was made, but this shift raised concerns regarding storage limitations and the inability to reuse the bulk bags. In response to these challenges, our team assisted in the development of a forklift attachment prototype and helped with design improvements. The forklift attachment carries and dumps the garlic bulbs into the wooden bins for storage. This innovative attachment provides a faster and more efficient transfer of garlic bulbs from bulk bags into wooden bins.
studentNames: Diego Barraza, Kevin Ortega Flores, Michael Trimbel, Steven Choi

abstract: The Morning Star Company harvests and produces the largest supply of tomato in the world. Located in Los Banos, California, Morning Star uses heavy agricultural tomato harvesters to pick up tomatoes from fields, ranging from Bakersfield to Sacramento. In the process of this harvesting operation, the harvesters are saturated with a large amount of dirt, roots, branches, and leaves, also known as material other than tomato (MOT). This debris is responsible for the premature wear of expensive components on the machines such as belts, rollers, and bearings, which is expensive and time consuming to repair. To reduce the amount of MOT buildup, our team designed a self cleaning harvester system, which consists of nozzles and a logic board, placed at hard to reach and high MOT buildup locations. This system will be used by the harvester operators, and with a flip of a switch, will spray high pressure water connected to a pressure washer, in order to clean and remove MOT to the designated areas. With this effective and controlled spraying of water, the system will reduce premature wear on components, as well as use a consistent amount of water, saving money and downtime on the harvesters.
studentNames: Alyssa Jones, Cesar Villalobos, Erick Patino Santiago, Seraj Sekander

projectTitle: Shuttle Truck Connection Automation with Robotic Arm
abstract: The Morning Star Packing Company produces, processes, and packages tomato products nationwide. When harvesting the tomatoes, the drivers must get out of the shuttle and manually connect the air brakes to the trailers. Our team's task was to increase the efficiency of the tomato harvest by automating the process of engaging the air brakes from the shuttle to the trailer. This was done by designing a new system that simplified the motion needed. The project uses a lift attached to the shuttle, which raises a plate with the airbrake connections funneled through. The plate is lifted to an end effector that is attached to the trailer. The two plates meet and they use magnets for alignment and multiple locking systems to ensure safety. The airbrake lines shall connect to the lines on the end effector and allow airflow. This disengages the brakes in the trailer, allowing the vehicle to move.
studentNames: alex hartzler, Angela Lizarzaburu, Carlos Diaz Diaz, jovannie ruiz

abstract: The UC Merced Experimental Farm researches soil to analyze its physical and chemical properties, such as texture and organic matter content. This research contributes to sustainable land use, environmental conservation, and food security. To sterilize the soil, they use a sterilization chamber that heats the soil to kill all the bacteria, fungi, and other pathogens present in the soil. The problem is that the chamber is difficult to empty after the soil has been sterilized, and requires at multiple people to do move and empty the sterilized soil. We designed a soil sterilizer flipper to increase the efficiency of the emptying process, improve the transportation of the chamber, and reduce the number of people required for the sterilization process. Our design has significantly reduced the labor cost and demand of sterilizing soil for the UC Merced Experimental Farm.
studentNames: Christopher Frison, Carlos Lopez-Pagan, Christopher Wilson, Mario Casillas

projectTitle: Off-Orchard On-Ground Aeration System for Almond Drying
abstract: Almond harvesting, due to new and existing policy, is going through a change in its methods. Traditionally, almonds are shaken from their trees and windrow dried. Then, they are collected and taken for further processing. In response to new demands, off-ground harvesting is being adopted by the market. This, however, removes the natural drying process in traditional harvesting since off-ground harvesting operates by direct shake-and-catch. Thus, our team was tasked with developing a system that could dry these wet stockpiled almonds. We ran simulations and did real-world testing of our system and were able to outperform windrow drying on a small scale. There is enough evidence to believe that, if upscaled properly, this system could be a definitive solution to this problem.
studentNames: Alejandro Lailson, Brian John Virtucio, Sergio Mendiola, Yuriana Medina-Fernandez

projectTitle: Solar Industrial Process Heating - Automated Cleaning
abstract: Our initiative, in collaboration with Winston Cone Optics (WCO), a renowned solar tech business in Merced, California led by Bennett Widyolar and Jordyn Brinkley, focuses on cleaning solar heating devices in the context of sustainable solar technology. The "1-point Mobile Frame Sprayer" is the new cleaning technology we developed to preserve panel quality and efficiency. With the help of this technology, solar collector efficiency was enhanced, and stains from hard water and scratches were negated. WCO wants to move industry away from fossil fuels and lower greenhouse gas emissions using solar thermal technologies. Our project produced comprehensive CAD models, simulations, and drawings to increase cleaning efficiency by removing dust and debris from the solar collectors. Our proposal essentially aimed to represent sustainability and modern engineering, supporting WCO's search for greener, more effective solar energy solutions through a "1-point Mobile Frame Sprayer."
studentNames: Estrella Martinez Mendez, Francisco Salcido, Gregory Wong, James Johnson Jr

abstract: Agrecom is a biosecurity company providing a spectrum of solutions in the areas of water treatment and sanitation, in agricultural, residential, and commercial sectors. Their inspection team determines the severity of rodent infestation at various facilities. Our team has designed a pipeline to automate the process of detecting rodents once cameras are placed in the client’s facility. The images retrieved from game cameras will automatically be uploaded onto a Raspberry Pi computer, via an FTP server linked to the cameras, where our machine learning image classifier will analyze the images and predict rodent presence in real time. Our database is then updated with the detection and other information that allow for various metrics of data to be viewed in our web application, including plots, and a heatmap. The implementation of our software eliminates all human interaction in the collection and analysis of data and allows Agrecom to remarkably expedite its analysis process.
studentNames: Dathan Hansell, Shalyn Nguyen, Brandon Pardi, Elguin Perez, Alejandro Vazquez

projectTitle: Flood and hydrology alert and monitoring system
abstract: Agrecom targets customers in the agricultural, residential, and commercial sectors and offers a range of solutions in the areas of water treatment, sanitation, and pest control. Recently, Merced has experienced floodings in areas throughout the county due to extreme weather causing nearby creeks and rivers to overflow. Without a website that consolidates this information, Merced residents may be left uninformed and unprepared. Our solution is to bring this information together in an easy to use website. Our website utilizes the Twilio API to send an SMS when a certain threshold for creek or dam levels are breached. Users can subscribe to these alerts to receive timely information. In addition, our website allows users to manually explore publicly recorded data for creeks, rivers, dams, and weather during times of crisis. With an easy-to-use interface and an efficient alert system, this project helps Merced County residents stay informed and safe during times of crisis.
studentNames: Paul Kim, Hao Mai, Rodrigo Martinez, Brandon Ortega, Andrew Plata

abstract: Betts Company is a manufacturing company located in the Central Valley. In order to ensure a safe and productive work environment the company needs to ensure all employees are properly trained for the tasks they are assigned. Currently, they store all of this data on Microsoft Excel sheets. In order to track data more efficiently and effectively, they would like a Training Matrix Application built that allows them to create, edit, and view employee training.  Team “PowerWorks” has created a user-friendly application built within Microsoft PowerApps that will enable HR to effectively manage employee training.
studentNames: William Breytberg, Qihao Cai, Ryan Chazen, Chukwuemeka Elendu, Guillermo Zarate

projectTitle: CTTA Member Database Management and Automation
abstract: The California Tow Truck Association (CTTA) is an organization that represents towing operators across the state. The association aims to empower tow truck companies and drivers to become more professional and progressive in the towing and emergency road-service industry. However, CTTA's current database management system is difficult to navigate and lacks the ability to transfer data between different systems, resulting in data loss. To address these issues, a team was tasked with building a new database management system that would communicate with other systems, store records without loss, and have an easy-to-use interface. The team collected feedback on the current system from CTTA, identified areas for improvement, and researched solutions to design the new system.
studentNames: Serenity Bassett, Xuanren Chen, Kulkaran Khaira, Alonso Ortiz, Inderpal Pannu, Mithil Patel

projectTitle: Inventory and Condition of Vegetable Seedlings in Trays - Phase 2
abstract: California Transplants is a grower supply company for seedlings located in Newman, California. They specialize in tomatoes, pepper, broccoli, cauliflower, melon, cabbage, and industrial hemp seedlings. Their facility consists of 274 greenhouses that use over 400 million seeds. Currently, CA Transplants manually counts germination rates or usable plants per order as a percentage. That data is then entered into their system manually which gives an estimate of the inventory per order. To save time, CA Transplants is looking for a system that automatically scans/classifies trays of plants in the greenhouses to help create an accurate inventory report. We focused on tomato plants, and created a tool to count the number of viable seedlings in a tray using image processing. A seedling is determined as 'viable' if it grows its cotyledon, or the first two or three leaves when the seed germinates. The solution we created constructs a virtual grid over the tray and processes each cell using pixel evaluation techniques such as thresholding to determine if a cell has a plant, no plant, or multiple plants. The resulting data can be uploaded to a database and exported to a CSV file.
studentNames: Guru Bachchu, Herminda Cisneros Duran, John Galindo, Mashaallah Moradi, Celeste Rodriguez Caballero

projectTitle: Bottling Change Part Wear Measuring Tool - Phase 2
abstract: E&J Gallo winery, headquartered in Modesto, California, is the largest family-owned winery and wine distributor in the US. The large-scale manufacturing of products on the factory floor requires several different parts to transport, align, and prep bottles for different stages of the manufacturing process. Over time, these parts can be worn down to a degree where the placement/alignment of bottles on the production line is no longer precise enough for other heavy machinery to operate on them. This leads to bottles being shattered, machinery being broken, and a halt in the production process while the error is corrected. Our team designed and built an application that utilizes image processing and computer vision to calculate the wear/usability of a given transfer part to determine if/when replacement is necessary as well as keeps a catalog for these transfer parts and their wear statuses. With this, Gallo line engineers will be able to determine if a transfer star is suitable for use before installing it, reducing the chance of equipment failure.
studentNames: Gabriel Benavidez, Isaac Luengas, Nabir Migadde, Ivan Tinoco

abstract: Farm-ng provided the team with an Amiga robot, tasking them to move the four-wheeled machine autonomously, free of constant direction. In the context of a farming application, this project focuses on designing an autonomous robot that can navigate through predefined GPS way points within the agricultural landscape and return to its starting point. By employing two GPS modules and a list of target coordinates, the robot will move through the farm, ensuring close proximity to each way point. This technology has the potential to enhance precision agriculture practices, such as crop monitoring, soil sampling, and targeted pesticide application, ultimately increasing overall farm productivity and sustainability.
studentNames: Johann Atchoukeu, Octavio Gonzalez, Adrian Posadas, Krishna Sharma, Tristin Thant

projectTitle: Farm Robotics Challenge -- Artificial Intelligence (Vision and sensing, dataset collection)
abstract: Farm-ng has created the Amiga robot to help farmers with their daily tasks. Farming is a challenging and demanding endeavor that involves manual labor, patience, time, and significant financial investment. The tasks involved in farming require considerable effort and expertise, which can make it a difficult and often arduous task. Our team has worked on an Amiga app that detects and follows the farmer to help with some of these daily tasks such as carrying tools, holding harvest boxes, and finger weeding. Consequently, this helps to amplify the efficiency of workers in a moment where it is extremely difficult recruiting farm workers. With the app's capabilities, farmers can optimize their workflow and allocate resources more efficiently, resulting in cost savings and increased productivity.
studentNames: Christopher Andrade, Rylan Chao, Kiet Dang, Danielle rodriguez, Dustin Vy

abstract: University students need to navigate multiple websites to locate relevant information, which may be outdated or irrelevant. Globidea is an initiative aimed at enhancing the overall student experience by providing a centralized website with current and up-to-date information. The development team has created a website consisting of five phases that students go through: Planning to Attend, Getting Started, Making Progress, Finishing Up, and Moving Forward. The website features a way for an admin to manage the information and ensure that it is placed in the most relevant phase to simplify students' search for pertinent information that aligns with their current stage of the student journey. Each piece of information added to the different phases and subheadings contains a hyper-text linking back to the original source and directing the user to the precise location where the information was discovered.
studentNames: Cassandra Chen, Andrew Duran, Fiza Haroon, Andrew Olmos, Wen Zhao

projectTitle: Using sensors to collect road user data
abstract: The Merced Bicycle Coalition aims to make Merced a more a bike friendly city by convincing the local government to implement safe bike infrastructure. In this process, they needed to track the frequency of different micro-mobile users at varying locations. We were tasked with classifying different micro-mobile users include cyclists, skateboarders, scooter riders, and many other small vehicle users. Using image data to train a machine learning model using Amazon Rekognition’s object recognition services, our system takes in video input and outputs how many times a given micro-mobility vehicle has appeared within the video. The revealed frequency of micro-mobility vehicles at the recorded locations will be used to further the development of new bike lanes that can be used by the micro-mobile users.
studentNames: Lucas Christenson, Peter Kinyanjui, Darren Roberson, Suyeon Shin, Athena Siaotong

abstract: Neil Jones Foods is located in Hollister, and its main business is canning tomato products. These products are distributed to various establishments across the US, including restaurants and schools. Unfortunately, the machine responsible for sealing the lids on the final product sometimes experiences malfunctions. This can lead to cans with lids that are not properly sealed, causing disruptions in the production process, reduced output, and lost revenue. To address this issue our team has designed a system that uses multiple cameras to view different angles of the can. Deep learning models are used to classify the can as damaged or acceptable. The prototype testing has shown a consistent and successful identification of problematic cans through the detection system. Once identified, the system can signal, via a discrete output, for the removal of these cans from the processing line.
studentNames: Cyrus Adriel Arellano, Luan Huynh, Luis Mora, Jovan Singh, Elson Zeng

abstract: Nutstar Software LLC. is a company that develops custom software for the tree nut industry. They deal with grower purchasing, inventory management, and sales tracking for different nut businesses. Nutstar has noticed that many of their clients have trouble tracking their inventory when it gets lost and are constantly losing value in their product due to poor inventory tracking; Our team designed a software solution that uses Angle of Arrival (AOA) Locators and tags to give precise coordinates within a predetermined 3D matrix in order to track inventory movement and prevent any future shipment losses. Our team developed this solution in a Windows Application where a forklift driver will only have to click a button to fetch the coordinates of the bins being moved and be displayed in the Windows Application in the form of Row, Column, and Loft.
studentNames: Misael Casarez, Tim Chan, Ricardo Rodriguez, Titus Sun, Joshua Terriquez

projectTitle: Real-time optimization of the deceleration profile of a mobile robot to achieve a highly accurate final position
abstract: Omron is a robotics company developing industrial, mobile, and collaborative robots for a variety of applications. A common problem in automated warehouses is that the floor’s condition varies widely depending on location across a warehouse. The vastly different states of the floor can cause slippage and overshooting or excessive traction that current robots cannot predict. Team 315 designed and applied two reinforcement learning algorithms, Deep-Q-Network (DQN) and Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) to adapt a simulated robot in an environment emulating the variety in warehouse floors. The resulting model will be deployed on autonomous robots and can be tuned by technicians if environmental constraints or robot specifications change over time.
studentNames: Lance Carrido, Gavin Cochrane, Jason Gates, Andrew Lin, Stanley Yang

abstract: SupHerb Farms is a company whose primary business is contracting culinary herb production, processing of those herbs, and sales of finished products around the world. SupHerb Farms would like to be able to utilize cameras to see if they can accurately determine harvestable weight of cilantro from each production field, prior to harvest. The team, Cilantro Connoisseurs, devised a neural network and corresponding system that would take images as input and output an accurate estimation of harvestable weight of cilantro.
studentNames: Eduardo Barreto-Mendoza, Natalie Diaz, Harpreet Dosanjh, Charles Mazhar, Robert Smith

abstract: In collaboration with Telit Cinterion, a leading provider of secure IoT solutions, products and services, this project aimed to develop an asset tracking admin dashboard to enhance visibility and management of valuable construction jobsite assets. The team designed a comprehensive dashboard with macro and micro views, detailed tool data, precise location tracking, and advanced geofencing capabilities to prevent tools from leaving designated areas. This solution enables Telit Cinterion’s Construction Industry customers to monitor and manage their assets, optimizing resources and increasing operational efficiency. The project showcases the team's expertise in creating customized IoT and dashboard solutions for intricate client needs.
studentNames: Luis Carrillo, Christian Doud, Sandeep Kaur, Emerald Nash, Jennifer Nhu, Christian Urbina

projectTitle: Assessing and Grading Wood Bin Parts
abstract: The Morning Star Company is a tomato processing company that supplies about 10% of the world’s tomato production. Morning Star packages their products in reusable 300-gallon wooden bins, which can be collapsed into 6 separate components for return shipping.  The bin sort colleagues visually and physically inspect millions of bin parts and grade them based on whether they are usable or unusable.  The issue with this process is that sorters may have different opinions on whether a part’s damage causes the part to be unusable, which leads to Morning Star spending more money to repair parts or manufacture new parts. The team’s solution was to create a program to consistently discern usable parts based on a diverse range of factors, identify unusable or repairable parts, and create a program with the potential to learn and perform better over time. The program uses a machine learning model to look for part damages and grade the parts in real time. The model’s decision along with the sorter’s confirmation are stored in a database where the entries can be reviewed for any inconsistencies.
studentNames: Tony Doan, Renato Millan, Lucas Ortiz-Gonzalez, Rui Pan

abstract: The team designed a TA (Teaching Assistant) Allocation web application for the client, the UC Merced Computer Science and Engineering Department, who are responsible for assigning TA's to positions. The web app, developed using React with Next.js for the front-end, Firebase for authentication, and SQL/Node.js for the back-end, includes a drag-and-drop interface for assigning TA's based on their preferences and a priority formula. It also allows for adding new semesters, inputting courses, and setting priority variables and preferences. Created for the faculty, the web app is expected to streamline the TA allocation process, reduce manual efforts, improve accuracy in handling preferences, and enhance user experience for the faculty involved.
studentNames: Soraya Boza, Gabriel Pulido, Ramon Sanchez, Daniel Tucker, Alyssa Yee

abstract: The UC Merced Experimental Smart Farm is a 45-acre facility that is dedicated to implementing cutting-edge agricultural techniques for future farming. Water management is a critical aspect of farming and currently there is no simple way to track valves. CropFlow developed a mobile application that tracks the status and activity of water wells and valves in real-time, automates computation of valve history, and enables the addition of new valves for scalability. An interactive map feature provides a quick overview of the farm's status, streamlining the monitoring process. The team's app represents a significant step towards efficient and sustainable farming practices, which shall benefit farmers and the wider community.
studentNames: Andrew Cardenas, Andrew Mouillesseaux, Valerie Nguyen, Derian Ramirez, Sawyer Shaddix, Johnson Tran

abstract: Innovate to Grow (I2G) is a unique “experiential learning” program that engages external partner organizations with teams of students who design systems to solve real-world problems as well as develop modules that address problem and user needs when searching and organizing information. One of these problems include the limited availability of services or applications that allow attendees and organizers to determine optimal meeting times. Team 310 was assigned to address this problem by creating a visual analysis tool that offers more flexibility when creating schedules and displaying real-time variations of different attendee schedules. The team addressed the problem by creating a web application that allows attendees to create a schedule with an availability range for each block and allowing the organizer to see variations of the average schedule based on relevance values of each attendee and their respective schedules.
studentNames: Uriel Barba, Stewart Choi, Vikram Narendar, Akash Singh, Martin Urueta

projectTitle: Evaluation of Coordination: Telemedicine Using Virtual Reality
abstract: The Valley Children’s Hospital (VCH), a healthcare institution in the central valley of California, provides excellent healthcare services to children. In order to enhance the VCH’s telemedicine services, this project explores the use of Virtual Reality (VR) to track eye movements and pupillary size during neurological examinations. The team developed a VR environment in which a standard neurological exam (visual exam) is performed to gather data on a patient’s neurologic-visual performance. This exam would track the patient’s pupil dilation and eye movements, extracting that data/performance to create a visual report in the form of graphs and models on a front-end website. The purpose of this VR project is to create a standardized exam for visual examinations to increase the accuracy of the neurological exam (visual exam). The front-end will allow the doctor to view exam information, along with generating a report on the exam results.
studentNames: Rodolfo Gutierrez, Tyler Ly, Axel Martinez Siliezar, Austin Matthews, Alfredo Torres, Sam Walley

abstract: Western Digital Corportation (WDC) is an American based-global company that has been at the forefront of computer storage innovation for half a century. Employing a workforce of thousands of skilled engineers, the individuals in research and experimental development inevitably generate massive amounts of technical specs, requirements, and design documents that are spread out across numerous sources. As a result, it is challenging to keep track of where these files reside at the time of access. The EZFile team has developed an innovative web-based file location organizing application that offers an all-inclusive solution to efficiently access, store and export data through the use of intuitive hyperlinks. The team's design and implementation will not only help increase productivity but also eliminate the headache of searching for missing files.
studentNames: Khalid Ali, Jose Gonzalez, Kevin Ruiz-Mejia, Alexys Sanchez Partida, Joey Truong

projectTitle: GUI interface for the image recognition and classification open source software
abstract: Western Digital Corporation, a technology company specializing in storage solutions for devices and data centers, requires a program that can use deep learning-based image processing and analysis to make specific observations about internal data. Currently, their data scientists manually perform object detection and image labeling through a command line, causing a time-consuming bottleneck in production and creating a technological barrier for domain experts. Therefore, the team was tasked to develop a platform that enables less experienced domain experts to perform object detection and image annotation without needing command-line operations or dependency installations. The team accomplished this by connecting the open-source graphical user interfaces of JustDeepIt and VGG Image Annotater to be deployed by a Dockerhub image.
studentNames: Samuel Campos, Angelo Fatali, Hunter Mcclellan, Amrita Mohandas, Jacky Yu

projectTitle: Solar Pump and Water Treatment Station
studentNames: Daniel Aleman, Ranjana Lal, Gerardo Rodriguez, Cortez Valle

projectTitle: Improving Lake Skinner's Water Quality with Oxygenation
studentNames: Andrew Alamillo, Antonio Ceballos, Emily Gomez, Dulce Monje Alcazar
Noah Blank, Andy Hernandez, Karendeep Kahlon, Clara Medina

projectTitle: Local Aquifer Storage and Recover (ASR) Design
studentNames: Angela Flores, Jocelin Rivera, Paul Sugimoto, Cindy Torres Camacho

abstract: Every year ASHRAE holds a design competition for HVAC systems in three different categories. The categories being Design Calculations, System Selection, and Setty Family Foundation Net Zero Energy Design. The team chose to enter into the Design Calculations category and for this year the task was to design and layout an entire HVAC system for a building in Cairo, Egypt. Given CAD drawings, ASHRAE standards, and an OPR, we provided heating and cooling load calculations, mechanical floor plan (duct size and layout, piping size and layout, etc.), coil sizing and selection, and Air Handling Unit selection.
studentNames: Xaiver Vega, Lukas Fong, Ethan Murcia, Noah Johnson, Ray Medina

abstract: San Francisco Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) is a heavy-rail public transit system that connects the San Francisco Peninsula with communities in the East and South Bay areas. There is a risk of intrusions into the track space from persons and motor vehicles and currently the only method to detect an intrusion is done through observation by the train operator.  BART trains operate at high speeds and have a large stopping distance, therefore any emergency stop issued by an automated system would need to be done quickly to mitigate the chance of collision with an intrusion. We developed trAIn vision to improve BARTs ability to manage track obstructions by using the Tensor Flow API to develop an AI object detection model to identify specific intrusions. The prototype then reports to BART train controllers who will issue an appropriate response, and activate an emergency stop of the train if needed. The trAIn vision prototype features a variable zoom camera capable of making accurate inferences on objects at distances of over 1000 feet.
studentNames: Joseph Whalen, Mohammad Syed, Ethan Osborne - Berman, Antonio Garcia, Angel Munoz

abstract: California Transplants is an agricultural nursery company that specializes in growing transplants for commercial use. The company currently grows several vegetables that require repeated mowing to increase the strength and girth of the transplant. The current mowing system is time consuming, requires too many employees and costs too much to run. We designed a system that is portable and will minimize operators and maximize savings as a byproduct. The machine is composed of a robot for travel and two large folding arms for suspended mowers. After testing with the robot and fabricated parts, the machine will then be implemented into long term usage for mowing operations.
studentNames: Alex Huynh, Galo Mora, Ricardo Diaz, Marco Garcia, Esmeralda Ochoa

abstract: Del Monte Foods, Inc. is one of the country's largest producers, distributors, and marketers of premium quality, branded food products for the U.S. retail market. Del Monte Foods is losing monetary value due to its fruit spillage loss on conveyor lines and during the filling of the cans. For this project the team was asked to create a safe and cost effective solution to reduce the fruit spillage in the canning process of the fruit cocktail. To decrease the spillage that occurs on the conveyor line the team developed a can cover system that provides enough coverage to reduce spillage without increasing the probability of a jam occurring. The team also decreased fruit spillage during the filling of the cans by creating a chute to precisely direct the fruit into the cans thus reducing the fruit spilled over the sides when filling.
studentNames: Brandon Baltazar, Adrian Rivera, Xerxes Zangeneh, Carlos Pedraza, Jessica Romero

abstract: Del Monte Foods, Inc. is one of the country's largest producers, distributors, and marketers of premium quality, branded food products for the U.S. retail market. At the Del Monte factory, two employees at each station are given the task of pouring containers of fruits onto a conveyor, a job which is labor intensive and incurs extra costs. Hence, we've applied engineering knowledge to automate the process, allowing the company to cut down on labor costs and allocate workers to more complex tasks. We created a 3D gantry system, with full 3 axis movement and a claw to pick up and flip containers to deliver fruit onto the conveyor belt. The system uses belt drives on the X and Y axis to allow for greater speeds and a ball screw on the Z axis for greater strength at lower speed when lifting cans.
studentNames: Basheer Hussin, Saul Macias, Arturo Baza, Carlos Padmore

abstract: Del Monte Foods is one of the largest international producers of canned fruits and vegetables. They wanted to increase the production efficiency of tomato paste in their Hanford facility and automate the process. To improve and automate their system as requested, we designed an automated control system that integrates with their current network which also employs a custom immersion sprayer to clean the tomato paste bins during the cycle. This solution would eliminate the need for an operator at this station and reduce downtime in production.
studentNames: Alexis Castillo, Ismael Diaz Garcia, Evan Roxton Chibbaro, David Vasquez Gramajo, Casey Leong

abstract: Del Monte Foods, Inc. is one of America’s largest producers, distributors and marketers of premium quality branded foods. Team Lane Divider had the opportunity to work with Del monte to decrease the bottleneck of empty cans on the conveyor belt. The loading area of line 2 is where empty cans fall over and create a jamming at a transition point on line 2. This has decreased Del Monte’s overall volume of empty cans coming out of line 2. Team Lane Divider has determined that the problem area stems from the loading area of the conveyor belt and the proposed solution is to have an overhead of 5 lane dividers that will guide the empty cans upright to the transition point and decrease the overall tipping of cans.
studentNames: Estevan Marquez, Ryan Yeung, Joseph Jordan, Andrea Moran, Alejandro Valencia Salazar

abstract: E. & J. Gallo Winery processes tons of grapes to make wine every year. To transform this much juice into wine takes multiple movements that require large, heavy, sanitary hoses. The brass couplings at the end of the hose are hooked up to stainless-steel pipe drops that connect tanks to processing equipment, or to other tanks for wine blending. The employees that attach or detach these hoses to the pipe drops are named "Rack & Blenders", and use a stainless-steel hose key. Hose connections are often tightly fastened and can take a large force to get on or off. Using the hose key with excessive force could cause a potential risk to the Rack & Blenders. If the hose key slips off the brass ears, it may cause injuries to the employees and damage surrounding equipment. Our team has designed a greatly improved hose key that provides the Rack and Blenders with a more safe, robust, ergonomic, and simple tool to perform their labor-intensive task with reduced risk of injury. The tool is undergoing operational testing.
studentNames: Reid Mcleod, Antonio Villalobos, Kamila Ramirez, Govargiz Sayyad Shahbaz, Fernando Cruz

abstract: Our team has designed a Wear Measurement Tool to address safety concerns at E&J Gallo Winery. The tool is used to determine when brass coupling ears have worn down excessively and require replacement to prevent potential injuries. The tool consists of a body, a cap, and stainless-steel pins that replicate the shape of any object pressed against them. The acrylic cap features markers indicating the remaining lifespan of the brass ear. The design considers safety, ergonomics, and sanitary requirements while minimizing capital investment. The materials used, stainless steel for the pins and bracket and FDA-approved acrylic for the cap, ensure safe use with food products. Our prototype includes cost estimates, material details, and training to ensure proper use.
studentNames: Perla Mendoza Rueda, Jacob Murillo-Gil, Thinh Nguyen, Neftali Galis Castillo

projectTitle: Patient Environmental Logger and Reporting - Phase 3
abstract: The Fresno Institute of Neuroscience is dedicated to enhancing the quality of life of neurologic patients through patient care, neurophysiology, teaching, and research. The Institute's research focuses on using personalized, innovative, and technology-based approaches to improve neurologic patients' quality of life. However, due to the variability of symptoms such as headaches and the difficulty in establishing universal treatment methods, a non-invasive data-collecting device is being developed to monitor patients' movements and evaluate pain levels. This personalized and adaptable technology, which utilizes a 3D biomechanical model, aims to enable customized therapeutic treatments for chronic neurologic patients. The project involves three elements: building the sensor device, creating an application for data analysis, and examining the output data. The team used a SensorTile, SensorTile cradle, LiPO battery, programming cable, and other components to create the prototype and test five body areas: ankles, knees, hips, back, and shoulders. The results provided a visual representation of patients' daily movements, including average displacement, velocity, and jerk movement. This information can be used by care providers to diagnose and determine patient treatment, resulting in reduced clinical visits, decreased treatment costs, and saved time for both providers and patients.
