This project builds upon the work of past Capstone groups, which have created the application for data analysis.
studentNames: Estefania Gamino, Sofia Herrera-Padilla, Gehad Elhanafy, Evelyn Archuleta, Phoebe Adamyan

projectTitle: Quantification of Organic Material in Air
abstract: It is well established that with the recent COVID-19 pandemic the rates of infection and contamination are increasing rapidly over the past few years. In part due to an increasing number of cases, contamination being the highest in hospital and clinical settings. The Fresno Institute of Neuroscience is an inpatient care clinic in the Central Valley that also focuses on research. In this study, we collaborated with Dr. Mark Stecker to produce a prototype that has the potential of quantifying organic material in air. We developed a CANARY detection system to detect viruses in the air which work in sequence with filters, a vacuum and sensors to deliver an audible and quick response about the presence of organic material. We also employed Beer-Lambert Law to determine the concentration of viruses and validate that the concentration linearly scales with increasing coughs in our sample.
studentNames: Aaron Amanios, Joana Gutierrez Coss y Leon, George Aguilar, Gilberto De La Torre, Emily Junez

projectTitle: Design New Perfect Service Lane Conveyor
abstract: The client, Frito Lay, a company that manufactures, sells, and markets a variety of snack foods, has a bottlenecking issue in their Geographic Enterprise Systems (GES) department. With a multitude of different lanes converging onto a single perfect service lane (PSL) belt, the boxes are getting stuck, inevitably causing this delaying issue and decreasing the efficiency of the box shipments out to customers. We have designed a layout that introduces another bi-directional transfer and belt system that will diverge the boxes from the 3rd floor of the mezzanine to the 1st floor, which connects to an additional audit station below and will allow for decongestion on the PSL belt. The system will be used daily at the Modesto facility and has been tested and reviewed by Wynright, an integrated material & automation company.
studentNames: Kim Nguyen, Noel Gomez, Jose Zamora Bello, Angel Jauregui, Rodolfo Sanchez

abstract: The Great Valley Seed Company located in Los Banos, CA specializes in farming over 40 species of native seeds of the San Joaquin Valley. Amongst the many processes they specialize in, this project focuses on the cleaning stage of the native seeds. The seeds arrive at the cleaning stage with about 5% seeds and 95% byproducts. The mixture is cleaned with various clippers, threshers, aspirators, and indent cylinders. The last cleaning stage is done by a small, wooden, homemade machine. However, this machine was difficult to adjust and the seeds have to undergo different cycles to achieve the desired purity. With these things in mind, we designed a two-screen aspirator. Our aspirator would have two cycles which would allow for the seeds to be cleaned twice in one cycle and it would have easier control of the air pressure within the machine to allow for easier separation of seeds. This is further backed by our simulations from ANSYS, SolidWorks, and calculations of seed behavior, pressure, and velocity.
studentNames: Michael Yang, Carlos Cruz, Heily Chavez Davila, Rene Mendoza Salgado, Alison Lopez Contreras

abstract: Designing a dependable and efficient seed harvester is imperative for modern seed harvesting. Hand harvesting of the Narrowleaf Milkweed seeds and floss by Great Valley Seed Company (GVC) results in the loss of over 50% of the collected product. This is primarily due to the plant's non-uniform maturation, making it challenging to harvest. To overcome this issue and reduce manual labor and lost yield, our team designed a vacuum and cyclone-based harvester. The proposed harvester is expected to decrease the amount of labor required and increase yield. The final design incorporates a vacuum for collection and a cyclone for separation. ANSYS and SolidWorks simulations were utilized in the design process, and pressure and velocity inlet and outlet calculations provide evidence supporting the design's effectiveness. The simulated pressure and velocity values were found to be 0.51 Pa and 0.79 m/s, respectively, which align with the calculated values. This design and further development of a seed harvester is capable of collecting and separating mature seeds from various plants with an overall efficiency of 92%.
studentNames: Leslie Benitez, Ching Ting Roy Ng, Vanessa Lopez, Jaime Cueva Escoto, Alexis Araujo

abstract: Ingomar Packing Company is a producer of multiple tomato dice and paste products. An integral component of their operation involves the manual assembly of 300-gallon wooden bins, which are then lined and filled with tomato products. Our group developed a system to reduce the physical requirements of bin assembly, allowing a wider array of workers to take part in the process. This was accomplished through the usage of a “tilt table” system to reorient bin component stacks in conjunction with a vacuum lifter system to physically move components into their assembly position.
studentNames: Austin Ashworth, Jackson Korsgaard, Anthony Travieso, Jair Martinez Martinez, Alexander Minton

projectTitle: Design and implementation of a robotic actuation mechanism for collecting leaf spectral data.
abstract: Working with the Internet of Things for Precision Agriculture, we helped to develop an actuation device to support an end effector. This lab, the IoT4Ag works to further the goal of supporting agriculture with information technology from multiple sources to assist in decisions associated with crop production. The device being built will support a leaf spectroscopy device such that data collection can be performed autonomously. Compared to manual collection this will save money, gather more robust data, and limit the amount of hard manual labor being implemented in films. This device is a tested prototype to support a 1 kilogram payload with the ability to be mounted on various robotic bases.
studentNames: Jennifer Esmay, Andrew Schwartz, Odysseus Hadjioannou, Rigoberto Jimenez, Edeline Ortiz

abstract: Our project (Team 119) was to make a piece of apparel that could hold multiple  mastectomy drains after a mastectomy surgery so that the patient could be hands free. We wanted the patient to continue their daily activities without any restrictions and overall make their recovery period easier. The way we solved this problem was by making a belt & pouch system that secures the drains in place by being true to the drain size and can be adjusted to the patient's waist size.
studentNames: Amani Byron, Chikaodili Obiorah, Ana Lopez, Jesus Beltran, Jingheng Huang

abstract: Laird Manufacturing is an agricultural equipment manufacturer that is known for dairy cow feeders. These dairy feeders mix the cattle feed with an auger and deposit the food using a chute as it is towed by a truck or tractor. Three of the mixer models that Laird sells, the VR800, VR1200, and VR1500 have wheelbase dimensions that do not allow their current chute designs to be fitted to the mixer. Whenever a client wants to buy a mixer of one of the three models, Laird must spend valuable time engineering a unique and expensive solution for each customer. Our design solves this problem by using a single chute design that can be fitted to any of the three mixers using an adapter system. Our team designed a prototype that was machined by Laird manufacturing for their VR1500 mixer. Our solution allows Laird to manufacture a single feed-out option for these three trucks and save money on design and labor for every mixer sale.
studentNames: Diego Rios, Joshua Murillo Gil, Francisco Aguirre, Joseph Del Mundo, Edward Rodriguez

projectTitle: Tulare Water Quality to improve Water Sustainability
abstract: Land O' Lakes is one of the largest agricultural cooperatives in the United States, headquartered in Minnesota. The Tulare facility mainly focuses on butter and milk powder production. The current production processes create a large amount of wastewater and organic waste. We were tasked with improving the quality of the water (conductivity) and proposing solutions to improve water sustainability, while consuming minimal capital. This was done by reducing BOD (biological oxygen demand), TSS (total suspended solids), or flow of wastewater. We concluded that reusing RO (reverse osmosis) treated wastewater in the CIP (cleaning in place) process would improve sustainability by reducing the usage of fresh potable water, thus sending less wastewater down the drain. Our solution has an estimated reduction of 41 million gallons of freshwater usage annually.
studentNames: Isabel Arreola, Scott Garcia, Meghan Mendoza, Tony Chen, Atnasia Mengistu

abstract: The objective was to create an end effector for an apple harvesting robotic system for the employer Milano Technical Group. The goals were to harvest an apple without bruising or puncturing it, cause no harm to the tree, finish harvesting in under two seconds, be able to actuate over three million times, and be hollow for internal transport.  The team decided to use a suction cup to grasp the apple and used a motor to implement a bend-and-pull motion that is popular with apple farmers. A prototype was constructed based on these principles and various lab tests were conducted to find the maximum tension on the stem so the design can be able to harvest.  After testing, it was found that the suction cup exceeded expectations but would need more torque for the worst case scenario.
studentNames: George Montross, Sheindel Gamerberg, Marcoantonio Lopez, Xavier Garcia, Sergio Manriquez

projectTitle: Air Quality Assistance System for Agriculture
abstract: Milano Technical Group specializes in providing advanced data analysis and information systems to empower the agricultural industry. Recently, our team, Air Quality Care, was assigned to create a device that could effectively minimize the amount of dust kicked up by tractor tires. Our innovative solution involved developing tire covers that could be fitted over the rear tires of the tractor. These covers were designed to redirect and reduce the dust particles that are typically emitted by the rear tires.
studentNames: Oscar Pacas Alvarez, Caleb Hoppe, Alvin Yu, Andres Lua, Maranda Nicole Azurin

abstract: Olam Food Ingredients, OFI, processes and packages roasted and salted almonds, macadamias, and almond flour. During the packaging process of the flour line, product would fall onto the floor, resulting in contaminated flour that cannot be used again, costing the company significant loss in income. Our team, CAP-116, was tasked with creating a solution to prevent flour from falling onto the floor. Working with the client’s engineers, our team designed shields that cover critical spillage points, and adjusted parts of the packaging line through software and hardware modifications. The testing results from our prototype have shown reduced flour spillage in our client’s flour packaging line.
studentNames: Anthony Navarro, Marlon Ayala, Isacc Ortiz-Madrigal, Nahum Lozano Samperio, Alex Chu

projectTitle: Failure analysis toolkit - Shock/Vibration Measurement  - Industrial Robots
abstract: The client, OMRON Robotics and Safety Technologies, designs and manufactures robots and safety sensor products used in factory automation. Our project consisted of creating a failure analysis toolkit to enable rapid troubleshooting of reported problems for their HD-1500 mobile robot. The solution we designed consisted of measuring shock/vibration that quantifies the forces the HD-1500 experiences both inside and outside of it. Using time stamping, the sensor data that is collected is then correlated to the robots travel path managed by OMRON’s software. This data along with the location will help troubleshoot failures caused by excessive forces incurred due to the conditions of the floor.
studentNames: Alexander Salinas Carino, Odes Kilpatrick, Miguel Paniagua, Jonathan Sevilla, Chase Williams

abstract: The client, Omron in Pleasanton, California tasked Team 120 on working with the Viper 650 six axis robot. The Viper 650 is used in many manufacturing operations such as material handling, assembly and many more uses as six axis robots play a crucial part in the production due to their wrist flexibility. Omron tasked Team 120 with creating a code in which the viper can detect the payload of an object without having modified the Viper 650 physically. The project reached its goal as it provides Omron a way to detect a payload without any physical modifications, but results have yielded 6-8% error in payload detected.
studentNames: Jamileth Bermudez, Daniel Ochoa, Jiajie Situ, Randy Hernandez, Joshua Vuong

projectTitle: Automated Drum Lid Application and Removal
abstract: Sensient natural ingredients specializes in the production of spices and flavor enhancers. Work In Progress (WIP) product is transported by fiber barrel drums within the manufacturing plant, which are manually loaded and sealed. Our client is concerned about the labor intensity and ergonomic hazards to the  team members. To resolve this issue, we have developed a prototype of an automatic drum lid applicator. This solution leverages a crane hand and CNC machine’s travel components to retrieve the clamp and lid to apply them to an idle waiting drum on a conveyer belt.
studentNames: Adrian Buitron Boada, Dion Deng, Christopher Flores Varona, Pedro-Alonso Rocha-Martin, Anthony Yang

abstract: Sensient Natural Ingredients based in Turlock, CA produces a broad range of organic, non-GMO, clean-label dry seasoning products for many renowned consumer and industrial companies. Currently, Sensient aims to increase the efficiency of the initial sterilization step in their chili production. Upon examination, we found significant variability in the sterilization process parameters. To maximize efficiency in this step, we propose a control system that automatically modulates system parameters to increase process stability and meet the sterilization standards set by the client. To attain this goal, we developed a heat-transfer model that incorporates system settings and logged data to approximate the sterilizer’s behavior. We used statistical models pertaining to temperature profile, superheated conditions, and profile time to test and further develop this model. Our model can be incorporated into a control system that uses a touchscreen user interface and Modbus gateway that integrates into the client’s current hardware. This solution will allow the sterilizer to operate more efficiently, reduce waste, and increase the overall throughput of the sterilizer.
studentNames: Bianca Ortiz-Larios, Edwin Vilches, Kwame Cherukuri, Samantha Tran, Talha Shah

abstract: Sensient Natural Ingredients (SNI), Livingston, is a large-scale processing facility for producing dehydrated onion and garlic products. Currently due to a variety of issues, garlic and onion have been sticking to the conveyor belt of an industrial dryer. While SNI already has methods to try and remove it, the product removed from the belt simply falls onto the factory floor, making a mess of the facility and results in the product going to waste. To combat this we have designed an improved wet brush alongside targeted water sprays to scrape stuck product off the belt in a more efficient manner. Additionally, we also designed a recovery auger with a z-style conveyor working in tandem to transport product scraped off the belt to a collection point. This will mean stuck product can be scraped off the belt and recycled back into the system, greatly reducing waste.
studentNames: Cristian Mejia, Eric Olson, Anthony Estrada Rivera, Juan Castrejon, Cristian Zapien

projectTitle: Implementation of nanoscale position scanning capabilities into a fluorescence confocal microscope for visualization of single protein molecules
abstract: Understanding biological processes occurring on the nanoscale is crucial for biomedical research. To achieve these capabilities with cost efficiency the Muñoz Lab at UC Merced is modifying their confocal microscope with nano scanning capabilities through a MadCity Labs nano driver, nano stage, and Picoquant hydraharp integrated through LabVIEW. Our team was assigned to implement this hardware into LabVIEW programs to access, utilize and control these experiments and data formed from it. Using built-in drivers our team formed a program that creates a customized grid across the observed sample. Which is then measured for photon intensity over a specified period of time. To confirm the accuracy of our work, a sample containing fluorescence micron-sized beads will be analyzed utilizing both the nano-scanning setup and C-Trap optical tweezers from Lumicks by members of the Muñoz lab.
studentNames: Artury Ramirez, Cesar Sotero, Jeremiah Timbol, Jocelyn Gonzalez, Victoria Okafor, Christopher Kaye

projectTitle: Early Diagnosis of Intracranial Pathology in Pediatrics by Sensor Development
abstract: According to the Academy of Pediatrics, there are about 1.4 million cases of concussions annually. Often, the diagnosis of brain injury and timely intervention is delayed with decision making by non-healthcare providers in the field. To address this issue, the team was tasked by Valleys Children's Hospital to develop a portable headgear for early diagnosis. The team's solution involves using dry EEG sensors with a P50 Auditory Stimulus Test. The P50 test records brain waves in response to repeated auditory stimuli every 500ms and analyzes them 50ms after the click. By measuring the percentage decrease of the brain waves, patients can be advised on whether medical attention is necessary in a timely manner.
studentNames: Vivian Nguyen, Christian Quintero Meza, Annie Ly, Caroline Amadet Barragan-Laguna, Kristal Navarro

abstract: Agrecom provides a spectrum of solutions in the areas of water treatment and sanitation, targeting clients in agriculture, residential, and commercial sectors. Agrecom lacks a centralized system that would rapidly transform client data into actionable information, replacing their obsolete method of manual classification. Our product is designed to identify critical areas of rodent activity, by inputting a parent Ranch folder into Amazon’s Machine Learning Rekognition Engine Model, which then outputs the total number of rats captured, per camera. Upon storing these statistics into the AWS RDS database, our cloud-based web-server then polls and displays a map that uniquely indexes all clients with their respective aggregate totals. Our goal is to fully maximize the data sample, in order to proactively mitigate the financial aftermath of rodent infestations, in effect, initiating a beneficial dialogue for both the client and company.
studentNames: Anson Yu, Gerardo Garduno, Mike Monokandilos, Milan Overholtzer, Matthew Randolph, Torrance Zhou

abstract: Betts Company is a manufacturer specializing in springs and heavy-duty truck parts based in Fresno, CA. Employees are expected to be properly trained to perform specific tasks in their work center; however, Betts was having trouble managing employee data. Betts needed a user-friendly multi-platform solution to keep track of employee training. Team " (SMP)" has designed a web application which allows Betts management to view employee training records and Betts HR to edit, and update said records. This web application provides easy access for management to search through employee training records while providing HR easy access to edit and view the data.
studentNames: Andrew Arriaga, Abbas Rizvi, Crystal Ling, Dominique Soriano, Omar Ramirez

abstract: California Transplants is a grower supply company for seedlings located in Newman, California. They specialize in tomatoes, pepper, broccoli, cauliflower, melon, cabbage, and industrial hemp seedlings. Their facility consists of 274 greenhouses that will use over 400 million seeds provided by their customers. Our project worked with tomato plants, and we have created a tool to count the number of viable seedlings in a tray using image processing technology. A ‘viable’ seedling is determined by if it grows its cotyledon, or the first two or three leaves when the seed germinates. The solution we created processes up to thousands of images by isolating the colors of the seedlings, detecting their contours, and finally determining the good and bad seedling counts to be uploaded to their inventory.
studentNames: Alan Barajas, Jamie Mariz Laudencia, Jason Petersen, Kyle Canotal, Rafael Eduardo Lintao

projectTitle: Bottling Change Part Wear Measuring Tool
abstract: E. & J. Gallo is a winery and distributor headquartered in Modesto California.  It is the largest family-owned winery in the US and is the leading provider of California wines. With its large-scale manufacturing, comes many parts that need to be changed and maintained. The solution that our team came up with is an app that uses object detection to see whether or not the capper from one of the machines is worn enough to warrant a replacement. If this proof of concept is continued, it could potentially help with wear detection across more parts as data is collected. We have also taken steps to tackle the problem of precision measuring on the phone so that the problem has the potential to be solved in the future.
studentNames: Bryant Chon, David Arizpe Arroyo, Daisy Hernandez, Jashandeep Singh, Miguel Martinez, Navjot Sandhu

projectTitle: Autonomous Vehicle user Interface and Data System
abstract: Milano Technical Group is an engineering consulting firm specializing in agriculture and industrial automation. They are currently working on an automated material handling robot which would make its way around the warehouse carrying up to 8,000 pounds. Our team's job was to create an interactive web-app for monitoring and directing the robots. Through this web-app supervisors can create forklift driver accounts and assign them tasks. Additionally, this web-app allows forklift drivers to pick up tasks assigned by the supervisors and direct robots according to those tasks. This product will significantly improve time efficiency for the forklift drivers by automating warehouse item transportation process.
studentNames: Ahmad Suleiman, Christian-John Forcadilla, Siddhant Abhijit, Veronika Nechaeva, Winston Lou

abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies (ORT) manufactures and produces innovative robot and safety sensor products. ORT is looking to implement a solution to reduce manual inspection labor by using machine learning to accurately automate defect detection under standard work cell lighting conditions. The team, Detect.io, utilized a machine learning model that the team trained with images of scratches and paint chips of given robotic parts from ORT. As a result of this project, ORT will receive images of samples with defects to strengthen their inspection database remotely for further research possibilities for ORT’s clients to have more consistent products.
studentNames: Danny Li, Gabriel Allan Navata, Somya Shah, Valentinno Cruz, Vince Nguyen

abstract: SupHerb Farms is a company whose primary business is contracting culinary herb production, processing of those herbs, and sale of the finished products around the world. Looking to improve upon their current visual estimations, SupHerb Farms tasked the team to develop a vision-based system that utilizes images to produce accurate yield estimations of basil plants photographed. The team designed a program that is able to segmentate only the basil plant in order to calculate a plant volume. Using inputted images from a user, the program creates an estimated volume. Making further calculations, an estimate is calculated for mass yield, which is presented to the user on a web page.
studentNames: Julian Hernandez, Santiago Ramirez Manzo, Stanley Yuan, Tahea Hossain, Vincent Filipinas, Zhihao Bai

abstract: Sweep designs and manufactures industrial wireless meters and big data workflow software for real-time equipment monitoring. They have recently launched the SweepAPI, a simple cloud-based data gathering platform for storing and analyzing time series data in a scalable fashion. Currently, Sweep does not have a feature that handles data during API server downtime. The team developed a way to buffer client data and send it to the appropriate endpoint whenever downtime is detected.
studentNames: Abel Getachew, Adam Shaker, Jaydon Harris Dudley, Kevin Espinoza, Ryan Nostrand, Ralphilou Tatoy

projectTitle: Automated barcode scanning of containers in an outdoor warehouse
abstract: The Morning Star Company accounts for over 30% of the California processing tomato production, supplying 40% of the U.S. ingredient tomato paste and diced tomato markets. Currently, the company has workers manually scan the barcodes located on the containers before placing them at the designated location. This scanning process requires a lot of time and only around 80% of the containers are scanned without errors. Our team has created a solution that automates this scanning process, making it more efficient and accurate. A camera mounted on the forklift will automatically scan the barcode when picking up the containers and detect if the barcode is properly scanned via an audible notification. The camera is connected to a monitor inside the forklift which enables the driver to input the designated location of the container, and to oversee the entire process.
studentNames: Brayan Ortiz, Jerin Sajimon, Lucas Netto, Luis Palafox, Malik Khan, Warren Liang

projectTitle: Vision System to Detect Product Loss in Tomato Harvesting
abstract: The Morning Star Company works on all aspects of tomato production, with subsidiary California Sun Grower Services focusing on harvesting. The client wanted a vision system on a harvester, facing towards the back, that monitors any product loss during tomato harvesting to allow for adjustments to prevent further loss. The team’s solution was to establish a visual detection system using an object detection method to find dropped tomatoes in the live camera footage. It keeps track of the tomatoes that it identifies to estimate a total count. The team integrated a notification system to alert the harvester with a pop up on screen after a certain amount of tomatoes were detected and an Excel database to store the data.
studentNames: Achyuth Kolluru, Brandon Kieng, Christian Guiang, Shane Clanton, Tiffany Peng

abstract: The Computer Science and Engineering (CSE) Department at the University of California, Merced uses a complex formula for assigning Teaching Assistants (TAs) to undergraduate courses. Applying the formula by hand is a time consuming, error prone process, resulting in potentially sub-optimal allocations. The team constructed a web application that is intuitive, reliable and easy to use, with a strong emphasis on security and privacy, due to the sensitive nature of the data. The department can use this application to make fair, equitable, and transparent assignments for TAs in a fraction of the time it would have normally taken, allowing professors to dedicate more time to their educational and research activities.
studentNames: Harleen Mann, Isabella Montoya, Lamine Geordan Sunga Kouangue, Valeria Cortez, Yee Thao

projectTitle: Inventory of Trees - Phase 2: Size and Alive
abstract: The UC Merced Experimental Farm supports agricultural research focused on sustainability, new technologies, and the overall future of farming. Local Farmers have partnered with UC Merced Experimental Farm in hopes to lessen the time spent on keeping track of the status of all the trees that are in their orchards. To solve this problem, our team was tasked with developing a software that automatically measures a tree and analyzes its condition. Our software utilizes machine learning accelerated image recognition to classify tree conditions so that farmers can efficiently manage the condition of their farms without wasting the time and effort of their employees.
studentNames: David Gantman, David Lucman, Ethan Reidel, Intiser Aziz, Seth Dougherty

abstract: United States Cold Storage is a warehousing/logistics company that provides shipping, receiving, and storage solutions for the refrigerated and frozen food industries. Loading a truck with proper weight distribution is a skill learned from experience. Given recently high turnover rates, newer employees often make mistakes loading trucks, leading to the truck being sent back to be re-loaded and increasing overhead costs. The team was able to create a tool that generates a suggested basic loading pattern based on the parameters of the truck and the pallet order. This tool will both decrease the number of errors an inexperienced loader might face and streamline the process for an experienced loader.
studentNames: Allen Li, Christopher Tapia, Johnny Zhu, Nathan Estrada, Samuel Nguyen

abstract: Valley Children’s Hospital (VCH) is a healthcare institution located in the central valley of California, providing excellent healthcare services to children. In order to enhance the VCH’s virtual medicine services, this project explores the use of Virtual Reality (VR) to track movement during neurological examinations for more accuracy. The team developed a VR environment and a front-end, in which a doctor can proctor a neurological exam for patients with a brain injury, such as a stroke. This exam would track the patient’s elbow flexion/extension, and extract that positional data to compute the angular velocity and create a classification of strength based on the angular velocity achieved. The purpose of this is to hasten the evaluation of the injuries sustained by the patient along key points on their spinal cord. The front-end will allow the doctor to view exam information, along with generating a report on the exam results.
studentNames: Abbas Siddiqui, Chloe Engel, John Dominic Biton, Leonel Paredes Barrera, Salvador Avelar-Lopez, Shreya Shriram

abstract: Veracruz Ventures is a company based in Portugal that focuses on smart farms, applying and developing cutting-edge technology include data management. Veracruz supports the Innovate to Grow program to develop data management software and search modules applicable to Ag and beyond. Existing meeting scheduler technologies have flaws such as assuming participants having the same relevance or needing to be present for the entire time, leading to meetings being canceled, postponed or lacking critical participants. The team developed a meeting scheduler that builds upon features from existing technologies such as Doodle and When2Meet. The application the team developed allows participants of a meeting to rank their availability for each time slot on a scale of unavailable, flexible, and available. The organizer has the ability to rank the relevance of each participant such that the resulting availability graph represents a 2-dimensional scale with one axis being the general availability and the other axis being availability of relevant participants.
studentNames: Aishwaria Rangasamy, Chester Merino, Erick Vargas, Eric Wu, Phien Nguyen, Sofiah Lawal

projectTitle: Constructing Composite Geographical Areas for a Search - Phase 2
abstract: When using Google maps, have you ever wanted to search within certain areas but exclude other areas, such as the coast or within 2 different cities? Veracruz Ventures is a company based in Portugal that focuses on smart farms, applying and developing cutting-edge technology include data management. Veracruz supports the Innovate to Grow program to develop data management software and search modules applicable to Ag and beyond. Our team goal was to allow users to search for points of interest within predefined areas on a map, rather than the whole visible map. The team created a web application using Python Flask and Google Maps Javascript API to create an interactive map that allows a user to create multiple shapes as defined areas to search, then makes an API request using Google Cloud for points of interest within those areas, and return in a list format those results.
studentNames: Christopher Gauthier, Donovan Malloy, Jorman Saucedo, Luis Morante

abstract: Western Digital Corporation (WDC) is an American computer hard disk drive (HDD) and solid-state drive (SSD) manufacturer and data storage company. The Flash SSD engineering department is located in Milpitas, California. WDC annually hires many new engineers in the Silicon Technology and Manufacturing (STM) department for various functions within the department. The manager faces some challenges in training new hires since recruiting new hires happens at different times and it is hard to do group training. Therefore, the WD_Bobcats developed a training web application that allows WDC managers to assign various courses to employees on a needed basis. This website will enable managers to give feedback and grade answers for their respective assigned employees. Upon completing those assigned courses, the new hires can illustrate their understanding of the material, prepare for their job position, and further allow the company to thrive. Managers will have a better understanding of the employee's knowledge, and employees will feel much happier having standardized courses that will sharpen their skills.
studentNames: Ashmir Moni, Gursagar Singh, Justin Noah Dumindin, Sarah Padilla, Wilfried Aubin Ngongang Yomba, Socheata Hour

abstract: Western Digital is a data infrastructure company specializing in data storage products such as solid-state drives and hard disk drives. They utilize Apache Subversion (SVN) for software versioning and software control. Western Digital had the need for a web application to analyze its engineers’ development activities to make better decisions for resource planning. The team has created DevTracker which is a web application that pulls information from the SVN server and uses it to generate key performance indicators (KPI) and display them to the user.
studentNames: Adrian Luciano Cervantes, Jose Luciano, Johnny Ly, Krishna Movva, Richard Mejia Gutierrez, Sean Saki

projectTitle: Evaluating and Designing Strategies for Reducing Vulnerability to Extreme Heat in Merced, CA
studentNames: Jennifer Dolores, Noe Garay, Cynthia Vazquez

projectTitle: Evaluating and Designing GHG Mitigation Strategies for Transportation Activity in Merced, CA
studentNames: Andrew Howard, Sawyer Ingersoll, Sierra Lema

abstract: The Blue Diamond Growers almond receiving station located in Salida, California proposed the opportunity to produce an improved truck bed almond sampling system. They receive a large quantity of almonds from August to December which must go through a sampling process to determine the quality, moisture, and variety for further processing. The current sampling techniques are inadequate because they do not collect a  representative sample of the entire truckload and lack ergonomic functionality. The goal is to develop a device that can extract 500g-600g of almonds along at least a 4 ft depth of a truck bed. This must be done without causing damage to the almond meats while achieving ergonomic functionality and collecting the sample efficiently (30 secs per sample). The design produced by our group consists of a motorized custom auger probe which is mounted on top of a railing system that will be connected to the sampling platform. Our team created a series of handheld auger probe prototypes, tested them in operating conditions, and determined a design that meets our desired client needs.
studentNames: Diana Sepulveda, Ivan Jimenez, Michelle Padilla, Ronan Eltherington, Victor Rubio

projectTitle: Quick down bottle inspection, and recovery for immediate use.
abstract: Delicato Family Wines sells millions of cases of wine each year. Currently they discard a good number of empty bottles that fall over. These bottles fall down between the depalletizer and the filler stations, and the main area for damage is on the lip of the bottle. Without a system in place to inspect these fallen bottles, they immediately go into recycling to ensure the safety of Delicato’s customers. Our design is the Bottle Inspector or B.I. It has two cameras and a laser mounted inside an enclosed shell. When a bottle is inserted, it will be rotated which allows the two systems to gather data quickly. This data is then used to determine if the bottle is cracked, chipped, or damaged in any other way. This system will be oversensitive to prevent any potentially damaged bottles getting to the consumers.
studentNames: Andrew Koerber, Brandon Wong, Devon Chappell, Lexi Harnack, Salvador Herrera, Brandon Gallardo

abstract: Delicato Family Wines is one of the fastest growing wineries in the U.S. with national and international distribution. During production, wine bottles may occasionally break creating glass shards that may possibly contaminate other bottles. Our team, Wine Watchers, was tasked to create a device that would allow for the detection of those glass shards without damaging or contaminating the wine bottles. Using the concept of light refraction, our team created a prototype that isolates wine bottles from all outside light sources and rotates above a powerful light bulb. A special low light camera was also connected from within the device to an outside monitor to allow live video feed of the rotating wine bottle. Our design has shown improved accuracy of detecting potential impurities within wine bottles.
studentNames: Julian Diaz, Omar Villalpando, Roberto De La Torre Real, Rose Fisher, Rod Erik Villa

abstract: The almond industry is aware of the dust generated during harvest and has designated the reduction of harvest dust by 50% as an industry goal.  Current methods of harvest involve shaking, sweeping, and gathering nuts intermixed with a drying period on the ground.  With off-ground harvesting systems, all steps can be consolidated, reducing dust generation by 75%, but drying time is also eliminated, which poses a problem because hullers require nuts to arrive dried. The objective of this project was to design a system that can enable farming operations to dry almonds on-site economically, using equipment already available. Our team performed various experiments on a smaller scale to simulate how the almond hulls would dry inside mesh compartments at different depths. The results showed that we are able to dry larger amounts of almond hulls in a single structure given proper airflow. The final design consists of repurposing an almond hopper trailer with multiple mesh compartments that serve to separate the almonds, and allows natural and artificial airflow to dry the almond hulls.
studentNames: Edgar Castilla, Julio Samaniego Jr., Marcos Alvarez, Medardo Ramirez, Nathan Downey

projectTitle: Powder Bagging Process Optimization and Defect Reduction
abstract: Land O’ Lakes is Fortune 232 of the Fortune 500 companies list and is deep-rooted in the dairy industry, producing a variety of dairy products for the entire country. The Tulare plant is part of the Dairy Foods business, focusing on the production of butter, milk powders, and dairy fluids. Team 204 was tasked with reducing the powder bagging defects produced by the current system in place, in which, the powdered product is bagged and placed on a conveyor line where various pieces of information are printed onto the bag such as production date and product number. Oftentimes the values printed on the bag are printed incorrectly due to various reasons, which can result in a reduction of the First Pass Quality. As a result of the significant loss of profit due to these factors, we integrated a reclamation machine and linear actuator as well as designed a swivel arm into the powder bagging station process to help minimize the defects, as well as maximize product production and overall revenue by reducing the number of powder bags being sold for animal feed. Reducing the number of powder bags sold as animal feed is essential as it significantly decreases the company’s overall profit due to the low prices at which the product is sold. This new system will detect printing errors and isolate defective bags from the main production line, allowing for the defective bag to be recirculated to a secondary line where a reclamation machine will cut open and recover the product inside, saving Land O' Lakes thousands of pounds of product.
studentNames: Arline Sanchez, Erik Barrera, Elio Garcia, Immanual Carney, Maria Rodriguez

abstract: Mizuho Medical Company is a worldwide provider of medical equipment, specializing in the development of innovative neurosurgical devices in order to advance surgical techniques and improve patient health. They currently offer an ultrasonic micro-vascular Doppler probe and transceiver that provide audio blood flow detection to aid in determining the success of cerebral aneurysm clipping. The client was interested in exploring whether the latest advancements in technology could provide a worthwhile upgrade to their current device. After considering all applicable technologies, we have proposed a design that utilizes laser speckle contrast imaging (LSCI), and relatively inexpensive components, to provide a low-cost solution for non-invasive blood flow detection. Through initial testing using an apparatus designed to mimic cerebral blood flow, we were able to calibrate our chosen electronic components and prove the basic functionality of this unique technology. Further development of the LSCI device, and refinement of the imaging processing software, would allow the company the opportunity to market the device for additional neurosurgical applications and expand its market share.
studentNames: Alexis Lozano Velasco, Alexander Ochoa, Chelsea Moniz, Christopher Patzan Lopez, Jon Embry, Marco Antonio De Jesus Padua

abstract: The Neil Jones Food Company produces a variety of California tomato and Pacific Northwest fruit products for industrial, food service, and retail customers. TomaTek owns one of the tomato plants under the company. As of now, this factory has eight fillers side-by-side and two different delivery systems on both ends of the filler lines. The boxes of bags for each filler are currently carried by hand to the fillers. This bag delivery method creates an issue regarding workers safety. The challenge is that there is not much space in the area therefore only one box is kept at the location. For our project we designed a box delivery system that delivers the correct boxes of bags to the correct filler station. This will improve the ffectiveness of overal work quality and efficiency of the tomato packaging facility.
studentNames: Bryan Reyes Morales, Daniel Perez-Zoghbi, Erick Modesto Aban, Isaac Gabriel Nartea, Joseph Hoang, Jose Aguado

abstract: The Morning Star Company is an industry leader that supplies 10% of the world’s ingredient tomato products, produced in some of the world’s highest-capacity tomato processing plants. At these factories, they unload tomatoes by flooding the tomato trailer with water and then flushing the tomatoes out of a door in the trailer. At the end of this process, they experience a tapering of the flow of tomatoes which impacts the effective capacity and efficiency of downstream processes. We were challenged with developing technologies that could contribute to improving the consistency of flow of tomatoes out of the trailers to further increase plant performance. We decided to use cameras and image processing in order to detect the tomatoes and show how they are flowing. This system was able to detect the red pixels from a live feed video and produced a graph that shows the trends in how the tomatoes are flowing. This system will let the client see how to improve their unloading process.
studentNames: Alyssa Castro, Alejandra Gil-Mares, Francisco Alonso, Omar Covarrubias, Oscar Cruz

projectTitle: End Effector for Robotic Tray Handling in Green House
abstract: Our client, The Morningstar company, who processes tomatoes, asked us to assist in creating an automated solution for their greenhouse's transplant growing operation. As the tomatoes are grown in trays arranged in aisles parallel with the central walkway in greenhouses, the trays nearest the walkway are more exposed. These trays are rotated 180 degrees to help even out their growth. We designed and manufactured a mobile, battery-powered chassis to transport a UR10 robotic arm down these aisles, and an end effector connected to the end of the arm to lift and rotate the trays. As the robot has a control pad and CPU container, these were relocated to the rear of our prototype for ease of access, with remote control implemented for the front motors to allow the user to manually navigate the robot when necessary.
studentNames: Diego Escobar, Daniel Fierro, Jewell Graham, Jasper Thienes, Nicholas Maldonado

abstract: Our client, The Pi Shop in Fresno, CA, helps people develop ideas into products and businesses. They tasked our team with developing a device to aid in the transfer of wheelchair bound people between their wheelchair and another surface, such as a chair, toilet, bed, car, etc. Our team created a lifting platform with an extendable transfer board that would help the user by enabling them to transfer across a level surface instead of having to fight gravity. This platform is powered by a winch and cable system, because a linear actuator was too heavy to be reasonably portable. Additionally, the drawer slides that allow the platform to raise and lower are oriented with one pair facing front to back, and the other pair facing side to side. This was done to increase the stability of the platform when raised, and when the user is shifting on it, by utilizing the drawer slides axis of greatest strength in both directions. We hope our device will give wheelchair users the help they need to get to and from various surfaces.
studentNames: Felipe Garza, Fabian Ramirez, Gordon Fredrickson, Jacqueline Garcia, Rusball Venalonzo

abstract: Our client is Dr. Nadarasa Visveshwara, a neonatologist at Valley Children’s Hospital in Madera, CA. Infants in the neonatal intensive care unit are often placed on high-frequency mechanically assisted ventilation, or HFV. It is postulated that overexposure to HFV can increase fluid pressure throughout the brain, including in the eye, which can result in retinopathy of prematurity, or ROP, an eye disease that can cause blindness in premature infants. To detect these pressure fluctuations, we designed a Doppler ultrasound sensor to monitor blood flow velocity in the blood vessel under the infant’s fontanelle. We incorporated a piezoelectric transmitter and receiver pair at two different inclination angles within a medical-grade silicone housing. We tested the sensor by pumping blood-mimicking fluid through channels in a skin-like material known as polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS). We used oscilloscopes to measure transmitted and received frequencies from our sensor and compared the flow velocity from the frequency shift to the actual flow velocity from the pump flow rate. Our sensor can be used for a series of tests to obtain FDA approval for clinical use.
studentNames: Heidy Gomez, Holly Hoang, Jesus Cruz Lopez, Jonathan Valdivia, Ruth Verrinder

abstract: The Fresno Discovery Center is the premier Children’s Museum in the Central Valley. Beginning in August 2021, UC Merced students updated an existing ball-drop exhibit with a twist. The updated ball-drop exhibit utilized multiple components designed to catch and return the falling ball while passing it through various obstacles. This exhibit showcased the student’s applicable knowledge of physics and engineering designing. Students in the Engineering Service-Learning course designed, tested their components, and delivered this product to the Fresno Discovery Center in May 2022.
studentNames: Jasmine Camacho, Ronald Fortt, Wendy Haw, Noorulain Irshad, Hannia Otero Castaneda, Hannah Joyce Villasin

abstract: The Fresno Discovery Center is the premier Children’s Museum in the Central Valley. Beginning in August 2021, UC Merced students updated an existing ball-drop exhibit with a twist. The updated ball-drop exhibit utilized multiple components designed to catch and return the falling ball while passing it through various obstacles. This exhibit showcased the student’s applicable knowledge of physics and engineering designing. Students in the Engineering Service-Learning course designed, tested their components, and delivered this product to the Fresno Discovery Center in May 2022.
studentNames: Helen Ayala, Nina Cofield, Lucia Gnerre, Joshua Phelps, Valeria Trujillo, Cynthia Vazquez

abstract: The Fresno Discovery Center is the premier Children’s Museum in the Central Valley. Beginning in August 2021, UC Merced students updated an existing ball-drop exhibit with a twist. The updated ball-drop exhibit utilized multiple components designed to catch and return the falling ball while passing it through various obstacles. This exhibit showcased the student’s applicable knowledge of physics and engineering designing. Students in the Engineering Service-Learning course designed, tested their components, and delivered this product to the Fresno Discovery Center in May 2022.
studentNames: Fasha Lennon, Luna Lynn, Laily Martinez, Laura Munoz, Alonso Ortiz, Itzel Otero Cast

abstract: Agrecom is a company providing water treatment, sanitation, and pest control services to local agricultural, residential, and commercial properties. One of their water treatment services involves taking live readings of water quality measures, which could only be viewed through on-site devices. Agrecom was seeking a more convenient way of configuring and viewing data remotely, as well as a means of long-term storage. This semester, Team Pacific developed a web application which centralizes all of the data on the cloud while making it accessible over the internet. This provides Agrecom with the convenience of configuring and viewing data remotely, and savings on the equipment that was previously used to display data on-site.
studentNames: Karna Errande, Jared Giampietro, Jason Van Tuinen, Luna Lynn, Sabir Kirpal

abstract: Since 1868, Betts company has specialized in producing truck parts for all types of trucks. The Betts Spring Manufacturing team provides services with custom designing and other industrial applications for their spring coils. Part of the process involves painting a logo with a serial number onto the coils, however there are often times where the logo or the serial number is unreadable and is sent back by the customer. To combat this problem, our team sought to create a program that will scan the logo, determine if it's legible, and notify the worker if it's good for packaging to allow the spring producing process to be much smoother with little to no human opinion.
studentNames: Kshav Ayer, Angel Monroy, Stephen Garcia, Sunita Thao, Vedaant Vyas

abstract: Betts Company creates custom designs and manufactures coil and leaf springs for transportation and other industrial applications. Sometimes, these manufactured logos on the springs can contain visual defects that impact both the customer and the company. The Visual Solutions team created an image classification program that can detect when a logo isn’t up to the company’s specifications or quality and will notify the operator whether the logo is good quality or bad. The goal is to detect errors on logos before the springs are packaged and delivered to the customer to provide quality assurance.
studentNames: Iveth Aguilar Centeno, Baisong Liu, David Yang, Erick Ruvalcaba-Sandoval, Yash Sharma

projectTitle: Automated Inventory System of Planted Trees in a Farm
abstract: Gemperle Family Farms is a family owned and operated farm, currently relying on manual labor to count and locate planted trees after the delivery from a nursery.  Team Tree2Tree was tasked with automating the process to reduce labor costs and increase accuracy. We developed a vision-based system utilizing Amazon Rekognition and built a machine learning model that detects trees through custom training labels. The software will be able to count and display tree data onto our web application, where users are able to see the count and relative location of the trees in a simulated map.
studentNames: Kenan Hurem, Adrian Serrano, Alann Tapia, Leandro Olmos, Xavier Ybarra

projectTitle: Automated Inventory System of Planted Trees in a Farm
abstract: Farms take inventory of thousands of trees across hundreds of acres by manually counting and measuring them. This process is very labor-intensive and repetitive. Innovate to Grow saw this problem as an opportunity. So, with the help of Gemperle Family Farms, team Tree 08 has laid the groundwork for automating this labor by creating a web app that detects and organizes data about trees from video and which we can extend to work with a drone or robot.
studentNames: Darshwak Dhami, Grant Lewandowski, Jose Magana, Marianela Garcia Mijango, Marcus Solomon

abstract: The Italian Scientists and Scholars in North America Foundation (ISSNAF) is the largest network of Italian professionals in North America. Annually, many users register to become a member or affiliate. In order to effectively communicate with their membership base, ISSNAF adds them to a mailing list. During this process, they are often faced with emails bouncing for various reasons. The Bobcat Mailer team presents a solution to this issue via a double email verification system that allows professionals to register to become members or affiliates.
studentNames: Aman Kaur, Kenneth Tran, Preethi Merugumala, Rahul B Rajaram, Ryan Saini

abstract: Italian Scientists & Scholars in North America Foundation is a nonprofit organization whose mission is to connect, empower and celebrate the Italian Intellectual Diaspora in North America.  ISSNAF has a problem common to many organizations that need to keep the members' email list up to date and manage bounces.  Members may register with a corporate email, which may later be closed if changing job; also organizations may use proxies that block lists, and mailboxes may just get full.  We created a system where a member can easily register two emails: if they no longer have access to one email, then they can update their information through the other.  This double email system enables organizations to keep track of their members, and handles the complexity of double email member verification without sacrificing security or creating accounts with passwords.
studentNames: Nykolai Coconi, Alvin To, Marco Limon, Nicholas Larson, Vanessa Soto-Sierra

abstract: A good product is known for the excellence in manufacturing to create a flawless product. UC Merced gave us an opportunity to meet the global leader in Industrial Automation- OMRON: A company that believes in :Innovation driven by social needs through automation to empower people.: Our team, Swole Autobots collaborated with the manufacturing team at Omron to automate the visual inspection process for Laser Safety Scanner OS32C. We provided an application to detect defects in their OS32C camera so that the final inspection process could be automated to reduce human error. This provides OMRON with more consistent and higher-quality products for its customers.
studentNames: Dani Bitar, Alex Lee, Dillon Marquard, Jessie Herrera, Lorenzo Scaturchio

abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies, Inc. (ORT) is a technology development company in the field of manufacturing industrial robots that strives to improve lives and contribute to a better society. Currently, Omron has an inspector review 50-60 parts a day. Team SZL created an autonomous solution with off-the-shelf products and created an image processing program that detects defects autonomously through an off-line application. In turn, this will help eliminate error and reduce time through an autonomous solution.
studentNames: Neida Alonso, Jatnael Montes, Lucas Tran, Richard Hernandez-Hernandez, Sergio Chairez

projectTitle: Predictive maintenance dashboard for fixed robots
abstract: Omron, a leader in robotic automation, tasked us with creating an application that can use sensor data received from a robot to algorithmically determine if the robot needs or will soon need maintenance. The application we developed represents the front end of this process designed to interact with a database where this log data is stored, process that data, and then present the results to the end-user via our application. Our predictive algorithm will use linear extrapolation to compare the sensor data from a robot to predefined limits on those statistics to detect if the robot currently requires maintenance or will within the next 24 hours.
studentNames: Shivneel Chand, Chester Yang, Duy Do, Matthew Mengell, Nicholas Manzano

projectTitle: Predictive maintenance dashboard for fixed robots
abstract: As Requested by OMRON, a leader in Consumer Electronics and Manufacturing Robotics, the Hummingbird Robotics team developed a Predictive Maintenance Dashboard to help ameliorate revenue loss due to unexpected maintenance-related downtime.  The Dashboard aims to predict immediate and future malfunctions by analyzing data received from fixed non-mobile robots. The Prediction algorithm uses two models. The first is a simple linear model that continuously monitors robot parameters to be in operational ranges. The second is an Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (ARIMA) model that aims to predict future breakdowns based on previously collected data.
studentNames: Nicholas Clifford, Alberto Valle, Connor Loo, Ezequiel Cuevas, Marthen Dominic Nodado

projectTitle: Smart UI for cable database management
abstract: SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory is currently building a X-Ray Free Electron Laser that will be the forefront of X-ray science. The cabling infrastructure in the facility however, is all done manually. Our team developed a user friendly web application that allows for cabling modifications to be inputted much easier, as well as implementing an approval system that allows administrators to approve changes an engineer makes. Inputting these changes from the web application into the database this way, we helped to replace a 20 year old procedure to improve time efficiency and reduce errors.
studentNames: Alejandro Alcala, Libanose Teffera, Mary Francine Delos Reyes, Sean O'callaghan

projectTitle: Smart UI for cable database management
abstract: Stanford’s SLAC National Accelerator Laboratory handles thousands of cables in their facilities through an Oracle database. The current database is unorganized because engineers are allowed to upload data freely without standardization, leading to many mistakes in the database. They asked for an advanced user interface using Python and the React API that accounts for quality assurance. The engineers will be able to access any cable file information, upload files automatically, add data, and edit data, while the project manager will be able to approve or deny file uploads. We provided a minimum viable product that takes in a local database. Users are allowed to upload files manually or through a spreadsheet file. They are also able to edit the data in the user interface and upload it to the approval queue. Project managers are allowed to approve or reject cables based on standardization in the approval queue. Once approved, it will go into the database.
studentNames: Ankan Bhattacharya, Aayush Koirala, Antony Kustura, Jaqueline Sanchez-Macias, Sabrina Gonzalez

abstract: In the face of large market demand, manufacturing firms across the country are under intense pressure to limit downtime and keep manufacturing delays minimal. Improved manufacturing efficiency through smart scheduling of resources aids a firm's position within a competitive marketplace and minimizes cost. Sweep is a company that strives to solve and optimize equipment efficiency using a data-driven approach. Team Blue Resonance has developed a program utilizing the SweepAPI and the Sweep manufacturing programming language to address this problem by creating optimal and automated production schedules for clients.
studentNames: Dale Alabastro, Abdias Tellez Benitez, Frank Olotu, Julian Grado

abstract: Sweep helps organizations gain a deeper understanding of their data, through automated data workflows to help uncover insights at a real-time pace. Given the ever-increasing demand to reduce manufacturing downtime, a production scheduler that is able to limit inefficiencies is an important tool to have. Currently, Sweep does not have an optimal production scheduler for optimizing production capability to effectively manufacture physical products on time. Big Precision has devised a robust algorithm which optimally schedules a given list of required tasks and the required resources to manufacture products. The team also developed both a command-line terminal application and a web application which uses the algorithm to generate the optimal schedule for manufacturing the products on time.
studentNames: Shawn Duong, Baljot Singh, Kenny Jung, Nathan Vasquez, Peter Mendoza

abstract: Sweep designs and manufactures industrial wireless meters and Big data workflow software for real-time equipment monitoring. They recently launched SweepAPI, a cloud-based data gathering platform that allows storing and analyzing time-series data. The project goal was to provide warehouse workers with an easy tool to track products, retrieve product data and adjust the parameters. Our team has developed two applications that work jointly to solve that problem: a web application that provides a user-friendly interface and a WebSocket API that processes and stores data. The back-end API manages client connections and handles inventory data. The app can send and receive stream data from Sweep API and convert it into a more intuitive format of products that various front-end applications can utilize. The API allows clients to authorize, request, or update organizations, directories, and streams. The user-friendly and responsive front-end application transmits data to and from the back-end API via WebSockets. Users of the application can authorize, select organizations, and request product data by barcode id, while managers can also add, edit, and delete products.
studentNames: Isaac Perez, Michael Pisman, Salvador Ruiz-Ramirez

abstract: Sweep helps organizations gain a deeper understanding of their data, allowing for more efficient decision making. They automate data workflows to help you uncover insights at a real-time pace. For large manufacturing businesses, managing an inventory can be difficult and a lack of insight often leads to downtime which decreases overall productivity. Team 306, House Cleaning, created a web application that acts as an inventory management engine, which is a user friendly interface that visualizes a company's inventory allowing users to track important metrics on inventory items like quantity and type. The items within that inventory are then sent as data to the SweepAPI  which takes care of storage and management within their own database.
studentNames: Charison Gill-Branion, Angel Jaimez, Jose Ochoa-Miranda, Omar Ramos-Correa, Samish Kavoori

projectTitle: Vision System to Identify Planting Quality
abstract: Morning Star is a vertically integrated company with business units supplying and servicing all aspects of the tomato industry. Morning Star faces the problem of not producing a 100% germination rate when transplanting tomato saplings due to human and machine error. In order to reach 100% germination rate the team has implemented a simple vision system. This vision system is located behind the planter detecting the saplings as they are being planted, producing confidence score where staff can later review results and make adjustments in future planting days. The model ultimately produces confidence scores over a video of the accuracy that a plant has been detected in a given region.
studentNames: Moises Limon, Alberto Melchor-Maldonado, Christine Pham, Darren Ng, Ivan Palacios

projectTitle: Vision System to Identify Planting Quality
abstract: Morning Star Company is responsible for about 30% of California processing tomato production. The problem is that the tomato transplants do not have a 100% germination rate when growing. Therefore the team project is to improve their production more by designing a vision system to identify whether the tomato plants have been successfully transplanted. Our team designed an identification algorithm to allow the farmers in the field to scan and check the transplants' status. The team also designed a web application that can show and download data of scanned plants.
studentNames: Luis Andrade Gonzalez, Andrew Lara-Kane, Jude Marvin Orsua, Michael Cheung, Zhuo Liu

projectTitle: Web-based, Multi-Platform, Open-Source Speech-Recording App for Brain Health
abstract: Through speech and language assessments, the Memory and Aging Center at UCSF is able to research the effect of neurological and neurodegenerative diseases on higher cognitive functions such as speech, language, and memory. Currently, the problem they face is that these assessments are subjective, specialist-dependent, and exhaustive and are also limited in sensibility and scalability. Most of the data they have currently gathered comes from English speakers, limiting the generalizability of findings across the globe and across different languages. To solve this problem, the 404 Not Found team created an open source speech recording web application that can test patients’ speech and language ability helping to gather data across different languages throughout the globe.
studentNames: Christopher De La Cruz, Akheel Shaik, Derik Gandhi, Isaac Gutierrez, Ysidra Van Kempen

projectTitle: Web-based, Multi-Platform, Open-Source Speech-Recording App for Brain Health
abstract: Neurodegenerative diseases affect about 50 million people worldwide (NIH). Doctors use speech and language assessments to help predict and diagnose these diseases in patients. However, these assessments tend to only come from English speakers and are very specialist-dependent. In collaboration with the UCSF Memory and Aging Center, (insert team name) developed a web based, multi-language, speech-recording application solution that displays text and picture prompts and records patients' responses. With this web application, UCSF and the UdeSA Cognitive Neuroscience Center (Argentina) will analyze the data as a way to find early signs of these neurodegenerative diseases.
studentNames: Jose Arias Zuniga, Betsy Avila Aguilar, Benita Onyenacho, Diego Ponce, Jocelyn Chan

projectTitle: To Pull or Not to Pull
abstract: The United States Cold Storage is a company that provides third-party logistics for perishable foods by arranging the transportation of a customer’s freight with import/export services. The United States Cold Storage has requested the team to create an ETA service that can provide a real-time distance of a trucker’s location with respect to their various facilities in order to reduce dock congestion. The team has developed a web application in which the truckers can send their ETA and any important information to an administrator application for the supervisors to view.
studentNames: Mauricio Alvarez Leon, Arshdeep Chauhan, Ivan Marquez, Zhenyu Peng

projectTitle: To Pull or Not to Pull
abstract: The United States Cold Storage is a company that provides third-party logistics for perishable foods by arranging the transportation of a customer’s freight with import/export services. The United States Cold Storage has requested the team to create an ETA service that can provide a real-time distance of a trucker’s location with respect to their various facilities in order to reduce dock congestion. The team has developed a web application in which the truckers can send their ETA and any important information to an administrator application for the supervisors to view.
studentNames: Kirankumar Ashokkumar, Hannah Joyce Villasin, Michael Lara, Mahodarini Nyalakonda, Sean Li

projectTitle: Constructing Composite Time Ranges for a Search
abstract: Veracruz Ventures is a Smart Farm startup based in Portugal, interested in applying and developing cutting-edge technology. The team was tasked with creating a solution that would ease the process of constructing a database search involving multiple contiguous time ranges. With the use of modern technologies such as NodeJS, React, and PostgreSQL, the team was able to create a web application that facilitated this process through a clear and intuitive user interface. The application allows for manual entry of time ranges as well as time adjustments through a range slider.
studentNames: Yuxi Chen, Amy Lau, Anthony Ricardo Wright, Jason Crawford, Jophiel Nino Novoa

projectTitle: Constructing Composite Time Ranges for a Search
abstract: Did you ever try to search through multiple ranges of time in your mailing list, or in a database? You may have noticed the waste of time that comes from redoing the search for each of those ranges! Our sponsors for Innovate to Grow, Veracruz.Ventures, a Portugal based smart farm startup, certainly have, so our team created an app to construct, save, and edit composite time ranges in a convenient way. The app is designed to help users by searching in multiple non-contiguous time ranges, including the option of saving, copying and pasting a pre-formatted string with the Boolean search operations.
studentNames: Prateek Datla, Anooj Vadodkar, Brian Madrigal, Matthew Felix, Ta Jour Harris

projectTitle: Constructing Composite Geographical Areas for a Search
abstract: Veracruz Ventures is a Smart Farm startup based in Portugal, interested in applying and developing cutting-edge technology.  Veracruz supports the Innovate to Grow program to develop data management and search modules applicable to Ag and beyond.  Our team goal was to allow users to search for points of interest within predefined areas on a map, rather than the whole visible map.  The team created a web application using Python Flask with a Google Maps Javascript API to create an interactive map that allows a user to create multiple rectangles as defined areas to search, then query a database for points of interest within those areas, and return in a list format only such results.  The application also enables saving user-defined areas of interest for future searches.
studentNames: Jowi Aizpuru, Andre Martin, Alan Triano, Benjamin Hoang, Jonathan Huynh

abstract: Western Digital is a data infrastructure company working in the field of digital storage: storage hardware, cloud storage, and other related products. They use Jira, a project management tool, to track team progress. However, the data Jira provides is not conducive to easily illustrating an operation's efficiency. The Key-(In)Sight team created  Keysight, an application that extracts this data from Jira to provide as Key Performance Indicators (KPI’s) in a clean, accessible web application for users. Keysight also keeps managers well informed through custom, automatic email notifications for issues that need attention.
studentNames: Ryan Chang, Adrian Mcbroom, Charles Parkinson, Joshua Stickles, Peter Sou

abstract: Western Digital is a data infrastructure company that manufactures, develops, and markets data storage devices and solutions. They currently use the Jira platform to track issues and manage projects. With many projects each containing thousands of different issues, Western Digital needed a better way to analyze data from the Jira platform in order to illustrate an operation’s efficiency. The team developed an easy to access web application that automatically pulls data from the Jira platform and displays it so any Western Digital employee could view key performance metrics on a project while also receiving email notifications on issues that need immediate attention.
studentNames: Alexander Holt, Alexander Hom, Alexander Roman Montiel, Jim Nguyen, Juan Rosales

projectTitle: Solar Industrial Process Heating - Cloud Access and Remote Control
abstract: Winston Cone Optics (WCO) is a company that focuses on the development of non-imaging optics. They are creating solar powered evaporators and solar coolers for a variety of industrial uses. They currently log solar thermal data with a commercial tool, to ensure their equipment is safely operating. They wish to transition towards a more cost-effective remote-data logger. The current logging technology is cost prohibitive for large scale deployment. Our team built a remote data-logger using the inexpensive Arduino, to reduce unit costs and increase potential adoption of their product with local businesses.
studentNames: Ghi-Hoang Barretto, Fizan Khan, Ismael Figueroa-Ramirez, Maximiliano Coraza

projectTitle: Solar Industrial Process Heating - Cloud Access and Remote Control
abstract: Winston Cone Optics (WCO) is a startup company spinning out solar thermal technologies developed at UC Merced. Their business is the generation of renewable solar process heat, the likes of which can replace fossil fuel based heating systems. WCO currently logs data and operates these systems in the lab,  and wants to move to remote logging and operation with a phone or web app. The team built a data logging software using Arduino technology that reads, formats, and displays data onto a web app.
studentNames: Harjot Grewal, Andy Hernandez, Austin Myhre, Eric Solorio, Navroop Khangura

projectTitle: Estimating Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Designing Mitigation Strategies in Merced, CA
abstract: In alignment with global efforts to limit global warming to 1.5 ? and meet requirements set by Senate Bill (SB) 32, the City of Merced must reduce greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions by 28% by the year 2030. Our project aims to evaluate different GHG mitigation strategies and determine which are well-suited to Merced’s context and goals. The proposed solutions are: 1) solar thermal heating for industrial food processing heat demand 2) anaerobic digestion for agricultural energy and waste recycling needs 3) energy efficient home upgrades for local residences. Our combination of mitigation strategies mitigates ~142, 000 MT of CO2 which meets the requirement set in SB32. We have also developed a decision-support tool and mapping model to help local decision makers and community members prioritize mitigation strategies based on various criteria such as cost, land use, ease of implementation, and proximity to sensitive communities.
studentNames: Vishaal Kumar, Jose Lopez Vela, Sonia Perez, Alexis Smith

abstract: Grayson Riverbend Restoration Project goal is to develop 285 acres riparian and high-quality floodplain habitat along the San Joaquin River for the benefit of multiple threatened and endangered species, including spring-run Chinook salmon, as well as the adjacent economically disadvantaged community of Grayson. The design objectives for our team were to provide an optimized habitat for the rearing of the endangered chinook salmon, by designing a friendly fish passage for the newly restored Grayson Riverbend Preserve. The chosen design solution consists of a box culvert design that will create low flow velocities and simulate natural river channel hydraulics that will support the salmonid life cycle. To promote a heathy environment a box culvert was designed and modeled using HEC-RAS which simulates a functional habitat to support the salmonid life cycle.
studentNames: David Baez, Christy Duong, Marianna Estrada, Joy Sajimon

abstract: The University of California, Merced is one the most sustainable university in the nation. With UC Merced’s triple net-zero goals, the campus is considering installing a rainwater harvesting system. Currently, the campus’s only source of water comes from the City of Merced well and does not utilize non-potable water. To address the needs of the UC Merced our team has been tasked with the design of a rainwater system that will capture and use non-potable water to irrigate green areas throughout campus. The proposed design location for the system is the Academic Office Annex building with an annual rainwater collection of 54,000 gallons. To demonstrate proof of concept a prototype was designed and installed at the UC Merced Community Garden that will collect about 400 gallons of rainwater annually that demonstrates UC Merced commitment to sustainability.
studentNames: Kainoa Ferguson, Steven Nguyen, Rosa Ruiz, Robylene Seapno

projectTitle: Eco-Friendly Resolution for Stations and System Facilities: Pigeon Mitigation
abstract: Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART), like many public areas, faces the problem of having a considerable amount of pigeons. Due to their nature, pigeons leave feces behind, nest, roost, and die at these BART stations; because of this, the stations require extra maintenance from the workers to conserve a pleasant and safe environment. Furthermore, to keep the stations clean, there is a need to allocate extra funds for these workers and the tools needed. Keeping the effective areas clean will result in financial earnings for the BART stations while creating a delightful eco friendly environment. As a team, we were tasked with reducing the amount of pigeon presence in the stations. The solution(s) must be humane and environmentally friendly for the daily commuters and wildlife. After visiting the location, our team has developed two passive solutions to help the birds avoid areas of interest. The first solution is targeted to cover nesting areas with Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene (ABS) 3D-printed covers. The second solution is targeted for signages; we assembled and designed a rotating bearing that will provide ledge or wire mitigation to keep the birds from landing.
studentNames: Alexander Lau, David Mendoza Bautista, Karla Ayala Avalos, Misaki Cramer, Nelson Cha

abstract: California Transplants is a large scale vegetable seedling operation based in Newman, CA. California Transplants is aimed at growing high-quality vegetable seedlings with a limited turnaround time. Time and labor are crucial components to the company and industry during the peak seasons of spring and summer. The empty trays that return after delivery to farmers must be cleaned and prepped for growing future seedlings to continue the cycle again. Team 117, the Robotic Plants, aided California Transplants with a semi-automatic empty tray palletizing system in organizing and preparing the returned trays for the upcoming cycles of use.  This will enable California Transplants to reallocate their workforce on more crucial tasks at the facility.
studentNames: Amiel Carl Sabile, Eduardo Cuevas, Philana Ng, Romeo Marroquin Ajcac, Saigeetha Bhaskar

abstract: Corigin Solutions, Inc. is a company based in Merced, CA, where they manufacture organic farming products to increase crop yield and margins. The problem they are facing is that one of their products, Corichar, a Biochar, is prone to smoldering caused by exothermic surface oxidation. The Capstone team created an apparatus to stabilize the biochar by quenching it with mist. The apparatus consists of a steel duct with spraying nozzles attached around the circumference. The product has been installed in the company's existing system.
studentNames: Anthony Tsang, Bryan Powell, Brandon Rodriguez, Justin Zepeda, Kevin Cambero
