abstract: Dave Wilson Nursery uses mixed material potting benches to grow their crop. Over time the decommissioned benches have been stacked in an unused field. The nursery has decided to expand and now the benches need to be removed. Prior to removal the benches need to be dismantled into their base components so that the material may be properly recycled and disposed of. Specifically, the pressure treated wooden legs of each bench need to be removed wholesale before the remaining wood and steel wire mix can be turned into scrap and recycled. Our final design is the Power Saw Press or P.S.P. It is a series of eight circular saws mounted to a scissor lift and housed in a protective shell. When a bench is inserted into the P.S.P. and the door is closed, thereby activating the pressure and magnetic safety sensors, the scissor lift and saws will activate, cutting the pressure treated legs away from the frame safely and efficiently. This will reduce the labor required to remove the surplus of benches by more than 50%, while maintaining the high rate of deconstruction set by the client’s staff. Additionally, we coordinated the disposal of the hazardous material.
studentNames: Chase Bowman, Jose Colon, Rodolfo Lopez Moreno, William Solis Lopez, Zeus Marquez, Erik Trinkle

abstract: Turbidity is the number of total suspended solid (TSS) samples in water droplets. In a greenhouse, turbidity becomes a problem when it rises too high because the condensed solids can harm growing plants. Our client, Duarte Nursery, is a family-owned and operated nursery located in Hughson, CA, and they are the largest permanent crops nursery in the United States. Duarte Nursery utilizes high-pressure nozzles to produce fog in their greenhouses and controlling the amount of fog is especially important in their operations because there may exist high levels of turbidity. We designed the ‘turbidity measurement tool’, which is a device that measures the number of TSS samples in the fog across a greenhouse using infrared light beams. By implementing the principle of light backscattering, the tool was used to provide fast and accurate turbidity measurements in a local area of a greenhouse using a calibrated turbidity sensor. Our tool shall be used by staff working at Duarte Nursery to improve the efficiency of their agricultural operations.
studentNames: Andrew Lai, Alejandro Sanchez, Isha Saini, Jason Ngo, Simranpreet Kaur

projectTitle: Irrigation by Plant Weight for Containerized Nursery
abstract: Duarte Nursery is a farming company with a long history of innovative growing methods. Duarte tasked our team with finding a manual, user-friendly method of tracking the water depletion in containerized plants and then facilitate the transmission of this data to the nursery’s database to optimize water use. Our team’s designed a system based on strategically placed load cells which will allow the client to examine the water depletion trends over time as water is processed by the plants. The farmers will then be notified of precise areas of the nursery that require irrigation.
studentNames: Bobak Peyman, Joseph Steffan, Lance Nua, Michael Portillo, Yekaterina Zhezherya

abstract: E & J Gallo Winery is a family-owned winemaker and major wine distributor, headquartered in Modesto CA, producing millions of cases of wine each year. These cases of wine are moved on tall, bulk glass pallets in the warehouse and wrapped with a thick plastic that needs to be manually removed by the operator. Depending on the height of the pallet, the operator must reach up and bend down to cut the plastic multiple times a day, and this can result in injuries due to cumulative stresses and overstretching. Our team designed a mobile cutter that would remove the need for an operator to cut the plastic by automating the plastic cutting process leaving the operator to simply peel the remaining plastic off. This cutter will be used by the operators to reduce cumulative stress and injury, and over time, save the company money by avoiding and eliminating current injury costs.
studentNames: Chris Buregeya, Emmanuel Nunez, Jose Bravo, Joshua Butler, Veronica Acevedo

abstract: Opening 50mL bottles by hand is ergonomically undesirable since the small bottle and cap diameters cause hand strain. The E. & J. Gallo Winery spirits plant may reject a significant number of these bottles per hour due to manufacturing defects. As a result, Gallo team members are continuously risking hand strain injuries from opening bottles. Not only is it a health concern, but it is also a slow and tedious process that can harm productivity. In our project, we came up with an ergonomic design that removes the need to manually open and drain the bottles. Our solution will substantially improve the process, with improved ergonomics and faster reclaim times. This solution can be used by Gallo team members in their daily tasks of opening and draining the bottles.
studentNames: Alex Hellewell, Abner Rosas Hurtado, Elisa Gonzales, Fernando Aguilar, Gerardo Garcia

abstract: E. & J. Gallo Winery is one of the largest family-owned wineries in the world. The winery, which is located in Livingston, engages in a variety of wine processing activities, including pumping wine through long, heavy industrial hoses that are constantly transported between different areas. The goal was to cut hose transportation labor hours while not increasing safety risks. Currently, the hoses are transported by having two employees carry each end of the hose on their shoulder. Our team created a two-cart model in which the carts are attached to the hose at two points: one at the hose's end and another near the middle. To use this tool, the worker simply secures the two carts to the hose with the attached safety straps before carrying the other end of the hose on their shoulder as usual. The number of employees required to transport a hose is reduced from two to one using this tool, without increasing the current safety risks.
studentNames: Amy He, Eduardo Bautista, Erick Mercado, Gladys Santana, Misael Miranda

abstract: Frito Lay Modesto is a snack food manufacturing facility, full-service warehouse, and distribution center. Snack foods are made, moved, and stored here before being distributed all over the western states. They recently had a new portion of factory constructed at the end of their conveyor line to handle outgoing shipments and now need the old conveyors that feed into the new section to run at the same speed of 200 feet per minute to allow for picking and replenishment optimization. After looking at a variety of potential solutions our team decided the most effective solution would be to modify existing gear ratios to increase belt speeds.
studentNames: Brandon Lopez, Jacob Glasser, Jose Manuel Rivera Soto, Lorenzo Sebastian, Lukas Terry

abstract: Gemperle Orchards is a family-owned orchard that principally produces almonds in the central valley. They have requested assistance with what would otherwise be the tedious process of placing an entire irrigation line underground. Environmental factors pose a threat to the irrigation line in the form of extreme heat and damage from critters. Our team's project was to create a tractor attachment that can dig the ditch and move the irrigation line into it in one single pass (a second pass using a blade to compact the dirt is also needed). After designing and proposing our solution to the client, we began to source materials to manufacture a prototype. Currently, this tool is used for this client, however, it is extendable to many farms and can be adapted to the client’s needs depending on their soil, equipment, and wants.
studentNames: David Soto, Eric Angel, Juan Martinez, Joyce Saltzherr, Leonardo Vargas Vazquez

projectTitle: Design and Development of an End-Effector for Collecting Leaf Spectral Data
abstract: IoT4Ag is an NSF Engineering Research Center that strives to ensure food, energy, and water security through advancements in agricultural technology. Early detection of plant stresses can help growers maximize their yield by preventing crop loss. Team Innovate to Growbot has designed an end-effector capable of taking close proximity spectral data, for use on a robotic platform. This autonomously gathered data will provide early detection of plant stresses without the cost associated with manual data collection.
studentNames: Aidan Ross, Colin Cox, Christopher Jimenez Ortega, Elgin Datcher, Kevin Johnson, Houston Dowen

abstract: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy chambers are used in many veterinary practices. When an animal is placed in the oxygen chamber, the concentration of oxygen can be doubled in order to help animals in need. Some health benefits can be seen from reducing swelling, controlling infection, and improving healing processes. Jon Klingborg, a veterinarian at Valley Animal Hospital in Merced, has implemented this hyperbaric oxygen technology into his practice and is working with college students from the University of California, Merced to make a more affordable chamber so more veterinarians can use it in their practice. The one in development contains a hydrophobic mesh cover to enclose the cage and a multisensor device to measure the environment. In this project, we worked on the major components that make up the multi sensor display.
studentNames: Alondra Zamora, Emily Tran, Kamen Grozev, Nathan Da, Timothy Su

projectTitle: Improvements of Welding Efficiency and Safety
abstract: Laird Manufacturing is a large manufacturing company that specializes in cattle feeding equipment. The problem Laird is facing is not having a sufficient number of workers to manufacture products. Our project was to find a way to improve productivity. We, the Welding Defenders, have designed a semi-autonomous cart to weld a 2 hour seam onto an auger flight. The cart we built will require only to be set up and removed, so that the parts are welded autonomously, and the welder can also manage manufacturing of other products, thus increasing productivity.
studentNames: Adan Guevara, Dylan Chiriff, Melissa Aviles-Aleman, Marlo Gamez, Victor Ashaolu

projectTitle: Waste Water Treatment Equalization Tanks Optimization
abstract: Land O Lakes is a Fortune 500 company with deep roots in the dairy industry, producing a variety of dairy products for the entire country, with the Tulare plant focusing on milk powders and butter products. Currently, the LOL Tulare plant sends their wastewater  to an external treatment plant that charges them for the amount of flow into the plant, the Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD),  and Total Suspended Solids (TSS). They are also charged fines if the pH of the waste water is below 5 or above 11. Our team designed a system to reduce the biological oxygen demand and total suspended solids present in the wastewater through the implementation of an aeration pump mechanism. This system will be installed on the outside of the two equalization tanks and will pump atmospheric air into the tanks to allow all of the solids to rise to the top and aerate. Implementation of this pump mechanism is estimated to reduce BOD and TSS costs by roughly 1-5%.
studentNames: Boston Williams, Dewayne Broome, Gerardo Fuentes, Ismael Gonzalez Almazan, Muhammad Ahmed

abstract: Land o' Lakes is a Fortune 500 company with roots in the dairy industry, producing a variety of dairy products for the entire country. The Tulare plant, the location of team 114's focus, is centered on the production of milk powders and butter products. Team 114 was tasked with reducing butter loss and in light of this request, a pump and a stopper were designed to help minimize yield loss as a response to the issue. Team 114 has approached resolving the problem by testing its concepts through simulated methods to improve production efficiency and maximize the reduction of yield loss.
studentNames: Eduardo Mauricio Gonzalez, Isaiah Ramos, Jason Brosman, Kimberly Parra, Luisito Cruz

abstract: Neil Jones Foods operates out of Hollister, canning tomato products for use in restaurants, schools, and more throughout the US. The machine that seals the lids on the final product occasionally malfunctions, resulting in cans with improperly sealed lids. These bad cans result in downtime in the system, reduced production, and ultimately lost revenue. Our team has designed a detection system to identify the problematic cans and signal a rejection system to remove them from the processing line. The detection system consists of a camera and low power computer to capture an image of the can. The image is classified through a machine learning model trained by the team. From prototype testing, the cans were consistently and successfully identified.
studentNames: Daniel Miliate, Junyi Xu, Manuel Arellano, Paul Andrae Ysmael Conanan, Ruben Ambriz

abstract: Neil Jones Food’s TomaTek facility in Firebaugh, California dices, arranges and packages hundreds of tons of fresh tomatoes per day, every day for the duration of the season. During this twenty-four hour cycle, tomatoes are running down conveyor belts at high speeds and often need to be diverted manually into various directions to allow for cleaning of dicers and prevent pile ups. This process results in loss of tomatoes and requires an employee to run down to the conveyors to fix it, which is inefficient. In order to improve TomaTek’s current means of tomato diversion, our team has developed a system of modules which attach to the intersections of the conveyor lines. This system utilizes pneumatic cylinders to drop gates in different orientations to divert tomatoes into desired directions.
studentNames: Adrian Alvarez-Gil, Andrea Meza, Alexis Ortiz-Vasquez, Hector Flores, Nicholas Serrano

projectTitle: E-sort Line Ladder in Hand Sort Room
abstract: Hughson Nut Incorporated is an almond producer and distributor under Olam International that generates $17 million in revenue and produces almonds in the millions of pounds per year. During the shipping process, almonds must be hand sorted from conveyor belts that are fed by raised distributors. Workers must currently use a ladder to climb to the elevation needed to adjust the flow levels in the distributors. This presents a risk of injury due to the height, ladder stability, and slick surfaces caused by oils from almond processing.  To avoid and mitigate this as much as possible, we have proposed the construction of a stable platform with railings and ample treaded surface area to move around on. The platform was designed using OSHA guidelines for elevated platforms and stairs. Testing of the design via SOLIDWORKS Simulation was conducted to ensure the design meets our basic safety criteria. This involved conducting a static loading test, a vibration frequency test, and a component fatigue test. Upon future implementation of our solution, we expect an increase in worker safety, and a subsequent decrease in worker injuries.
studentNames: Andrea Duque, Gregory Elcombe, Lidia Haile, Paxton Kounlavouth, Sebastian Vazquez

abstract: Hughson Nut Inc, a subsidiary of Olam Food Ingredients, is one of the largest almond and macadamia nut packers which produces a full line of almond products. Our client is the Leedom location and is facing high product loss which results in $250,000 a year in profit loss. Our client suffers from nuts falling between conveyor belt transfer, nuts being stuck between belt and side railings, oils from the macadamia accumulate that captures nuts and drags it underneath the conveyor belt which is non-consumable. Lastly, high impact velocity from the  belt transfer is too fast causing the nuts to bounce off of the belt and onto the floor.  Our team designed and implemented new attachments, a roller support and  a ramp extension with a squeegee attachment, onto the existing conveyor belt to prevent product loss. The roller support aided in the conveyor belt slack to prevent nuts from falling in between the belt and the side railings. The ramp extension allowed nuts to fall onto the conveyor belt much softer. Lastly, the squeegee eliminated all oil accumulation to prevent product loss. Our projected goal is to save 80% of all products that equates to $200,000 a year in profit gain.
studentNames: Donald Grondin, Jon Sasaki, Robert Benson, Raymon Cachola, Robert Cespedes

projectTitle: Dice Line Ladder Use at the Metal Detector
abstract: The Hughson Nut, Inc. Olam Group company has three facilities in the Central Valley. Olam company processes Almonds and Macadamias for the purpose of manufacturing and consumer use. We worked with the Leedom Road facility which focuses on Consumer-Packaged Goods, where they process over 80 million pounds of product per year. In this facility, employees are required to test the metal detectors in the dice line in order to ensure that the system is functioning. This is done by an employee using an A-frame ladder to reach the 8ft overhead conveyor belt. However, using the ladder is a safety hazard as the worker could fall from it, injuring themselves. Considering the fact that these tests occur every 2 hours, with three samples introduced for 2 system, this is a very real possibility . To avoid the workers using the A-frame ladder we have designed an inclined conveyor belt system that will carry the metal samples up and onto the metal detector line, while allowing the worker to remain on floor level.  Our conveyor system will be mounted onto the frame of their system to minimize external space usage.
studentNames: Andy Valle, Justin Reyes, Leslye Gonzalez, Michael Difrieri, Paul Arana

abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies is a leader at the forefront of industrial, mobile, and collaborative robot development. Currently, our client’s process for diagnosing and troubleshooting brake failure is not as efficient or cost-effective as they would like for it to be, thus there are areas of opportunity for improvement. Our team identified an opportunity for improvements and designed a Failure Analysis Toolkit composed of a sensor system targeted to detect common mechanical, electrical, and thermal failures assisting engineers and technicians in the investigation and troubleshooting of failed brakes in Omron Facilities and client locations. The Failure Analysis Toolkit accomplishes this feat by using onboard sensors to measure discrepancies and provides critical sensor read information to the user.
studentNames: Cifriana Mina Dela Cruz, Demitrius Zulevic, Emanuel Rosales Villalobos, Javier Gomez, Jose Serrato, Abrian Villalobos

abstract: Industrial robots require regular calibration in order to fit the industry standard. The SCARA Calibration Team developed a new calibration method for OMRON’s robots. This calibration system was required to calibrate the system within 15 minutes or less to improve accuracy and precision. The team was able to develop a calibration system that included an algorithm and hardware fixture solution that would include Renishaw Ballbar. The ballbar would only detect the data from circular testing if they are circular like geometric errors, positioning instability, and inconsistent feed rates with the use of Autonomous Control Environment interface.
studentNames: Christopher Edmondson, Giovanni Garcia, Gamaliel Ng, Hailey Harkness, Jolene Hoang

abstract: Omron Adept Robotics and Scientific Technologies is an automation technology development company located in Pleasanton, CA. Omron specializes in developing new mechanics and software for a broad utility of robots, including the main focus for our project: autonomous mobile robots (AMR) with load detection for warehouse maneuverings. The goal of the project is to have Omron’s AMR model LD-250 detect load and notify clients when the loaded weight is outside of its safe operating range, therefore, creating a new safety parameter and reducing the need for maintenance as a result of overloading. Using detectors and runtime data extracted from the AMR we developed a function for the load tolerance of the robot. We then constructed a Matlab code, filtering values of fluctuating current and acceleration when being overloaded and adjusting our calculations to increase percent of precision. We calculated the accuracy of the code by comparing it to the physical load on the robot. This code will provide a new safety measure in the AMR as well as help Omron’s clients learn how to utilize the AMR to optimize and automate their warehouse maneuvering needs.
studentNames: Andres Soto-Barajas, Dat Nguyen, Josue Mendoza, Kelly Thai, Sterling Ayers

abstract: The client, Sensient Natural Ingredients (SNI), is a food processing company located in Livingston, California. SNI is a producer of onion powder and other dehydrated vegetables, herbs, and spices. After the product is cleaned and sliced, it is dehydrated in-house and then milled. SNI’s main issue is downtime because the slicers are not standardized. Thus, blade maintenance ranges from 6 to 9 hours. This increases their budgetary issues since they slice over 2.0 million raw pounds on a daily basis. Our objective was to design either a new slicer that can be standardized across the factory or design a system that supports the workers on-site to reduce the machine's downtime while maintaining the volume intake of onions. Our team designed a new slicer, Fan Blades, with double-edged blades and a pin within the shaft to make the slicer bi-directional. Therefore, the slicer can continue running for twice the time before it goes under maintenance.
studentNames: Brisa Botello Sanchez, Kevin Moreno-Ruiz, Luz Mascorro Ramirez, Nathan Ibarra, William Preciado

abstract: Sensient Natural Ingredients is one of the largest producers of dehydrated onions and garlic in the world. This project is designed to increase the flexibility and efficiency of the repackaging lines. In an effort to do this, an improved sealing system was designed that allows the repackaging line to accept both the fiber drums they are currently using and the cardboard totes that are aimed to be incorporated into the reckpackaging line. Thus, the team created an attachment to modify the tipping and dumping machine to accept either type of container. This included a set of attachments which were linked to a common base on the tipping and dumping machine. This solution allows SNI to transition from dumping drums to totes easily and with minimal downtime.
studentNames: Christopher Vasquez, Esteban Cruz, Jordyn Barat, Kenny Hong, Matthew Vezien

projectTitle: Improved Moisture Measurement and Control in Pomace Drying Operations
abstract: The MorningStar Company is the global market leader in tomato processing, where tomato skins and seeds are dried and made into pomace, which is then used as an ingredient in dog food. Our project addresses the issue of efficiency in the drying operations. Current moisture measurements before and after drying are taken manually and the dryer temperature is adjusted per batch, leading to inconsistencies both in undersampling and in taking periodic measurements. We decided to automate the moisture measurement process via an in line setup, where the pomace could be measured in real time before and after reaching the dryer. We chose a set of moisture sensors and identified areas appropriate for their installation. To begin testing, we trained a machine learning model that simulated the performance of the sensors. After basing it off data from the previous season, this model can be used by our client to predict the sensors’ performance by referencing our written guide on the program. Upon implementation of our system, our client will save money by avoiding shipment of overdried pomace, and automate their current methods of moisture measurement to produce accurate and timely values.
studentNames: Ahmaree Sanders, Michele Campbell, Noah De Jesus, Oluwatosin Ajetunmobi, Sorina Munteanu

abstract: The Morning Star Company is a California Central Valley business focused on processing tomatoes.  In addition to making bulk tomato paste, bulk diced tomatoes and canned tomato products, Morning Star also owns Valley Sun Products, the world's largest producer of sun-dried tomato products.  Morning Star is exploring alternative methods of drying tomatoes with the ultimate goal of reducing the cost of manufacturing.  One potential solution is to use a kiln dehydrator that dries the tomatoes to half of their original moisture content, followed by finish-drying in the sun.  Our project was to use a simulation software to model the air flow and humidity within the dehydrator chamber, and to find the optimal conditions of the dehydrator. Our team designed a 2D version of the dehydrator and simulated the air flow as well as heat transfer within the dehydrator under optimal conditions that do not affect the qualities of the final product, dehydrated tomatoes.
studentNames: Alexi Chavez, Aaron Marenco, Jesus Carreno Nunez, Mason Moraga, Nicole Jae Olandria

projectTitle: Alternative to Steam Ejector to Create Vacuum in Evaporation Process
abstract: The Morning Star Company provides 40% of domestic tomato products and accounts for over 30% of California’s processing tomato production. Tomato paste is created in a multiple effect evaporation process that includes heat exchangers to indirectly heat the tomato juice, and steam ejectors to create a vacuum, which then creates the flow of steam. In order for operations to run smoothly throughout the season, steam ejectors are used to create and maintain a vacuum during the tomato paste evaporation process. During this process, a large amount of steam is currently exhausted into the atmosphere. This steam loss translates to both efficiency and monetary losses. After an in depth analysis of the problem and possible solutions, our team’s final design consists of pipelines that will direct the vented steam into an accumulation tank and into existing pipelines so that the steam can instead be repurposed back into the system for other heating uses.
studentNames: Alyssa Arntzen, Daniel Alvizures, Francisco Alvarez Rodriguez, Marvin Prieto, Michael Rodriguez

abstract: This project was conceived for a new startup venture, in partnership with UC Merced Innovate to Grow and the product incubator Pi Shop in Fresno.  Bikers might encounter an obstacle that would require them to position their center of mass behind and lower than the bicycle seat, or else they could flip over the handlebars.  This risk is higher on steeper terrain. We designed an attachable bike seat that pads and supports bikers when they are in this lower position and trying to regain their seating position. This design also allows for adjustment of the seat angle during a ride. We produced a prototype that will be tested for consumer acceptance, to see if the product is viable for market production.
studentNames: Angel Andrade Rodriguez, Ambrose Liu, Claudette Marikta, Eli Roos, Umar Shaheed

abstract: Agrecom is a company with pest control, sanitation and water treatment services. Neptune worked under Agrecom’s mentorship to create a system to work alongside their existing monitoring setup. The team developed a program to visualize data received from a variety of water sensors and output them into graphs such as pH, oxidation-reduction potential, water level, and flow rate.
studentNames: Alexander Woersching, Chelsea Zhao, Jacqueline Nguyen, Tommy Nguyen,  Vianca Reynoso

abstract: Agrecom provides agricultural services based in Merced specializing in pest control and water treatment. Two teams from the University of California, Merced are working alongside Agrecom to create a low cost way to read data from sensors, and display this data on the outside of the monitoring enclosure Agrecom has created for clients. The project team needed to develop the friendly UI interface to be displayed on a seven-inch Raspberry Pi display; the data being displayed originates from a predefined API. Team 14 developed a solution using grafana that allows for data visualization and configuration of the monitoring enclosure in a low cost manner. To conclude, Team 14 delivered an effective solution that met the requirements of the client.
studentNames: Ivan Padilla, Misael Bolainez, Nathan Huynh, Timothy Ha

abstract: AgTech Insight seeks to help farmers and agricultural companies understand how different technologies could connect to one another based on their needs and interests. The team developed an info-graph web using Agtech Insight’s startup database that visualizes different companies and how they connect. Anyone viewing the graph can easily see how specific technology companies in AgTech can interact and connect.
studentNames: Alejandro Sanchez, Aramy Truong, Emelio Barba, Vincent Nguyen

abstract: Agtech Insight is a consulting firm that wants to connect the different agricultural companies in their database. Our team has developed a web-app which has the ability to create custom info-graphs. These custom info-graphs visualize the matching categories based on a minimum number of keywords matched. The goal is for anyone to get information on specific companies and different fields of agricultural companies.
studentNames: Brayam Corral Flores, Erick Barajas, Manuel Toro Garcia, Sreeda Vemuri

projectTitle: Operations Control Center Shift Log Workbench
abstract: The Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) provides public transport in the San Francisco Bay Area. BART is starting to outgrow their current event management system which needs to be updated to support California Public Utilities Commission (CPUC) codes and have input latency reduced. Our team’s solution is a web-based application that targets BART’s current pain points in their log entry process while keeping the core features of their current system. Some key features of the product include flexible entry searching, low input latency, and assisted autocomplete for CPUC codes in a more user-friendly design.
studentNames: Aaron Leung, Kelvin Nguyen, Mritthika Harish, Shivali Aggarwal

projectTitle: Operations Control Center Shift Log Workbench
abstract: Management and event reporting are very crucial when it comes to the operation of a big transportation system. Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) attempts to improve its management efficiency through a web-driven application. This application allows Operation Control Center (OCC) Managers to report any day-to-day incidents like maintenance work, police activity, delays, equipment abnormalities, and other events impacting the regular operation of the system. The team designed and optimized BART’s web app to increase efficiency and workflow within the management of the OCC. The web app creates improvement in the process of inputting, categorizing, condensing, extracting, and reporting data.
studentNames: Francisco Lira, Gregorio Contreras Cervantes, Julio Lopez, Mercedes Jimenez Garcia

abstract: Diamond J Dairy is a first generation farm who’s Core belief is that there is always something to learn. The farm has a strong focus on sustainability, efficiency, and modernization, and continues to keep up with the latest and most efficient farming technologies. To keep up with the ongoing vision, this team has worked to build a cost-effective, flexible & robust system able to optimize the use of water by cow sprinklers, using industry leading computer vision and image processing techniques, with the help of AWS web services to maintain security.
studentNames: Bharadwaj Satyanarayana, Kevin Hernandez, Rohit Vemuri, Tanner Nodolf

abstract: Diamond J. Dairy is a modern dairy farm that utilizes an automated, state-of-the-art soaker system to keep their cows cool and maximize dairy production. Currently, there is significant water waste because the soaker system turns on when cows are not present in the soaking area. For environmental and economic reasons, water conservation is a high priority for Diamond J. Dairy. The Cool Cow Coders have developed a vision-based program that recognizes and detects cows within the soaking area. When deployed throughout the farm, the team’s software will aid water conservation efforts by ensuring soaker valves are only activated when a cow is within its soaking vicinity.
studentNames: Aditi Kisara, Crystal Reynoso Figueroa, Shivanshu Gupta, Zachary Harry

abstract: Chronic pain affects more than 20% of adults in the United States (CDC). However, finding the root cause of this pain can be a difficult and expensive endeavor. Pain can be intermittent and often does not present itself in the physician's office. In collaboration with the Fresno Institute of Neuroscience, the Project ANJIL team introduces an integrated pain tracking solution that measures both qualitative and quantitative data to help physicians determine how different life factors and activities influence patients' pain levels over time. A mobile base station device is combined with multiple self-contained sensor packages. Sensor data is communicated to the base station via Bluetooth Low-Energy allowing each sensor package to remain operational without recharging for multiple days. Patients are able to enter pain and ancillary information through a user-friendly touch interface. Together, the sensor data and patient data allow the physician to better understand each patient's chronic pain.
studentNames: Adam Weingram, Isaac Shin, Junia Jacinto, Lafrance Daniels, Nu-Chong Lee

projectTitle: Delivering Early Math Education Materials to Families
abstract: Early Family Math is a non-profit organization that focuses on providing educational materials to those with limited resources. Currently, there is a website built that allows families to access information regarding the organization. However, there is no efficient or scalable method that can deliver and allow families to access materials according to their needs. The team developed a web app that allows families to register and receive emails which consist of materials in accordance to the child’s educational level.
studentNames: Connor Bourke, Elaine Huang, Matthew Chang, Ying Zhang

projectTitle: Delivering Early Math Education Materials to Families
abstract: Early Family Math is a non-profit organization seeking to help children between the ages of 0 and 8 develop basic math skills. Currently, they have a website where parents can go on and download whole chapters of activities which can be intimidating. They are creating a new form of content delivery for their products in order to increase user acquisition and retention. Our team developed an application that allows families to sign up and receive Early Family Math’s activities and storybooks using traditional email services.
studentNames: Andy Bui, Benjamin Crawford, Brandon Duong, Brodey Nance

projectTitle: Battery Simulation Application for Autonomous Mobile Robots
abstract: Omron produces autonomous mobile robots (AMRs) to solve a variety of material transport issues. Battery life is a crucial factor for their customers. Therefore, there is a need for a user-friendly GUI that can predict expected run times and charge times, given the expected environment. The team developed a battery simulation that shows the run time, current consumption over time, and capacity (mAh) for the current run. Additionally, automated scripts to summarize data from AMR logs and generate new optimal models to predict current usage.
studentNames: Austin Near, Jason Chan, Nicholas Haines, Yoan Palacios Guzman

projectTitle: Battery Simulation Application for Autonomous Mobile Robots
abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies, Inc. (ORT) manufactures autonomous mobile robots. Omron had the need for a software tool that customers could interact with which displays predictions of run times and charge times for batteries of autonomous mobile robots. Our team’s approach was to develop prediction models that find the best fit of the data in order to present battery and run-time predictions within a display.
studentNames: Giovanni Perez, Jesse Luna, Nathan Edwards, Prabhnoor Brar

abstract: Sensient Natural Ingredients is a vertically integrated producer of dehydrated vegetables. During the harvesting process excess waste material is picked up and included in the sorting of the produce. The Team developed a real time object detection software, capable of detecting dirt among onions and garlic. It identifies contours and color gradients and produces a suggestion to the machine operator of when to slow down or speed up. This will help remove excess dirt more efficiently during the sorting process thus decreasing loss in produce and unwanted material being shipped to the factory.
studentNames: Mark Ogbunamiri, James Short, Kyle Leung, Kyle Tucker, Samuel Kashiwase

abstract: Sweep designs and manufactures industrial wireless meters and big data workflow software for real-time equipment monitoring. A collection of Docker containers and server-side software deployed across multiple servers causes issues when accessing their log data. Our team built a Docker container that can be deployed globally across all servers to collect log data and send it to SweepAPI for monitoring. This container auto configures for common log locations and allow admins to define extra log files to monitor. Unifying log management allows admins to monitor dynamic logs while also limiting server login authorizations.
studentNames: Apsara Fite, David Chu, Louis Perez, Solomon Denning

abstract: Sweep provides real-time data analytics services to companies to reduce the likely hood of downtime in different micro-apps. Currently, Sweep has the SweepAPI which is a simple data gathering platform that stores and analyzes time series data. There is no effective way to figure out if there is an outage in specific micro apps, therefore Team HM is helping to reduce the amount of time a certain service might go down for. Our team is tasked to automatically gather the metric data from a server such as the CPU usage, hard drive space, memory being used, bandwidth, and index nodes (Inodes) without knowing where the server might be installed. After the metrics are gathered they are sent to SweepAPI for monitoring for any down micro-apps.
studentNames: Cesar Garcia, Graciela Moreno, Gordon Tong, Higinio Ramirez, Miguel Paolo Al Tercero

projectTitle: Merging Selected Results from Multiple Searches
abstract: Have you ever performed multiple searches on a dataset, and wished they were all in one place? With the aid of Veracruz Ventures, an AgTech company, the team created an app where a user can perform multiple searches and sort through these searches all without having to leave the application or end up with multiple tabs.
studentNames: Aaron Kaushal, Alex Taylor, Loreta Camacho, Ranvir Singh

projectTitle: Merging Selected Results from Multiple Searches
abstract: Veracruz Ventures is an agricultural company investing in sustainable innovation including data management. As such, there is a need for the development and deployment of a user-friendly management system interface that leverages multiple search results from a database to maximize the user's retention and acquisition of data. As such, our team  built a multi-purpose user interface that allow any user to spawn and search through multiple data tables in a single page from a database, which can not only freely manipulate the selected search results but also compound multiple search results into a single data table by using the Open source Jquery Library, Cloud Tables and Python Flask. This is further compounded by the application having the foundation of retaining and sharing the finalized results to any tab or person, respectively.
studentNames: David Salse, Fabiola Pineda, Sidharth Babu, Sy Loc

abstract: Western Digital is a computer hard disk drive manufacturer and data storage company that designs, manufactures, and sells data technology products. Western Digital’s device laboratory contains a large amount of computers. Due to its grand scale, it is difficult to keep track of and oversee the usage of the many desktops that are utilized by their employees to monitor processes. Our team has developed software for a Web Application specializing in monitoring the computers in the device laboratory into an organized user interface that displays key aspects of the lab computers to be easily tracked by the lab manager.
studentNames: Matthew Nor, Partap Singh, Subhalakshmi Balaji, Sofia Figueroa

abstract: Western Digital is a computer storage manufacturer that creates, designs, and sells data technology products such as Hard Disk Drive (HDD) and Solid State Drive (SSD). They have a central lab in Milpitas, California where they do most of their silicone testing. A lab manager is responsible for managing lab computers to device engineers who do silicon testing. Currently, the lab manager has no way to efficiently check which computers are still in use. The team used an open-source program to gather information from each lab computer into a central computer. The team then organized the necessary information into a database and created a web app allowing the lab manager to view the information.
studentNames: Colin Schmierer, Eric Li, Nicholas Ly, Samuel Carlos

abstract: Our project is to design a wetland to treat agricultural runoff for the City of Newman, CA. The City of Newman is seeking a creative engineering solution to lower the environmental impact of polluted agricultural and urban runoff while diversifying water resources. The design constraints and requirements are to remove 50% total phosphorus and 75% of nitrogen (specifically nitrates) from irrigation water. The treated water would be utilized to dilute municipal wastewater effluent prior to land application and groundwater recharge on city-owned agricultural crops. To address the needs of the City of Newman, we designed a free water surface treatment wetland, and evaluated the design on technical, environmental, economic, and sustainable factors.
studentNames: Leonel Arvizu, Charlotte Calvario, Ismael Ontiveros, Stephanie Segura

projectTitle: Proposed expansion of the San Joaquin Valley Air Pollution Monitoring Network
organization: San Joaquin Air Pollution Control District
abstract: The San Joaquin Valley exceeds air pollution standards for two key pollutants, Ozone and Particulate Matter 2.5. The current air monitoring network is outdated and fails to reach increased pollution emissions and equity needs, leading to detrimental health effects on vulnerable communities. To address this problem we designed an expansion of the current air monitoring network administered by the San Joaquin Valley Air Pollution Control District (SJVAPCD) by assessing the current network deficiencies and shortcomings. The constraints and requirements of the design are Regulatory Compliance, Equity Impact Factor, Land Cover, and Site Distribution. Target communities and final site locations were spatially determined and located to expand air pollution monitoring, ultimately anticipating that the SJVAPCD will evaluate and implement the design proposals to mitigate hazardous air risks to vulnerable communities in the San Joaquin Valley.
studentNames: Elaina Cavada, Nefertari Marin, Jesus Salas Hernandez, Tasos Tentoglou

projectTitle: Experimental Smart Farm: Water Storage and Groundwater Recharge
abstract: Climate change and the growing demand of water have strained irrigation resources in the Central Valley. The UC Merced Experimental Smart Farm (ESF) located on the campus of UC Merced is required to enhance the groundwater (GW) storage capacity to ensure sufficient irrigation during below average water years. The pre-existing water supply for the ESF are nearby groundwater wells that pump the water to flood-irrigation risers. Given the constraints of California’s Sustainable Groundwater Management Act (SGMA) of 2014, ESF is required to operate under conjunctive and sustainable water use. To enhance groundwater storage and operate under SGMA we designed a sustainable onsite water storage system that utilizes a micro- reservoir, above ground storage tank, and a recharge infiltration basin under the farm’s cultivation area. The proposed design will recharge 240 acre-ft of groundwater and provide drought tolerant irrigation during low water years.
studentNames: Brooklyn James, Stephanie Orozco, Megan Pinkus

abstract: The San Francisco BART system runs trains that are propelled by 1000V DC energized contact rail (the "third rail") parallel to the track. Currently, the maintenance team physically probes this rail to confirm its de-energization state before working, and are seeking a redundant method to determine the energization state of the third rail, in order to increase the safety of the maintenance crew.  Our team has designed a supplemental visual system indicator, using a buck converter circuit to step down the voltage extracted from the third rail, and displaying the energization state of the third rail through a LED system.
studentNames: Jacob Hardy, Kovin Aghassi Lelham, Nhan Cao, Victor Nguyen

abstract: Blue Diamond Growers, who primarily process almonds, use Cablevey systems to transfer almonds within their facility. During this process, friction and excess almond particles create a black residue that is difficult to remove from the inner walls and pucks of this conveyor. The objective was to create an in-line mechanical system that did not introduce water to their transfer process. Our team created a two-step cleaning process that will use a circular air ring to remove residue from the pucks and a Pipe Pig attachment to scrape the remaining residue from the inner walls of the Cablevey system.
studentNames: Cindy Cervantes, Juan Garcia, Mary Baltazar, Quinncie Reider

abstract: California Agricultural Solutions (CAS) has 100 fertilizer tanks of various shapes and capacities, making it difficult to know how much fertilizer solution is remaining within. While some tanks are left out in the field at various locations, the client would waste petrol checking in on their tanks. The potential of parts and tanks being stolen is also a concern, as they are necessary for the company to continue operating. Team 10 designed a device that could help with not only locating their trailers if stolen but also by utilizing a submersible pressure sensor to measure the capacity within the trailers.
studentNames: Anthony Morales, Eddie Santiago, Kelvin Flor, Tiara Armanino Dayrit

abstract: Our client Corigin solutions has an excess amount of bio-oil which is a byproduct when producing bio-char. Our team has conceived a solution to make use of this oil by converting it into a tar-like substance that will then be primarily used for pot-holes, and then eventually for mainline road infrastructures. The bio-oil is pretreated with crumb rubber from recycled tires and then heated for an extended period to reduce the innate moisture content of the bio-oil. Finally, the crumb rubber modified bio-oil is then mixed with common road aggregate to provide the necessary structural integrity that is needed to be as strong and durable as the roads that are driven and walked on everyday.
studentNames: Abraham Avendano, Jonathan Aguilera, Justin Pham, Linda Castellanos-Torres

abstract: Diamond J Dairy farms operate approximately 2,000 cows. In order for these cows to deliver healthy and rich nutritious milk, the water they drink must be clean. Part of the dairy farm's challenge is effectively delivering that high quality water to the concrete troughs that the cows drink from. The problem that the farm faces is that the concrete’s porous nature results in hidden hotspots for algae growth. Additionally, the feed that the cows bring with them encourages algae growth reducing the clean water quality they need. We developed a low cost chemical approach that quickly gets rid of the algae concentration in the water and provides an effective solution to the porosity of the troughs. By applying a concrete resurfacer to the bottom surface of the troughs, the presence of algae stuck to the bottom surface is eliminated. Additionally, a dilute bleach solution is poured into the water to reduce the population of aglae that grow in the drinking water for the cows. Moreover, a deep skimmer net is implemented to remove food debris and reduce the amount of nutrients which helps prevent algae growth.
studentNames: Jonathan Mendoza, Vienna Banez, Wendy Ninomiya, Yi Hao Xie

abstract: E & J Gallo has soaked bags in their wine tanks that need to be removed through a small tank door. The main objectives of an effective design are to make a system that spends less time in the oak removal process, reduces workers' time in the tank, and improves the ergonomics.  There were many safety and design constraints that influenced our ideas, but the team decided to use a winch and tarp system to drag bags across the tank floor. A winch, connected to a gondola, goes out, up to 40 feet, to a loaded tarp inside the tank. The team developed this idea into a working protype that has been tested on site with fruitful resuls.  The team hopes to push for this protoype to be product-tested, potentially improved and implemented.
studentNames: Jose Gomez, Josef Hand, Kevin Aguirre, Ricardo Garcia Limon

abstract: Flory Industries is an agricultural company that manufactures and sells nut harvesting equipment worldwide. Flory's shuttle truck has intermittent auger controllability issues at low rotational speeds. The objective of this project was to analyze the auger system, simulate the hydraulic circuit on Automation Studio, test different theories to eliminate or fix the augers' performance and provide Flory Industries with possible economically feasible solutions. Over the semester, Team Augers tackled these objectives and identified the fundamental problem in the hydraulic circuit, where at low speeds, the pressure and flow rates were fluctuating. The team recreated the oscillatory behavior in the system using Automation Studio and ran physical tests by varying the relationship between the compensator and proportional valve. As a result, the team suggests implementing a higher rating compensator within the current hydraulic system to allow a higher pressure drop to stabilize the lower speeds of the auger. This is coupled with tuning the proportional valve threshold to remove the remaining low oscillating speeds.
studentNames: Curtis Kremerskotter, Daniel Navarrete, Shruti Chakraborty, Sakawat Thanvir

abstract: The Fresno Institute of Neuroscience provides clinical care, patient diagnosis and evaluations, research, educational and administrative consulting services for patients with neurological illnesses in the California Central Valley. Many of these patients frequently deal with chronic pain and other neurological conditions and note that their symptoms vary. Therefore, it is necessary to create a device that logs patients pain levels along with collecting external factors, in order for physicians to understand external factors influence on their pain. To solve this problem, our team created a small, inexpensive, and user-friendly encapsulation that contains the arduino sensor that will track patient pain, posture, and daily activity. The encapsulation was 3D printed in Stereolithography (SLA) resin material. Working alongside the computer science engineering team, the data from the arduino sensors planted on the patient will be transmitted via Bluetooth System. Patients will be able to log their patient pain levels on the Android Pad which will then filter movements that caused pain. Obtaining the data that marks the movements and external factors that cause patients pain, will allow the physicians to develop a treatment plan that alleviates these external factors.
studentNames: Aurora Rodriguez Teodoro, Diana Cruz Garcia, Ean Camacho-Escobedo, Jennifer Mendez

projectTitle: Precision Granular Fertilizer Application on Young Almond Trees
abstract: Gemperle Orchards is an almond farming operation. Currently they apply granular fertilizer by hand. Granular fertilizer allows for a more direct fertilization to the young almond tree and avoids feeding the surrounding weed flora. Our client is in the process of replanting some of their acreage and has tasked our team to design an agricultural implement that can accurately dispense granular fertilizer to the base of young almond trees. The agricultural implement that our team has designed is to be hitched onto our client’s ATV. Through research we found an existing blueberry fertilizing implement and based some subsystems of our design on it. Our biggest contribution to this agricultural implement is our barrel subsystem. The barrel subsystem holds and dispenses fertilizer only when a young almond tree is detected. Our agricultural implement ensures that the client continues to precisely feed their young almond trees while avoiding inefficient use of fertilizer.
studentNames: Daniela Garcia, Ivan Hernandez, Joshua Young, Victoria Mendoza

abstract: Henry Miller Reclamation District operates approximately 2,000 gate turnouts over 47,285 acres between Los Banos and Dos Palos in Merced County. These gate turnouts channel water for San Luis Canal Company (SLCC) customers. Currently, water flow measurements are manually logged once or twice per day by canal operators. This method assumes a constant flow rate over the course of 24 hours, which is not true since water level fluctuations and gate height adjustments can occur throughout the day. To address this issue, UC Merced’s Capstone Team 12 designed a system to track water flow measurements more accurately and more consistently. Specifically, this design would log data in intervals of 15 minutes in order to capture changes in gate height and water level fluctuations. This system consists of an upstream pressure transducer, downstream pressure sensor, gate position sensor, and data logger.
studentNames: Brian Ceja, Joseph Webb, Tanner Ragan, Wilson Chuong

projectTitle: Low-Cost High-Resolution Digital In-Line Holographic Microscope (DIHM)
abstract: The team worked with IBM Research Center in San Jose which is part of the Center of Cellular Construction. The problem is to take a digital in-line holographic microscope and create a waterproof shell to enclose it so that it can be submerged underwater to detect, identify, and capture images and videos of free-floating plankton. Plankton are being used because they are great indicators of ecological change. The original design of the microscope was altered to reduce the size of the water channel between the camera and the laser diode, to taper the bottom of the shell cap so the PVC pipe would fit better, and to make the design of the shell thicker so it would be more resilient to pressure and water. The material used to print the shell encapsulating the microscope components is a 3D printer filament called ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene). The main microscope components include a Raspberry pi zero, Raspberry pi zero camera, a laser diode, and a USB cable. One of the constraints was to keep the system cost-effective as it is a goal to mass produce the microscopes to be used for research.
studentNames: Alexander Nguyen, Emmanuel Egun, Jacob Espinosa, Mariella Quevada

abstract: Like humans, many animals develop respiratory issues throughout their life. For animals, it may be more difficult to find solutions to increase their oxygen intake. Since creating a respiratory mask that will be universal for all animals is close to impossible, Dr. Klingborg’s idea is to focus on the idea of an oxygen environment. The need for an oxygen solution is essential for all veterinary clinics, but the current solution is subpar or extremely expensive. This project is a modular door that will attach to various existing cages and will supply oxygen from an oxygen concentrator on the side cart. The design is not only able to supply oxygen but will also filter out carbon dioxide while controlling temperature and humidity as needed. This project will improve any veterinarian's experience as it simplifies all of the complexities such as replacing the existing door, oxygen tank refills and even mobility of the system.
studentNames: Anthony Alfaro, Austin Dahlin, Eberardo Camorlinga-Ruiz, Son Tran

abstract: Merced Community College has a 100-acre farm that uses furrow irrigation to water its pumpkins and tomatoes. Furrow irrigation is a gravity-based method which requires “blocks/checks” to slow down the flow rate of water. The “blocks/checks” function similarly to a dam, as it pools the water in the furrow until it reaches two holes and flows off into the next sectioned area. The objective of this project was to address the extensive labor required to monitor the “blocks/checks,” during irrigation. Manually monitoring the furrows is an inefficient and costly task. The team designed a sensor network, extending across 1-acre of land, using microcontrollers and radio modules. The sensor network assists irrigators by reporting the water height along the “blocks/checks” in 10 min intervals.
studentNames: Andy Lin, Alexis Prieto, Jose Landeros, Ravinderjit Sidhu

abstract: Merced Community College District has a 100-acre farm that grows a pumpkin patch annually. The farm uses a greenhouse to propagate the seeds and then transplants them using a mechanical transplanter. The farm requires a simpler way to seed trays with less time and labor. The Seeders have developed a vacuum seeder that matches the dimensions of the trays. The seeder will use suction to store the seeds in an organized position where an employee is able to place it above a tray, release the suction, and have the seeds fall into the cells of the tray all at once.
studentNames: Jesus Beatriz, Jose Galvan, Marquise Jefferson, Shushen Chen

projectTitle: Design Gripper for 3 Axis Robot in Agriculture
abstract: Currently, there is a significant labor shortage that prevents the quick packaging needed to ensure retailers receive fresh and quality fruit. Milano Technical Group seeks to resolve this issue by automating the process of placing packages of stoned fruit inside boxes. They have developed a tri-axial robotic arm to address the labor shortage issue and prepare the shipments with the speed required. However, they lacked the necessary attachment to properly grasp the bags of stoned fruit. The mission of this project is to design and build an attachment for the robotic arm that is capable of grasping the bags of fruit. Therefore, we have developed a robotic clamping device that will tackle the problem. The device enables the tri-axial system to package the bags of stoned fruit inside boxes for shipment.
studentNames: Alfonso Villafuerte, Renato Mendoza Chavez, Ranulfo Tinta-Hernandez Jr, Shangkun Yang

abstract: In order to package Neil Jones tomato cans, a certain offset pattern must be achieved. Currently, there is a worker who manually adjusts unorganized cans, but this is a safety hazard and inefficient. Cangineers approached this problem by figuring how to even out the distribution of the cans better. This solution is to have two doors that will be moved by an air cylinder from the left and right of the conveyor belt allowing the cans to flow without the need of human adjustment. The team’s solution will adjust cans into an offset pattern automatically to make the palletizer more self-sufficient, allowing the packaging of cans to be safer and more efficient.
studentNames: Andres Torres, Kevin Kuang, Marcos Morales, William Le

abstract: Neil Jones Food Company consists of three processing facilities, including TomaTek. Located in Firebaugh CA, TomaTek processes California tomatoes into various tomato products. For our team's problem, hot tomato paste pouches go through a cascading cooler. This cascading cooler mixes the paste within the pouches and cools these pouches with water. Upon exiting the cooler, the pouches fall onto a conveyor below in a random orientation sometimes stacking on top of one another. Currently, two employees on each manufacturing line monitors these pouches separating, flattening, and aligning as neccessary. Our team was tasked with automating this process and did so by creating a continous flow tapering wall that will only allow for properly aligned and non stacked pouches to continue towards packaging while circulating rejected pouches back into the system.
studentNames: Gui Rong Chang, Jose Morales, Mark Dutka, Marvin Parungao

abstract: Robots need to have regular calibration in order to fit the industry standard. Working with the company OMRON, the SCARA Calibration Team was assigned to create a new calibration method for OMRON’s SCARA robot line. This calibration system must be able to calibrate the system in 15 minutes or less, produce the exact same results every time, and be able to be used as many times as needed. Through a semester of designing, testing, and organizing, the team was able to develop a calibration system that builds upon its predecessors to become more efficient than other systems by implementing new code to the SCARA robot as well as designing a new calibration rod. The rod calculates the maximum work area of the robot, and verifies based not only on eyesight but also with a precision test where the robot places a rod through a precise hole.
studentNames: Gualberto Hernandez Echeverria, Jahlani Mccrumby, Lizbeth Sanchez, Nicholas Clifford

abstract: Sensient Natural Ingredients provides farm-to-table ingredients and solutions that cultivate natural ingredients to bring flavor, texture, color and nutrition to food products while keeping labels clean and simple. Currently, they are experiencing several problems with their chili reaming process. The drum would spin, the blade would have trouble reaching the last two inches of product, and approximately half a pound of product would be lost during this process. CAP 07 team designed a new blade that will help reach the product and changed the material used for the friction padding to prevent the drum from spinning. To tackle the product loss, the team designed a cone that would be attached to the system that will prevent the product from spilling over the drum.
studentNames: Arturo Juarez, Diego Banuelos, Juan Amezcua Ramirez, Juan Guzman

abstract: The Morning Star Company is a tomato processing and harvesting firm. Their harvesting season begins in July and ends around the middle to the end of October. The harvesters are being run 22-23 hours a day to maximize the amount of tomatoes harvested during this time period. The only pauses are during shift changes in which harvesters are cleaned and quickly serviced. This continuous workload causes the hydraulic system to generate excess heat throughout the day and eventually causes systematic failure. Once this happens the harvester must be shut down for several hours until the hydraulic system returns to an acceptable operating temperature. This causes our client to lose harvesting time and in turn, revenue that would have been generated from the harvested tomatoes. To solve this, we have performed an analysis of several heat exchangers to decide what would act as a more efficient replacement for the one currently in use. In addition, our team has selected a reversible fan to clear debris from the heat exchanger that may be causing inefficient heat dissipation.
studentNames: Garrett Johnson, Hla Lwin, Ibrahim Sabih, Michael Ponce

abstract: The Morning Star Company harvests and processes tomatoes, including the creation of tomato paste. In the condensation process, large amounts of water known as condensate is lost due to the particulates it contains. This is a huge opportunity to save the plant thousands of gallons of water a day if it can be cleansed and reused for other processes. Our solution to this issue was to create a system of filters and heat exchangers that can cool the hot condensate and allow it to pass through a reverse osmosis system, creating nearly pure H2O for the plant to use.
studentNames: Connor Burns, Jared Diliberto, Matthew Perez, Rodrigo Elizarraraz

abstract: During air rescue missions, operators use a litter basket attached to a cable hoist to airlift medical patients into a helicopter for transportation to a hospital. As the basket is lifted up, it can get caught in the turbulent air created by the helicopter’s propellers, otherwise known as rotor wash. Because this action can cause trauma and fatal injuries to the patient, it was brought to the attention of our client, The Pi Shop. The Pi Shop is a product incubator located in Fresno that partners with Blue Dolphin Design and Engineering to help young entrepreneurs build businesses around their ideas that solve solutions to problems such as the one presented by the air rescue operations. Our client tasked us with finding a solution that either prevents or minimizes the spin of the basket. The resulting design from our research and development was the Air Basket Stabilizing Sail prototype, otherwise known as A.B.S.S. It is a controlled sail that attaches onto the helicopter’s rescue basket hoist cable above the basket and can be controlled by an operator. To reduce the spin, the position of the sail is modified to utilize the rotor wash and create a rotating force counter to that created by the rotor wash.
studentNames: Daniel Cooney, Daniel Thomas, Jasmine Heer, Luz Mascorro Ramirez, Thomas Anderson

abstract: Bike seats remained the same for over a century, while bikes evolved to support different types of cycling.  When riding a mountain bike downhill or with obstacles, the rider may shift the body behind and lower than the seat, and may hit or catch the rear edge of the seat trying to regain seating position.  The Iguana's designed seat extension concepts to pad and guide back on to the seat.  Finally, the team built a mechanical adjustable attachment that clamps a seat extension to the main seat.  This idea was developed for a possible new startup venture in collaboration with The Pi Shop, a product incubator.
studentNames: Centurion Barron, Emmanuel Abok, Nodebechukwu Paully-Umeh, Zen Unity

abstract: Agrecom is dedicated to resolving the pest control dilemma at residential and commercial levels with innovative solutions that successfully establish long-term control. When servicing a client, the deployed technician optimally receives a map with the respective bait stations. Continuously, the technician visits every station to typify individual pest-related activities. Subsequently, every station’s data is stored in USB-drives, organized in spreadsheets, and ultimately summarized as a readable report sent to the client days later. To address the report-generation’s redundancy, we developed an application that automizes report-generation via a technician-oriented interface, a centralized database, and QR codes to track individual station’s activity.
studentNames: Luis Garcia-Velasquez, Christian Baronia, Hubert Le, Bykema  Mobley, Jesus Fragoso

abstract: Agrecom provides pest control services to clients in agricultural, residential and commercial sectors, and uses a variety of documentation methods to keep track of bait usage and pest activity. Currently, this involves the usage of pen-and-paper reporting methods that are transferred between computer and paper several times before storage. Our team has developed an app and supporting database that provides a way to fill out and store these reports digitally, and prints this information to be filed physically or given to clients.
studentNames: Eduardo Raygoza, Christopher Achiles, John Villalta, Mrudhun Arivalagan, Chris Ahrens

abstract: Agrecom is a water treatment facility that is in need of an interface for its clients and administrator. Our completed task is to develop a client and admin portal that allows for convenient access to data derived from sensors through a third-party API called HOBO link. Admin portal has the ability to add clients and sensors. While on the client portal, clients are able to view their data. Our application is completed and deployed.
studentNames: Eric Morris, Eshant Prakash, Manvir Dhillon, Angel  Snavely, Wesley Chan

abstract: Agrecom is a water treatment facility who currently uses a third party site to receive and transmit their clients’ water sensor data. The current process is all done manually and involves recording the data by hand and then relaying that information to their clients, as only the Agrecom team has access to this site. The project team has created an online web portal that connects to the already existing API to show clients their sensor data in an efficient and accurate manner in real time without having to go through Agrecom to access this data.
studentNames: Ian Lopez Chavez, Nampal Singh, Harmanjeet Singh, Huy Vu, Tarandeep Singh

abstract: Training is a crucial aspect for maintaining a secure and productive work environment but comes at a monetary cost. Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) attempts to solve this issue by petitioning a web-driven application to give constant access to their substation technicians for more training time at a reduced cost. BART values the safety of their technicians and will like to give constant access for training to their technicians. We designed a simulation of the Alternating Currents portion of the CO2 Panel with a modular approach that serves as a platform for future efforts. The CO2 Panel allows the manipulation of current flow through the primary and redundancy modules which permits the substation the handling of issues and keeping the substation operational.
studentNames: Fernando Diego Chino, Irma Gonzalez Padilla, Austin Chan, Gerardo Rodriguez, Adrian Sanchez Magana

abstract: BART trains its new engineers and maintenance staff on proprietary control panel configurations. However, to offer on-site staff training on the equipment it is often necessary to either impact revenue operations or do the training late at night. The main objective was to create an application that can simulate selected parts of the control panel functionality so that a new engineer can get familiarized with the panel before in-person on-site training. This project implements a browser-based application that simulates the main panel, supports alarm simulations, and local/remote commands. Additional enhancements include the implementation of an event notification panel, where a user can acknowledge and dismiss alarms and events.
studentNames: Jordan Benoit, Baudelio Perez, Tianyu Gao, Ricardo Sanchez-Macias, Michael Ngo

projectTitle: Automated Battery Monitoring for Life Safety Assets
abstract: Once upon a time, Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) engineers were tasked with visiting stations and manually monitoring battery cells’ health. Our objective was to simplify this task that took a few hours to complete and make it doable in a few minutes from a computer screen. Our project involved pulling and processing data from the BART intranet and making it remotely available in an intuitive and graphical manner.
studentNames: Brendan Blasius, Uche Ezeokoli, Juan Torres Esquivel, Brandon Salazar

projectTitle: Automated Battery Monitoring for Life Safety Assets
abstract: Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) is a public transportation agency located in the San Francisco Bay Area in California and relies on electricity for daily operations. In order to ensure redundancy in the event of power loss, BART has battery backups for life safety assets. Importantly, maintenance of these batteries is crucial to maintain power during an outage. We developed an application that will allow BART to remotely monitor the batteries at all stations. Important battery parameters for maintenance such as temperature and voltage will be collected, then displayed onto an interactive dashboard for BART's employees.
studentNames: Howard Yuan, Diana Palafox, Nicholas Chin, Martin Vazquez Orozco, Ma. Franchesca Lopez

projectTitle: Automated Battery Monitoring for Life Safety Assets
abstract: Bay Area Rapid Transit (BART) is a public agency in charge of controlling, maintaining, and operating the public train system located in San Francisco Bay Area. A problem of BART is that their engineers need to manually check the batteries that they deem problematic, meaning loss of man time and manpower. Our application automates this process which allows BART engineers to use an interface that provides the battery’s statistics from one centralized software component, saving them dozens of hours, and allowing their engineers to dedicate more time to other tasks.
studentNames: Jose Zarate, John Muro, Salvador Rodriguez, Ean Camacho-Escobedo

abstract: Cisco designs and sells networking technology. Cisco’s clients expect purchased hardware to function right out of the cardboard box. The server setup process can be difficult and messy; without guidance, a server can be inoperable for weeks at worst. Our web app guides technicians through the installation process step by step by providing wiring diagrams, diagnosing connections, and root-causing server communication errors.
studentNames: Nathan Ross, Andrew Wong, Daniel Maciel Manzo, Jacob Gilmartin, Jamal  Bills

abstract: Cisco sells network hardware and solutions, but to use the hardware Cisco's clients need to convert the hardware into a topology designed by an engineer and this step is very error prone due to human error occurring from the technician. Errors from the technician can cause delays and are hard to solve. Our solution helps alleviate this problem by converting the topology into step by step instructions and periodic testing to ensure that the technician's chance of error is significantly reduced.
studentNames: Carl Kenneth Martin, Busher Bridi, Christian Quinonez, Kevin Matei, Kumaran Akilan

abstract: Cisco Systems develops, manufactures, and sells networking hardware. Since Cisco sells the hardware they manufacture, they are also the ones that set it up for clients. Setting up server racks is difficult and messy - often prone to errors. Our application, designed in the Ionic framework, is a progressive web app, available both as a web app and mobile app. The application provides the onsite technician step-by-step instructions to reduce errors before they occur. It also tests port connectivity to stop them from leaving with an incorrect configuration.
studentNames: James Nguyen, Andy Zhang, Jason Zhu, Brandon Escobar, Armaan Kapoor

abstract: Patients with chronic pain or other neurologic conditions frequently note that their symptoms vary with environmental conditions. This includes heat and light but also body position and level of activity. It would be useful for the patient and their physician to know how these factors influence their main symptoms such as pain, fatigue, or weakness. This might allow the patient and their physician to build a treatment plan that mitigates these environmental factors. We were able to log the patients' information and view their data with graphs and have the medical professionals able to interact with the app as well as the patients with a different version of the app.
studentNames: David Dominguez, Jonathan Contreras, Karthik Gnanakumar, Kennedy Nguyen, Michael Issa

abstract: Elders with a serious problem of chronic pain and neurological conditions note that their symptoms vary due to environmental conditions. Heat, light, and body positions are one of the many symptoms that cause patients with chronic pain to feel weak, fatigue, or pain. Our project strives to connect patients and physicians from the comfort of their home, staying safe during this pandemic, and bettering the patient’s life by building a treatment plan that will mitigate these environmental factors. We are able to do so by allowing the patient to record their symptoms and pain level, get notified of when to take their medicine, and graph the data received from the sensors for the clinic to record.
studentNames: Zong Lee, Saishrithik Sareddy, Tristen Trias, Emmanuel Durago, Emanuel Estrada

abstract: Italian Scientists & Scholars in North America Foundation is a non profit organization whose mission is to connect, empower and celebrate the Italian Intellectual Diaspora in North America. Our team was committed to helping the organization manage its data and making searching for information convenient and accessible. Our project delivered a user-friendly frontend web application that empowers admins and viewers to manage a database of members’ profiles and the organizations they’re affiliated with. Our fluent user interface allows for easy access to all the information to help the Italian Scientists and Scholars in North America Foundation continue the mission they represent.
studentNames: Cristine Penero, Alvin Collier, Uriel Montes, Jaime Rivera Vazquez, Katharina Ng

abstract: Italian Scientists & Scholars in North America Foundation is a non-profit organization that Connects, Empowering, Celebrating the Italian Intellectual Diaspora in North America.  Currently they are storing information about members in a MySql database, making it extremely inconvenient and time consuming to access and manage data. We developed a solution that displays the information through a React application that will make accessing and reading data easier for both users and admins. Our solution will not only create a better experience searching the database, but also become an effective tool for admins to manage the data as well.
studentNames: Sydney Cripe, Jairo Carreon, Rogelio Aguilar, Jesus Martinez-De Santos, Ryan Valadez

abstract: ISSNAF, the Italian Scholars and Scientists in North America Foundation, is a not-for-profit organization whose mission is to connect, empower and celebrate the Italian Intellectual Diaspora in North America, including more than 3000 scholars, researchers, and technologists in North America. Currently their database is a MYSQL database and only a few members can find information and manage the data. We developed a solution that displays the information in a PHP website accessible for both admin and user. Developing in PHP, allows the website to have simple interface, while being able to process large amounts of data for all ISSNAF members to access and allow admin to maintain the database.
studentNames: Alfonso Nevarez, Yingshi Liu, Pedro Torres, Ankit Sompura, Benito Hernandez

projectTitle: Service Reminder Application for Industrial and Mobile Robots
abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies is an industry leader in manufacturing control equipment, advanced robotics, automation technologies, and electrical components.  They hope to leave a lasting effect in bettering society by improving the lives of all individuals through the benefits of robotics.  As with all physical devices with automation, maintenance is required.  To ensure that Omron can service the robots, the RobotWatch team delivered an Android application that notifies the user of the severity of the maintenance needed for robot upkeep.  Additional improvements also include the ability of the user to input robot data and get a corresponding notification.
studentNames: Clifford Lee, Bruce Duong, Gilberto Aramburo, Isaiah Acosta, Jordan Montano

projectTitle: Service Reminder Application for Industrial and Mobile Robots
abstract: Omron Robotics and Safety Technologies, Inc. (ORT) is a technology company developing cutting edge robotic and safety technology and products. In addition to technology development, ORT also manufactures robots for Industrial Automation applications. Our service reminder application will provide ORT a platform where their technicians can review the maintenance status of these robots and be reminded when robots require upcoming maintenance via notification alerts.
